title: nlir power-move: move-aur2-nudge.sh (:&[reminder,why,ask] = warm nudge) + POWERMOVES.md curation (folds aur-1 #6/#7)
description:
nlir power-moves tick (aur-2, @&[...] composer lane). MOVE #13 — "the nudge": :&[THE_GENTLE_REMINDER, WHY_IT_MATTERS, THE_LOW_PRESSURE_ASK]. Chase something without being pushy — a soft reminder + a quiet reason + a low-pressure ask; the : leading op keeps it warm (swap @ for a stiff formal chase).

Two people can't start their work until you check it, but that's okay for today — it would be great if you could take a look by tomorrow!"

ALSO curated POWERMOVES.md (I own it): added the nudge + meeting-recap (my moves) + folded in the pending aur-1 counter-offer #6 [@(!^-1 & grounds), @'alt'] + weighed-decision #7 [~>@^-1, ~>!^-1, @(^-1 & 'decision:...')] into the quick-ref + lane lists, and added the slot-rule note to the COMPOSE section. title: Debug LLM realisation failure after configuration refresh
description: After entering valid LLM configuration (URL and key) and refreshing the WASM app, the system reports "llm realisation failed: realisation via operator command: no llm realiser set". title: [parser] opaque errors for incomplete/malformed ^-index sigils (unexpected end of input / coerce empty string)
description:
Found by a dogfooding QA pass of the lexer/parser (msm-0, parser/lexer/CLI lane). The core is solid — escapes (`\ ` `\t` `\n` `\\` `\"`), raw vs interpolating quotes, `$name` interpolation, arithmetic precedence/associativity, assignment (`k=v; $k`), and nested lists all behave correctly. Two ERROR-MESSAGE-QUALITY gaps (not functional bugs) worth polishing:

1. Incomplete role-index sigils (`^*`, `^_`, `^/`, `^`) with NO index produce the opaque `parse error at token 1: unexpected end of input`. These require an index by design (e.g. `^*-1`, `^_-1`, `^/-1`), but the message doesn't say so. A clearer error would be e.g. `message-index sigil '^*' requires an index (e.g. '^*-1')`. Compare the GOOD error for a valid-but-empty index: `^-1` → `no message at ^-1`.

2. A malformed range like `^0^2` yields `cannot coerce string \`\` to number: no deterministic or model coercion produced a number` — an empty-string coercion error that leaks an internal detail instead of naming the malformed range/index.

Both are in the message-index parse path (my M^N range lane, cf bd-c3fc30). Low priority polish: better diagnostics make the `^` indexing surface much friendlier to dogfood. Filed via reflect-session-style QA from an idle dogfooding pass.

Acceptance: incomplete `^*`/`^_`/`^/`/`^` (no index) and malformed `^`-ranges emit a diagnostic that names the sigil + expected index form, rather than `unexpected end of input` / `coerce empty string to number`.

Addendum (dogfood of the shipped quote-eval forms feature, from nlir-dev-msm-3): same error-message class now also affects the new {…} form syntax — an empty form `{}` (e.g. `{}%5`) reports `parse error at token 1: unexpected token RBrace`, and applying a form with no args (`{$0}%`) reports `unexpected end of input`. A friendlier message would be e.g. "empty form: a {…} form must contain an expression" / "expected arguments after `%`". Minor DX polish; the forms feature is otherwise robust (context-key closure inside form bodies, higher-order application, deep compose, out-of-range `$N`, and non-form apply all behave correctly with clean errors).
creator: ms-mac:nlir:ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-0

title: nlir-wasm P1 prereq: feature-gate the lib's native-only deps (ureq/mcp-cli/updatable-cli/feedback-cli) behind a default `native` feature
description:
nlir-wasm epic bd-360d0c, P1 (bd-3fcf96) keystone prerequisite. Found by authoring crates/nlir-wasm + native-checking: P0 (bd-bec201) gated the effectful CODE behind NativeRealiser #[cfg(not wasm32)], but the nlir lib's native-only Cargo DEPS are still non-optional, so any wasm build compiles them for wasm32 (where they don't build):
- ureq (HTTP) — used only in llm.rs (run_llm)
- mcp-cli / updatable-cli / feedback-cli — used only in lib.rs (build_router, AppError's StructuredError impl, updater_config/feedback_config/status_with) + main.rs (the bin, irrelevant to the wasm lib)

Fix: lib features
  [features] default=["native"]; native=["dep:ureq","dep:mcp-cli","dep:updatable-cli","dep:feedback-cli"]
  (deps → optional=true)
then #[cfg(feature="native")] the mcp/feedback/updatable surfaces in lib.rs + the ureq use in llm.rs. nlir-wasm depends with default-features=false → core only (eval/config/lexer/parser/realise/realiser/context/value/stack/index/messages — already dep-clean). Native default=native keeps today's behaviour + gate green, unchanged.

Owner aur-0 (Cargo.toml + lib.rs gating); the llm.rs ureq gate coordinated with msm-0 (their active lane). Blocks P1 wasm-build; also unblocks aur-0's native cargo-check of crates/nlir-wasm (default-features=false drops the git deps that currently fail ssh-auth resolution).
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-0

title: [nlir][onboarding] checkout refresh persistently fails on ms-mac — git fetch 'No user exists for uid 501', beads stale
description:
## Symptom (routed by log-monitor-msm -> ctrl, 2026-07-03)

nlir (newly onboarded ~13:53 today) has a PERSISTENTLY failing checkout refresh on ms-mac: `POST /api/v1/projects/nlir/checkout/refresh -> 500` (fatal=false) recurring every sweep, keeping nlir's beads STALE (board shows nlir as one of the stale projects).

## Root cause (from ms-mac daemon.log)

    checkout refresh error for nlir (consecutive failures: 40): daemon error: git fetch origin main failed: No user exists for uid 501

40+ consecutive failures. The underlying `git fetch origin main` for nlir's checkout fails with "No user exists for uid 501" (getpwuid(501) failing — uid 501 = the macOS operator user).

## Key discriminator

This is nlir-SPECIFIC: cacophony's checkout refresh on the SAME ms-mac daemon (pid 81113, healthy, ~6h uptime) works fine. So it is NOT a general daemon-env failure — something about nlir's checkout/remote differs.

## NOT the bd-95abff class

This is a git-fetch-ENV error (uid resolution during git fetch), not the bd-95abff silent-materialization-wedge class. Different root; do not fold.

## Fix directions

1. Compare nlir's remote/checkout config vs cacophony's — likely nlir uses a remote type (e.g. SSH) or a git config (core.sshCommand / a credential helper) that triggers a getpwuid(501) lookup the daemon's launchd git-subprocess env can't satisfy, whereas cacophony's remote doesn't.
2. If it's a daemon git-subprocess env gap (missing HOME/USER for the fetch), `services.caco-daemon-extra-env` is inherited by the daemon's child git/subprocess operations (per AGENTS.md) so HOME/USER could be set there — but confirm WHY cacophony is unaffected first before a blanket env change.
3. This is a NEW-PROJECT ONBOARDING settling issue — the nlir owner / config-onboarder (Harry onboarded it ~13:53) should verify nlir's remote + checkout config.

Non-fatal, nlir-specific, ms-mac daemon otherwise healthy. Routed by log-monitor-msm; ctrl found the root cause + filed. cc Harry (nlir onboarder).

## Diagnostic (msm-0, read-only, 2026-07-03 ~19:40)

Refutes fix-direction #1 and narrows to a daemon env bug:

- **Both nlir AND cacophony use `ssh://git@github.com/...` remotes** (checked `caco config show --section projects`). Not a remote-type difference.
- **Both daemon canonical checkouts (`~/.cacophony/daemon/checkouts/{nlir,cacophony}`) have IDENTICAL local git config**: same `core.sshCommand = ssh -F /dev/null -i ~/.ssh/caco -o IdentitiesOnly=yes -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new`, same `remote.origin.url` class, and BOTH have local `user.name`/`user.email` set. So the failure is NOT a checkout-config difference.
- **nlir's checkout is proven functional**: `git -C ~/.cacophony/daemon/checkouts/nlir ls-remote origin main` (HOME set, normal env) returns the ref cleanly. The key/remote/config all work.
- The `No user exists for uid 501` is git's `getpwuid(501)` failing to resolve HOME when HOME is unset in the fetch subprocess. Since cacophony succeeds on the SAME daemon with IDENTICAL config, this is a **daemon env-propagation bug**: the git-fetch subprocess for the newly-onboarded nlir project is missing HOME (existing projects like cacophony inherit it; a project onboarded mid-daemon-run at ~13:53 apparently does not).

### Recommended fix (daemon/operator lane — NOT nlir checkout)
1. Simplest: **restart the ms-mac caco-daemon** so nlir's fetch subprocess inherits HOME like cacophony's (likely clears the 40-consec-failure state, since onboarding mid-run didn't propagate env).
2. If it persists after restart: set `HOME=/Users/harryaskham` (and `USER`) in `services.caco-daemon-extra-env` so every daemon git subprocess has it, or fix the onboarding path to propagate the daemon's env to per-project fetch subprocesses.

nlir's checkout/remote/config need no change. Handing to Harry/ctrl (daemon owners).

CONFIRMED root cause (msm-1, 2026-07-05): nlir's checkout uses an SSH remote + core.sshCommand:
  remote.origin.url = ssh://git@github.com/harryaskham/nlir.git
  core.sshcommand   = ssh -F /dev/null -i /Users/harryaskham/.ssh/caco -o IdentitiesOnly=yes -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new
OpenSSH calls getpwuid(getuid()) at startup to resolve the user's home/name; in the daemon's launchd git-subprocess env that lookup fails for uid 501 -> "No user exists for uid 501" -> git fetch aborts. cacophony's checkout is immune because it does NOT go through ssh (HTTPS/token remote), matching fix-direction #1 exactly.

CONCRETE FIX (operator/daemon-env, NOT an nlir-repo change): either (a) switch nlir's daemon checkout + project remote config from ssh:// to https://github.com/harryaskham/nlir.git with the same token/credential-helper cacophony uses, or (b) give the daemon's launchd git subprocess a resolvable env (export HOME + USER/LOGNAME, or add getpwuid-satisfying context) so ssh's getpwuid(501) succeeds. (a) is the clean durable fix — it removes ssh from the refresh path entirely. Tagging operator-action; needs the operator/controller to change caco project/checkout config (out of scope for an nlir source PR).

CORRECTION (msm-1): my "cacophony uses HTTPS" premise above is WRONG — msm-0's diagnostic already established BOTH nlir and cacophony use ssh:// remotes with identical daemon-checkout config. So the discriminator is NOT remote type; it is HOME env-propagation to nlir's fetch subprocess (nlir onboarded mid-daemon-run didn't inherit HOME, so ssh's getpwuid(501) can't resolve it, while long-running cacophony's subprocess has HOME). Defer to msm-0's recommended fix: restart the ms-mac caco-daemon (re-propagates HOME), or set HOME/USER in services.caco-daemon-extra-env. My only additive confirmation: the failing path IS ssh (core.sshCommand present), consistent with a getpwuid-on-missing-HOME failure. Operator-action stands.
creator: ms-mac:cacophony:ms-mac-cacophony-caco-ctrl

title: nlir dual-game: golf-aur2-opposite.sh (! antonym) + target-aur2-59-antibiotics.sh (:simplify medicine→plain)
description:
nlir dual-game tick (aur-2). Two examples:

FORWARD golf-aur2-opposite.sh — "the opposite": !'increase' -> "decrease", !'hot' -> "cold". The negation operator aimed at a LONE concept-word doesn't just prepend "not" — it flips the concept to its ANTONYM. A one-sigil antonym finder. (! on a full SENTENCE negates its claim; on a lone concept it inverts the concept — a fresh use of !.) Verified.

TARGET target-aur2-59-antibiotics.sh — reverse game via :simplify MEDICINE jargon (antibiotics vs viruses) -> plain (aur-2 : lane), the classic "why don't antibiotics help my cold" misconception. TARGET (~130c) plain antibiotics. EXPR (110c) :'antibiotics kill bacteria but have no effect on viruses which is why they do not help with colds or the flu' -> "Antibiotics are medicines that can kill germs called bacteria. But there's another kind of germ called a virus, and antibiotics can't fight those. Colds and the flu are caused by viruses, so antibiotics can't make them better." Closeness: same fact, plain+accessible (high); : expands into a fuller kid-friendly explanation (register play).

Both verified via claude-sonnet-5.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-2

title: llm-mode operand coercion doesn't use the LLM coercion fallback (eval uses deterministic-only Value::coerce)
description:
Found dogfooding nlir in LLM mode (Harry's directive). In `--mode llm`, operand coercion does NOT use the LLM coercion fallback, so a non-numeric operand to a number operator errors instead of being interpreted by the model.

Repro: `nlir --mode llm -e "'ten'+'twenty'"` -> "cannot coerce string `ten` to number: no deterministic or model coercion produced a number" (exit 1). SPEC explicitly wants this to work: "an LLM coercion — interpret the text as a value of type T ... turns a vague 'ten to twenty' into 15." Expected: 'ten'->10, 'twenty'->20, add -> 30.

Root cause: src/eval.rs coerces operands via `Value::coerce(target, sep)` (the deterministic-only path, bd-20df97) at all 4 coercion sites (lines ~257, 344, 560, 614), never `llm::coerce_with_llm` (the deterministic->LLM->error fallback, bd-ecb930). So step-3 LLM coercion from the config `types:` map is never invoked in llm mode. The misleading error text ("or model coercion") comes from Value::coerce's CoerceError::Unrepresentable, which describes the model step even though Value::coerce never runs it.

This is the eval<->coercion seam (mirrors the realise_llm seam bd-dc3c72): aur-2 owns the coercion API (coerce_with_llm), aur-1 owns eval.rs. Proposed fix: aur-2 adds `llm::coerce_operand(value, target, &Config, sep, mode, env_lookup, cli_model) -> Result<Value, LlmCoerceError>` that dispatches det -> Value::coerce, llm -> coerce_with_llm; eval.rs adds EvalError::LlmCoerce + From<LlmCoerceError> and swaps the 4 `.coerce(...)` sites to it (mode-aware). Det mode behaviour unchanged.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-2

title: Create canonical example conversation demonstrating interactive workflow
description:
Build a canonical example conversation showcasing nlir's interactive capabilities with a realistic multi-turn dialogue. The example should include:
- 3 user turns and 3 assistant turns minimum
- At least 2 tool calls (e.g., reading a short code file via file tool, executing bash command)
- Interactive session context preservation across turns
- Demonstration of slicing current session to construct different user messages
- Clear comments/annotations explaining what's happening at each step
- Example should be realistic and demonstrate practical use case
Acceptance criteria:
- Conversation flows naturally with user and assistant alternating
- Tool calls are integrated seamlessly and show practical file/command operations
- Session context is visibly used to inform responses
- Example can be used as reference material in documentation
creator: helsinki:_:node-token

title: [operator-action][infra] darwin CI runner: job steps can't produce a portable native build (restricted env + nix evals non-darwin system)
description:
The aarch64-darwin release build cannot produce a portable binary from CI because of the self-hosted nix github-runner's job-step environment. Diagnosed exhaustively during bd-7e465a (ms-mac, run 28708607800 and predecessors).

Findings (confirmed on the runner + via log co-reads):
1. Job steps run in a RESTRICTED env: a service user with HOME=/var/empty (echo `lh=/var/empty`), a minimal PATH without /usr/bin, so the system Apple toolchain /usr/bin/cc is unreachable (`cc=none` even after an unconditional `export PATH=/usr/bin:/bin:$PATH`).
2. The login user's rustup toolchain at /Users/harryaskham/.cargo|.rustup is unreadable to the job user (/Users/harryaskham is 0750).
3. So the ONLY reachable toolchain is the nix store. Building inside the flake devShell (`nix develop --command cargo build`) DOES compile nlir on the runner (2m06s). BUT the runner's `nix develop` resolves a NON-darwin devShell (it copies stdenv-linux + gcc-wrapper-14.3.0), whereas the SAME flake on the same aarch64-darwin host locally resolves clang-wrapper-21 + cctools. Result: the devShell lacks darwin cctools, so the portability post-step fails with `otool: command not found` (exit 127). `nix eval builtins.currentSystem` locally = aarch64-darwin.

So the runner's nix is evaluating a non-aarch64-darwin system for job steps. release.yml cannot work around this cleanly.

Proposed fixes (pick one):
(a) Run darwin runner job steps as the login user (harryaskham) with /usr/bin + Xcode CLT on PATH, so the existing native-cargo (rust-not-nix) release.yml works and yields a portable binary. Simplest to reason about.
(b) Fix the runner's nix so `nix develop`/builds evaluate aarch64-darwin (clang-wrapper + cctools). Then the devShell + `install_name_tool` libiconv-rewrite approach (already in release.yml, bd-7e465a) completes.

Interim already available: a portable nlir-0.1.1-aarch64-darwin.tar.gz was built + verified locally on ms-mac (otool shows only /usr/lib/libiconv.2.dylib + libSystem; runs `nlir 0.1.1`; sha256 5ee3319e9e78aed0535f33c0e6c5fb4d18ed34a749095f1eb78e0da443d9fa2a) and can be attached to v0.1.1.

Acceptance: a release.yml -f tag=v0.1.x dispatch produces + attaches nlir-<v>-aarch64-darwin.tar.gz whose `otool -L` shows only /usr/lib deps, from CI, with no manual step.

UPDATE (msm-0, further diagnosis): The workflow layer is exhausted. Landed the explicit-devShell fix (`nix develop '.#devShells.aarch64-darwin.default'`, adcf099) to force the right system — that CORRECTLY targets aarch64-darwin, but it surfaced the true blocker: the github-runner's nix cannot BUILD the darwin flake. Run 28719755717 darwin job: `error: Cannot build …-feedback-cli-patched.drv` → `error: Cannot build …-nlir-src.drv` → `… __impureHostDeps of derivation 'nix-shell'` → `error: Build failed due to failed dependency`.

Two different nixes on the one Mac: the github-runner SERVICE nix is 2.34.7 (`system=aarch64-darwin`, `extra-platforms=aarch64-darwin x86_64-darwin`, `builders=@/etc/nix/machines`) and FAILS to build the darwin devShell (a dep, feedback-cli-patched, won't build; __impureHostDeps darwin-sandbox host-deps). The login/interactive nix is Determinate Nix 3.13.2 and builds the identical flake devShell fine (verified locally: 2m49s, portable binary, otool clean).

So the infra fix narrows to: make the github-runner build darwin like the interactive Determinate nix — point the runner service at Determinate Nix 3.13.2, OR fix its darwin sandbox / __impureHostDeps / /etc/nix/machines builder config so darwin derivations build locally. Once the runner can build the darwin devShell, the landed `.#devShells.aarch64-darwin.default` + otool libiconv-rewrite completes and yields a portable CI asset. Interim locally-built asset (Determinate nix, sha 5ee3319e…) remains ready to attach.

UPDATE 2 (msm-0): `--builders ''` was INSUFFICIENT. Re-dispatch (run 28721262361, commit 22f1279) applied the flag (confirmed in job log line 171) but failed IDENTICALLY: `Cannot build feedback-cli-patched.drv` → `nlir-src.drv` → `__impureHostDeps of nix-shell` → `Build failed due to failed dependency`. So it is NOT builder-delegation — the restricted CI job env (service user, HOME=/var/empty) cannot build the flake dep feedback-cli-patched (network/substituter/sandbox), dying at eval of the devShell's __impureHostDeps. In-workflow fixes are now exhausted (explicit devShell + --builders '' both landed, both correct, neither sufficient in the restricted env). Durable fix is runner-level (aur-0's path): ~/collective/modules/home/git.nix runner devshell / service env (network + darwin build capability) + darwin-rebuild. Interim locally-built portable asset (sha 5ee3319e…) remains the fast unblock.
creator: ms-mac:nlir:ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-0

title: Implement glyph composition using dot notation for polymorphic form joining
description: Implement glyph composition syntax using '.' (dot) operator to enable polymorphic composition of forms, operators, and builtins. Support both standard form composition and LLM mode where dot-composition joins two words/concepts together polymorphically in the prompt, treating it as a conceptual union rather than a literal operation. This allows users to compose complex glyphs from simpler building blocks and enables more expressive prompt engineering in LLM mode.

title: SPEC: `_seed` doc overstates reproducibility — no configured backend honors seed (add caveat)
description:
aur-0's empirical honor-matrix (post _seed landing bd-72d6d3/a15ca41ab): NO reachable backend honors `seed` for reproducible output — claude CLI has no seed, Anthropic Messages no native param, the reachable OpenAI-compatible model (gpt-5-mini @ :4000) gave 3 different outputs at seed=42, gpt-4o unavailable. But SPEC L60-64 currently claims "OpenAI-compatible backends reproduce output" — which OVERSTATES reality (the reachable OpenAI-compat model does NOT reproduce).

FIX (aur-1 SPEC lane, docs-match-reality — same principle as bd-4d1f9c `#` / bd-7ec58b `..`): soften the `_seed` system-key doc to reflect that reproducibility is backend-dependent AND no currently-configured backend honors seed, so `_seed` is presently a no-op (ready for a seed-honoring backend). The threading is correct + ready (my spec + msm-3's impl validated) — this is a backend-availability gap, not an impl gap.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-1

title: nlir-wasm: graphFrames_async export — wire step_frames_async for llm-mode in-browser graph animation
description:
Follow-up to graph-viz (epic bd-8ac9ad) + nlir-wasm (bd-360d0c). The det-mode in-browser graph animation (G5 "Animate ▸") drives the SYNC eval::step_frames via the wasm crate's graphFrames() export. LLM-mode in-browser graph animation (watch an operator realise as the dataflow graph evolves) needs the ASYNC path.

The eval-lane primitive already exists + is landed: `eval::step_frames_async<R: Realiser>(expr, &Config, &mut Context, mode, &R) -> Result<Vec<graph::Frame>, EvalError>` (@e45adc9, mine) — mirrors step_frames but drives step_once_async, awaiting the injected Realiser at effectful redexes (det never awaits). Same Frame{graph, reduced} output, so aur-1's graph_svg::render(&frame.graph) + the G5 scrubber/highlight are unchanged.

Task (crates/nlir-wasm, aur-0's lane): add a `graphFrames_async(expr, configJson, contextJson, mode, realisers) -> Promise<{ok, frames:[{svg|graph, reduced}]|error}>` export that calls step_frames_async with the JsRealiser (exactly how step()/step_async and evaluate()/evaluate_async pair). Then G5's Animate can offer llm-mode (behind the LLM settings, like the run path). Non-blocking: det animation already works via the sync path; this only lights up llm-mode animation.

Acceptance: wasm export wired to step_frames_async; G5 llm-mode Animate plays realiser-driven frames; native + wasm builds unchanged (step_frames_async is pure/cross-platform, already on main). Eval-lane primitive done (msm-0); this is the wasm-crate wiring.
creator: ms-mac:nlir:ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-0

title: nlir tui: Ctrl-P palette shows the full grammar (quote/apply/assign/…), not just operators
description: Operator request (Harry, 2026-07-05): "add syntax primer to the workspace — we can see operators but the builtin syntax is complicated, not obvious how to quote, eval, assign, call, etc. include the full surface of the language." This bead covers the TERMINAL workbench surface: the nlir tui Ctrl-P palette previously listed only config operators, so the grammar special forms (quote {}, apply/call %, assign =, reference $name, sequence ;, grouping (), list [], message refs ^, do-N ({f}_N), strings, serial `) were undiscoverable in-app. Now the palette is a full syntax primer: special forms first (tagged "syntax"), then operators (tagged det/llm), each with a one-line summary; Enter inserts a sensible token. Curated special-forms list mirrors the `nlir help` special-forms section. Title renamed Operators -> Syntax. The WEB workspace equivalent is aur-1's page (coordinating separately).

title: nlir power-move #24: the compare-and-contrast ~>'the difference between X and Y' — one crisp distinction
description:
nlir power-move #24 (aur-2) — "the compare-and-contrast": the ONE crisp sentence that says how two concepts actually differ. The classic "what's the difference between X and Y?" — for docs, interview prep, teaching, design reviews. Sibling of the glossary entry (which defines ONE term; this distinguishes TWO).

SHAPE: ~>'the difference between X and Y' — ~> (expand then distil) draws out the real distinguishing axis (ownership, timing, scope, ...) in one accurate sentence; plain > alone spills into a multi-paragraph essay (verified), ~ reins it in. Optional plain pairing: [~>'the difference between X and Y', :'... in plain terms'] = technical contrast + everyday analogy.

Filled + REAL (claude-sonnet-5): ~>'the difference between a mutex and a semaphore' -> "A mutex enforces ownership-based exclusive locking for a single resource, while a semaphore uses a non-owned counter to allow flexible, multi-threaded access to a pool of resources." (also tested optimistic vs pessimistic locking + a two-kids-and-paper plain analogy via the : pairing)

Deliverables: examples/move-aur2-contrast.sh (runs nlir live, --examples gate exit 0), POWERMOVES.md quick-ref row, showcase card nlir-compare-contrast.png (my 14th card; mutex vs semaphore; single --only regen, honest llm pill) + scripts/build-showcase.py row.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-2

title: G0: dataflow graph model + extraction (core src/graph.rs) — AST + binding-reference resolution [KEYSTONE]
description:
Graph-viz epic slice G0 (KEYSTONE — unblocks all rendering). Proposed lane: msm-0 (AST/eval owner) or shared.

Build the DATAFLOW GRAPH MODEL from a parsed nlir expression, in a new pure core module (e.g. src/graph.rs):
- Walk `parser::Expr` → a Graph { nodes, edges }. Node per AST node: Apply (op), literals (Bare/Number/Quoted), ContextRead, StackPeek/Index, Message/MessageRange, List, Group, Serial, Assign, Value.
- OPERAND edges: an Apply node → each of its operands (data flows operand → op).
- BINDING-REFERENCE RESOLUTION (the key ask): an `Assign{key}` node gets a dataflow edge to EVERY `ContextRead(key)` node that uses it as input — so a variable assignment visibly feeds the nodes that consume it (resolve references, not just syntax nesting). Handle shadowing/scoping sanely (last assignment wins by eval order).
- TEXT TRUNCATION helper: `truncate_ends(s, head_words, tail_words)` → "first few words … last few words", so long literals / model-output Values render consistently. Shared by all renderers.
- Node identity is STABLE (an id per AST position) so step frames (G2) can keep a consistent layout as subtrees reduce to `Expr::Value`.

Pure + cross-platform (no native deps) so it compiles into the wasm core too. Unit tests: operand edges for `2**3**2`, binding edges for `k='x';[$k,~$k]` (Assign k → both ContextReads), truncation. This is the shared substrate G1 (SVG) + G3 (CLI) + G5 (wasm) all render from.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-2

title: Decide where MessageViews SPEC defaults live (config resolve vs messages fallback)
description: SPEC §Example config defines canonical `context.messages.views` defaults (`^`=[assistant], `^_`=[user], `^*`=[user,assistant,system], `^/`=[system]), but config `MessageViews::default()` is all-empty and `resolve_defaults` (src/config.rs) does not populate them. So the built-in `^` behaviour (and the SPEC `msg` test `^-1`) only works because src/messages.rs `effective_roles()` bakes those SPEC defaults as a fallback for an empty configured view. That's a reasonable seam, but it splits "view defaults" across two modules. Decision for the config owner: either (a) centralize the SPEC view defaults in config defaults-resolution (then messages could drop its fallback), or (b) keep them in the messages layer and document config `MessageViews::default()` as intentionally empty. Either is fine; flagging so the two don't silently diverge. Filed via reflect-session mixin from bd-f9809a/bd-e8064e/bd-43ac5e session.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-1

title: nlir coding-pipe move: the PR summary (git diff | nlir -e '[#$_stdin, ~$_stdin]') — #36
description:
nlir power-moves tick (aur-2) — FRESH CODING-PIPE MOVE (my first on Harry's smart-pipe direction, #36). "the PR summary": pipe a diff, get a title + a description.

SHAPE (as a PIPE): `git diff | nlir -e '[#$_stdin, ~$_stdin]'`. nlir is a smart pipe ($_stdin = whatever you pipe in, my earlier feature). #$_stdin = the SUBJECT of the change (a PR title); ~$_stdin = the whole diff distilled (the body). Two list elements → a ready-to-paste PR title + description. Distinct from aur-0's commit-pipe (~$_stdin, a one-line commit message) — this pairs a #-title with the fuller write-up.

Real (claude-sonnet-5, a sample verify_token-hardening diff piped in): -> "Token verification logic in `verify_token`\nThe diff updates `verify_token` to reject `None` tokens and also check for expiration, instead of only checking session membership."

Files (mine only, per divvy — coding-pipe MOVES are mine, CARDS are aur-0's): examples/move-aur2-prsummary.sh (SHAPE+pipe+filled+real output; pipes a sample diff into nlir live; runs exit 0) + examples/POWERMOVES.md (quick-ref table row). No build-showcase.py card — @aur-0 owns the coding-pipe showcase cards (they render `cat file | nlir` cards); flagging this move for them to card if they like.

title: Parallelism may not concurrency-run independent llm operand subcalls (30s for 4-way; investigate scheduler vs HTTP-client mutex vs endpoint)
description:
Driving nlir in llm mode: independent LLM operand subtrees do NOT appear to run concurrently despite the parallelism epic (bd-a32894, std::thread::scope, defaults.parallelism=8). Evidence (anthropic_messages HTTP backend against a real endpoint):
- single `!('the sky is blue')` ≈ 4s
- `!(a)&!(b)` (2 independent ! + &) ≈ 14s
- `!(a)&!(b)&!(c)&!(d)` (4 independent ! + &) ≈ 30s

30s ≈ 5 sequential ~5s calls; concurrent would be ~2 call-times (~10s). So the 4 independent `!` subcalls are running sequentially, not concurrently.

Needs investigation (aur-1's parallelism lane): does the scheduler parallelize the operands of a single n-ary Apply (the `&` operands)? Is the anthropic_messages ureq HTTP client behind a shared mutex that serializes? Or does the endpoint rate-limit? Reproduce with a command-backend model (claude CLI) to rule out endpoint serialization. Config: defaults.parallelism=8, per-operand `!` uses model sonnet (anthropic_messages). Found by msm-0 while building the llm-mode working-cases library; may be endpoint-side, hence P3 pending confirmation.

## Refined diagnosis (msm-0 + aur-1, 2026-07-03)

NOT a scheduler bug. Confirmed the scheduler + COMMAND backends DO parallelize:
- A `command:` operator that `sleep 2`s: 4-way `~a&~b&~c&~d` = 2.1s (not 8s) — concurrent.
- aur-1: 3 concurrent subject extractions via command (claude/pi CLI) joined ~13s — parallelism live.

So the ~30s serialization I saw is SPECIFIC to the `anthropic_messages` HTTP path against the LiteLLM endpoint (helsinki:4000). nlir uses a FRESH `ureq::post` per call (no shared client/Mutex in llm.rs), so it is NOT a nlir lock. Most likely LiteLLM/Claude endpoint concurrency limits serialize the concurrent HTTP requests, or ureq's default agent behavior. Downgrading: probably endpoint-side, not a nlir bug. To fully confirm nlir fires concurrent HTTP, a local sleeping mock-HTTP anthropic server would settle it. Separately noticed `--parallelism 1` did not force sequential in the sleep test (flag→scheduler wiring gap worth a small check).

## RESOLVED — direct HTTP path DOES parallelize (aur-1, real-endpoint evidence)

Tested the anthropic_messages HTTP backend against the LiteLLM endpoint at the CORRECT URL: http://helsinki.miku-owl.ts.net:4000/v1 (note: the operator's given URL http://.../v1 was missing the :4000 port → port 80 connection-refused from aurora; LITELLM_BASE_URL=http://helsinki.miku-owl.ts.net:4000). Config defaults.model=direct, op with no model override (so the direct backend actually runs, not a command backend).

Clean before/after timing (3 concurrent subject extractions + join):
- single #'x' baseline = 4s
- #'a'&#'b'&#'c' at parallelism 8 = 8s (~2x baseline = 3 subjects CONCURRENT + 1 join)
- same at --parallelism 1 = 17s (~4x baseline = fully sequential)

8s vs 17s => the direct HTTP path parallelizes correctly. The scheduler parallelizes ALL backends (command AND anthropic_messages HTTP). The earlier ~30s 4-way observation was transient endpoint behaviour (cold/loaded), NOT a systematic nlir serialization or client-mutex bug. No mock-HTTP server needed — real-endpoint data settles it. Closing.

(The --parallelism-flag-not-wired sub-note was split to bd-e9dedc and is LANDED.)
creator: ms-mac:nlir:ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-0

title: Map/fold a form over a list (+ lists as first-class results) — the next functional primitive
description:
Harry (2026-07-05): "it's not clear how to do things like mapping a form over a list, or building real programs out that leverage the new forms."

CORE GAP: there is no way to map a {…} form over each element of a list today. `{$0+1}%[1,2,3]` is an ARG-FRAME (binds $0=1,$1=2,$2=3 → returns 2), NOT a per-item map. docs/design/quote-eval.md already calls this out: "there is no general per-item map today. map.({:$0},[a,b,c]) is precisely that: it returns a LIST [:a,:b,:c], not a join. Forms are what make a real map/fold expressible." do-N-times (`_`) landed; map/fold over a LIST is the designed next functional primitive, not yet built. This bead = build it (the answer to Harry's ask).

SYNTAX PROBLEM: the design doc wrote `map.(form,list)` but Harry has since RESERVED `.` (quote-eval.md D2). So the `.` call syntax is out — need a settled syntax. Candidates for msm-0 (parser/eval lead) + aur-1 (sigil/operator-consolidator lane) + Harry:
  (A) builtin `map`/`fold` forms pre-bound in every context, applied via the existing `%`: `$map%({$0*$0},[1,2,3])` → [1,4,9]; `$fold%({$0+$1},[1,2,3,4])` → 10. Reuses apply + arg-frame + the `$name%args` named-lambda path — zero new glyphs. (Recommended.)
  (B) a dedicated map glyph — but the only free ASCII is `\` (earmarked for macro-splice), so this costs the last glyph.
  (C) an apply-map marker distinguishing map-each from the arg-frame meaning of `%[list]`.

FOUNDATIONAL SUB-GAP (map "returns a LIST", so this is needed too): lists are not first-class RESULTS yet — a bare `[1,4,9]` collapses to its last element `9` in det mode (lists are only consumed by spreading ops like `&[a,b,c]`). Map/fold must yield + render a real list value. So this likely pairs with "lists as first-class values" (Value::List rendering).

RELATED ERGONOMIC GOTCHAS found while probing (fix alongside or as follow-ups):
  1. do-N on a NAMED form breaks: `($inc_5)` parses `inc_5` as one identifier ("unknown context key inc_5"). Workaround: parenthesize the read — `(($inc)_5)%0` → 5. Worth a lexer/parse fix or a doc note.
  2. `[$sq%1,$sq%2,$sq%3]` returns 9 (last), not [1,4,9] — same lists-aren't-first-class-results issue.

WHAT WORKS TODAY (for the cookbook): named lambdas via `$f%args` (f={$0+1};$f%5→6), compose ($inc%($dbl%5)→11), nest ($sq%($sq%2)→16, $add%($add%(1,2),$add%(3,4))→10), inline do-N (({$0+1}_3)%5→8), deep arithmetic. The forms are a real functional core over scalars — map/fold + first-class lists are what extend it to list-processing "programs".

Lane: eval-core map/fold + Value::List = msm-0 (eval) / aur-1 (sigil). Tests + showcase/cookbook = me (msm-1, quote-eval test/docs lead). Needs Harry's/msm-0's syntax ruling first (recommend A).

CONVERGENCE (2026-07-05) — 3-way team alignment (msm-1 + aur-1 sigil-lane + aur-0 QA/map-law author). Refined framing collapses the decision:

1. $map/$fold as builtin forms via the existing % need NO sigil ruling — they're just more named forms (zero new glyphs; \ stays reserved for macro-splice). Nothing for Harry to rule on syntax.
2. Clean, non-breaking semantics (aur-0's map-law): implicit op[list] already folds to joined text (existing) vs explicit structural $map%(form,list) → per-item, returns a list (new). Two distinct, non-overlapping behaviours.
3. THE ONE DECISION Harry/msm-0 owe: do lists become FIRST-CLASS VALUES? Today a bare [1,4,9] collapses (to 9/joined text), so map/fold have nowhere to put their result. This is the whole weight of the bead: a Value::List variant (render [1,4,9] + persist + coerce + no auto-collapse in a value slot) — the exact parallel to the Value::Form shipped for quote-eval, so aur-2's Value::Form work is the implementation template.

Team recommendation to Harry: YES to lists-as-first-class-values. Once approved: msm-0 builds Value::List + the $map/$fold builtins; aur-1 rewrites quote-eval.md map/fold; msm-1 adds tests + cookbook/showcase idioms; aur-0 dogfoods slice-by-slice. DECISION-READY — needs only Harry's yes on first-class lists.

CORRECTION (2026-07-05, msm-0 eval-lane catch): the "lists collapse / lists-as-first-class-results sub-gap" framed above is a MIRAGE — my error. Lists ARE already first-class and render sep-joined: [1,4,9] -> 1/4/9, and [$sq%1,$sq%2,$sq%3] -> 1/4/9 (a manual map already works today). The "-> 9" I reported was the last line of a multi-line result printed inline by the CLI "EXPR -> RESULT" summary, grabbed by my own tail -1. So there is NO Value::List / lists-first-class eval refactor to do — the enabler is already met (Value::List + render join-with-sep exist).

Net: bd-14af74 reduces to JUST the map/fold engine builtins (msm-0's eval lane) — map/fold as reserved builtins detected at % application (higher-order: apply the form per-item over the list / reduce). Syntax is team-settled + needs no ruling (glyph-free $map/$fold via %). Optional follow-up: bracketed [1,4,9] display mode (aur-2's call; sep-join is today's default). So this now needs only Harry's thumbs-up on glyph-free $map/$fold, then msm-0 builds the engine (msm-1 tests/cookbook, aur-1 docs). Cookbook's incorrect "lists collapse" gotcha fixed @9f0da3c + locked with cookbook-list-firstclass / cookbook-manual-map tests.

title: nlir repl: :step interactive step-through UX (crossterm shell over the step engine)
description:
Harry ask #1 of 2 (sgu24-app 2026-07-05): "in nlir repl, pressing tab to expand only the next step of the eval and inline it — see it realtime, learn the lang."

Scope (aur-0, the REPL UX half; msm-0 owns the step ENGINE bd-9c366d): a `:step EXPR` REPL meta-command that enters a raw-mode (crossterm, TTY-gated) step viewer — Tab/Space expands the next eval step and reprints the partially-reduced expression inline, Enter runs to completion, q/Esc/Ctrl-C cancels. Non-TTY (pipes/tests) degrades to a single full evaluation so scripted REPL use is unchanged.

Seam: msm-0's `Evaluator::step_once(&mut self, &Expr) -> Step{Reduced(Expr),Done(Value)}` + precedence-aware `Expr::render_step()` (landing to main independently, ⊥ the held Δ+bare-views grammar). Parent: REPL epic bd-c6719d.

title: size-tier models: defaults.model.{small,medium,large} + op model tiers (back-compat)
description:
Harry-directed size-tier models feature. `defaults.model` can now be `{small, medium, large}` size tiers, and an operator's `model:` may name a tier (small|medium|large) which resolves through defaults.model.<tier>. Fully back-compatible: a single `defaults.model: sonnet` (concrete alias) still works, and an op's concrete `model: haiku` still resolves directly. Unblocks aur-1's file-load op (next in the config-edit sequence).

Impl (my config/models lane):
- src/config.rs: new `#[serde(untagged)] enum DefaultModel { Alias(String), Tiers(ModelTiers) }` + `struct ModelTiers { small, medium, large: Option<String> }`; `Defaults.model: Option<String>` → `Option<DefaultModel>`. Methods `tier(name)` (tier→concrete alias) + `default_alias()` (Alias, else medium tier) + `referenced_aliases()` (for validation). Validation: defaults.model checks every referenced tier/alias names a defined model; op/type `model:` may be a tier (new `model_alias_is_valid` helper) or a concrete model. resolve_defaults uses `default_alias()`.
- src/llm.rs: `resolve_model` tier-resolves the operator/CLI model through defaults tiers (else passthrough); no operator/CLI model → the default alias (single or medium). `coercion_model_key` tier-resolves too (correct cache key).
- config.example.yaml: `defaults.model` → `{small: haiku, medium: sonnet, large: copilot}`; the 13 ops' `model: sonnet` → `model: medium` (behaviour-identical: medium=sonnet) to demonstrate op-level tiers.

Gate green: fmt + clippy -D + cargo test (237, incl new size_tiers_resolve_via_defaults_and_stay_back_compatible: tier→concrete, concrete back-compat, medium fallback, unset tier → UnknownModel) + example_config_parses_and_validates + nlir test 18/18. (The parser fuzz SIGABRT is msm-0's pre-existing intermittent flake, skipped.)

title: nlir binary vs config schema drift: unknown-field parse failure needs a friendlier error / forward-compat
description:
Friction (reflect-session, from bd-d18743 dogfood session): the installed nlir
binary drifts from the config schema and hard-fails with an unhelpful error.

Observed: `~/.nix-profile/bin/nlir` predated the `assoc` operator field (landed
via bd-df62f1), so it refused to parse the live `~/.config/nlir/config.yaml` at
all, printing:

  nlir: failed to parse config .../config.yaml: unknown field `assoc`,
  expected one of `op`, `arity`, `fixity`, ...

This is a total loss of function for the whole tool, from one additive config
field, with no hint that the fix is "rebuild/update nlir". Every field added to
the operator/config schema silently strips usability from any not-yet-rebuilt
install.

Fix-shape suggestions (pick one, generic):
- Friendlier error: when serde rejects an unknown field, detect the
  binary-older-than-config case and suggest `nlir self-update` / rebuild,
  rather than a bare serde "unknown field" list.
- Forward-compat: make additive operator/config fields non-fatal (warn +
  ignore unknown fields, `#[serde(deny_unknown_fields)]` relaxed for the
  operator table, or a config `schema_version` the binary can compare and
  surface a clear "config newer than binary" message).

Impact: agents/users whose installed nlir lags the checked-in config get a
confusing dead tool until they rebuild. Filed via reflect-session mixin from
the bd-d18743 dogfood session (aur-2).

Addendum (aur-1, consolidating dup bd-6b4616): immediate operator remedy is `just sync-install` (or rebuild ./target/*/nlir) so the installed binary matches the checkout schema. Extra impact worth noting: because the failure surfaces as a generic "unknown field" on load, an agent reasoning from it can reach a confidently-wrong "the config is broken" diagnosis when the real cause is a stale binary — which argues for the friendlier "config newer than binary → rebuild/self-update" error you suggested. My dup bd-6b4616 discarded in favour of this one.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-2

title: nlir power-move + card: respectful dissent (@&[!PROPOSAL,REASONING,ALT]) — my 34th
description:
nlir power-moves tick (aur-2) — FRESH MOVE + CARD (my 34th). "respectful dissent": disagree on principle, constructively.

SHAPE: @&[!THE_PROPOSAL, MY_REASONING, WHAT_ID_SUPPORT_INSTEAD]. Stronger than partial-accept (yes-some/no-some) — a full reasoned NO to a proposal, softened by your reasoning + a constructive alternative you'd back. GOTCHA (learned + documented in the .sh): the ! slot NEGATES, so give it the POSITIVE proposal ('!we should rewrite...'), NOT an already-negative one — a double negative flips the meaning + the model flags the contradiction.

Real (claude-sonnet-5): @&['!we should rewrite the billing system from scratch this quarter','the current one is ugly but it works and handles years of edge cases we would have to rediscover the hard way','I would support carving out the worst module and refactoring it behind the existing interface instead'] -> "I would advise against undertaking a complete rewrite of the billing system this quarter. While the current system is inelegant, it remains functional and accounts for years of accumulated edge cases that we would otherwise need to rediscover through a difficult and time-consuming process. I would instead support isolating the most problematic module and refactoring it while preserving the existing interface."

Files (mine only): examples/move-aur2-dissent.sh (SHAPE+slots+filled+real output + the !-slot gotcha; runs live, exit 0), examples/POWERMOVES.md (quick-ref table row + COMPOSE moves list), scripts/build-showcase.py card row + showcase/nlir-respectful-dissent.png (single --only regen, honest llm pill). 34 moves + 34 cards.

title: wasm llm realiser errors collapse to "{}" and mislabel as "operator command" — surface the real JS error
description:
Operator (Harry) reported on the latest WASM build: `llm realisation failed: realisation via operator command: js realiser rejected: {}`.

Two defects, both in crates/nlir-wasm/src/lib.rs:

1. USELESS `{}` (the reported symptom): `js_err_string` fell back to `JSON.stringify(error)`, which returns "{}" for any JS `Error` because `name`/`message`/`stack` are NON-ENUMERABLE own properties. The workspace `llm` realiser throws informative errors — `new Error('llm endpoint ' + res.status)` (e.g. "llm endpoint 401") and a failed `fetch` throws `TypeError: Failed to fetch` — but all of them were collapsed to "{}", hiding the real cause. Fix: read the JS Error's `name`/`message` (via `dyn_ref::<js_sys::Error>()`), then `.message` reflect for non-Error error-likes, and only use JSON.stringify for plain data objects (ignoring an empty "{}"). Now surfaces e.g. "Error: llm endpoint 401" / "TypeError: Failed to fetch".

2. MISLEADING PREFIX: every wasm realiser failure (including the `llm` path) was wrapped as `RealiseError::OperatorCommand` → "realisation via operator command: ...", which is confusing for an LLM-endpoint failure (reads like a shell command failed). Added a dedicated `RealiseError::Realiser(String)` variant → "realisation via realiser: ..." and switched the wasm `realise_err` to it. (This is exactly the "dedicated Realiser variant" the P3 code comment flagged as a nice-to-have.)

Net: Harry's opaque "...operator command: js realiser rejected: {}" now renders as e.g. "...realisation via realiser: js realiser rejected: Error: llm endpoint 401", revealing the true cause (endpoint status / CORS / model-id). The underlying rejection itself is environmental (endpoint/key/CORS/model), now diagnosable.

Validated: cargo fmt, native clippy -D warnings, 240 lib tests, cargo check + clippy for wasm32-unknown-unknown on nlir-wasm — all green. Full .wasm bundle build is CI (wasm.yml).
creator: ms-mac:nlir:ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-3

title: Dictionaries + polymorphic `.` accessor + semantic `..` accessor
description:
Harry's request (2026-07-07): add dictionaries/records + a polymorphic `.` accessor, plus a semantic `..` accessor that goes through the LLM.

## `.` — polymorphic structural accessor (deterministic, infix)
Dispatch on the LEFT operand's value type:
- **List** → index (0-based): `[0,1,2].1 == 1`
- **Dict/record** → key lookup: `{k=v}.k == v`
- **String** → char at index (0-based): `"the".2 == "e"`  (t=0, h=1, e=2)

One polymorphic accessor unifying list-index (today `$nth`), dict-access (new), and string-char (new) — composable-core aligned (one op, polymorphic on type), same spirit as the other primitives. Likely high-priority infix binding so `x.k` binds tight.

## `..` — semantic accessor (LLM-realised, infix)
On strings, `..` makes an LLM call to resolve a *described* access:
- `"the planets from the sun"..3 == "earth"`  (the 3rd planet from the sun; sun→mercury(1),venus(2),earth(3))

So `..` is the LLM twin of `.`: `.` = deterministic structural access, `..` = semantic access the model realises. Mirrors the existing det/llm duality (e.g. `~>` det=containment / llm=implies; the `det:` field). `..` would have a `model:` for llm mode and a det stub (structural fallback or "the Nth described by X").

## Dictionary value type
- Literal `{k=v, k2=v2}` (key=value pairs) → a Dict value.
- **Disambiguation from FORM syntax**: `{...}` currently means a form (`{$0*2}`). Need to distinguish: a `{...}` whose top-level body is one-or-more `key=value` assignments → Dict; a `{...}` with an expression body → Form. (Design decision — could also key off presence of `=` at top level. Note assignment `k=v` already exists in Serial context; a dict literal reuses that shape.)
- Dict renders as its pairs; `.k` reads a value; supports the `.` accessor above.

## Design notes / open questions
- `.` fixity/priority: infix, tight-binding (tighter than `%`?). Polymorphic value-dispatch at eval (like the `$if`/`$nth`/`$sort`/`$contains` value-builtins, but as an infix `.` operator).
- `.` vs float literals: `3.14` — ensure the lexer still reads a decimal number and doesn't split on `.`. `.` as accessor applies between a value and a key/index, not inside a numeric literal. Lexer longest-match care (like the `==` / `=>` cases).
- `..` vs `.`: longest-match `..` over `.` (same lexer discipline as `==`/`=>`, op.len()>1 guard aur-1 generalized).
- Dict literal vs form disambiguation is the main parser design decision.
- Relationship to `$nth`: `.` subsumes list-indexing; keep `$nth` (negative-from-end) or unify? `.` could also support negative indices.

## Acceptance
- `[0,1,2].1` → `1`; `"the".2` → `e`; `{k=v}.k` → `v` (det).
- `"the planets from the sun"..3` → `earth` (llm mode).
- Lexer: `3.14` still a number; `..` longest-matches over `.`.
- Canonical `tests:` entries for the det cases + an llm-gated `..` case.
- Coordinate with aur-1 (grammar/sigils) + aur-2 (value/config/types) — dict is a new Value variant.
creator: ms-mac:nlir:ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-0

title: Concurrent in-flight subcall dedup (two simultaneous identical LLM calls both compute)
description: The per-run realisation cache (bd-1d078c) is a Mutex<HashMap> that dedups SEQUENTIAL identical subcalls, but two truly-simultaneous identical subcalls on different scoped threads (bd-780dbf) may BOTH miss the cache before either stores, so both compute — no in-flight dedup. For pure det ops this is harmless (same result); for identical concurrent LLM calls it means a duplicate model call. Fix: an in-flight registry keyed by the realise cache key holding a shared "computing" placeholder (Condvar/OnceLock) so a second thread waits for the first's result. Low priority; correctness holds either way. Filed via reflect-session from the bd-780dbf/bd-1d078c parallelism session.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-1

title: Wire double-quote $name interpolation into eval (lexer must carry the quote kind)
description:
SPEC §Interpolation: a double-quoted string `"…$name…"` interpolates bare `$name` at eval time; single-quoted `'…'` (raw) and bare literals do not. The interpolation FUNCTION exists (context::interpolate, bd-22fa7e), but it is NOT wired into the evaluator, so `nlir -e 'k=x;"v is $k"'` yields the literal "v is $k" instead of "v is x".

Root cause: the lexer collapses both `"…"` and `'…'` into a single `Token::Quoted(String)` with no flag distinguishing interpolating (double) from raw (single) quotes, so the parser's `Expr::Quoted` can't tell eval whether to interpolate.

Fix (cross-lane):
1. Lexer (msm-0's lane): carry the quote kind — e.g. `Token::Quoted { content, interpolate: bool }` (true for `"…"`, false for `'…'`) or a distinct token — and likewise `Expr::Quoted`/a new `Expr::Interpolated` in the parser.
2. Eval (aur-1): in eval, call `self.context.interpolate(&content)` for interpolating strings (I already have Context::interpolate); leave raw/bare literal.

Discovered by aur-1 while adding greedy eval-time read tests (bd-91e573). I can do the eval-side wiring once the lexer/parser carries the interpolate flag.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-1

- semantic:   `'the planets from the sun'..3` → "Earth" (llm — index into a DESCRIBED sequence)

Same answer "Earth", two access modes — a self-documenting duality. det-exact so it's the strongest CI gate (verify-showcase re-runs it in det mode and asserts "Earth" exactly). Verified locally: `[Mercury,Venus,Earth,Mars].2` → Earth; `.-1` → Mars (negative-from-end).

title: Expand operators list schema with complete operator definitions
description:
The operators list currently only shows a limited set (+, /, -, >, @). Create a comprehensive schema that includes all discovered and useful operators, their syntax, semantics, and behavior. Include documentation of builtin operators and create a NLIR primer explaining the operator system. Acceptance criteria: (1) Complete schema defined for all operators, (2) Builtin operators documented, (3) NLIR primer created explaining operators and syntax, (4) Schema covers operator precedence, arity, and semantics.

TRIAGE (aur-2, config/operators lane, 2026-07-07): this bead's premise is STALE. The "operators list currently only shows a limited set (+, /, -, >, @)" no longer holds — the operator schema/reference has since been built out comprehensively. Current state against the four acceptance criteria:

(1) Complete schema for all operators — MET. `config.example.yaml` defines the full operator table (25 operators via `nlir help`), each with op/arity/fixity/priority/assoc/mode. `nlir help` renders a config-derived reference grouped by fixity (prefix/infix/postfix/mixfix) with one-line semantics + [arity·prio·det|llm] tags.
(2) Builtin operators documented — MET. SPEC.md documents special forms ($name, =, ;, grouping, backtick low-prec) and the builtin/operator boundary; docs/design/basics-primitives.md is the primitives primer.
(3) NLIR primer — MET. README.md (usage tour), SPEC.md (normative contract), docs/design/basics-primitives.md + composable-core.md + quote-eval.md, and docs/cookbook.md together form the primer set explaining the operator system and syntax.
(4) Precedence/arity/semantics — MET. SPEC.md has operator tables (fixity·arity·what-it-does) and an "Operator attributes (config)" section (op, arity, fixity, priority, assoc, ...); `nlir help` surfaces precedence + arity per op.

No artifact was found that still shows only the limited 5-op set (grepped site/, docs/, README, SPEC, src/). The site workspace (site/workspace/workspace.js) already carries the full name-keyed operator schema.

RECOMMENDATION: close as satisfied-by-later-work, OR reply with the SPECIFIC remaining gap if one is intended (e.g. a single consolidated machine-readable operator schema file, if that's the ask — none exists as a standalone artifact today; the schema lives in config.example.yaml + `nlir help`). Not claiming/closing from here since there are no commits referencing this bead to validate a close against; leaving for the operator/creator (harryaskham) to close or re-scope.

title: G1: graph → SVG shared renderer (core, pure) — the one renderer for kitty + wasm
description:
Graph-viz epic (bd-8ac9ad) slice G1 — the SHARED renderer (this is the piece Harry wants shared between CLI-kitty and the wasm webapp). Proposed lane: aur-2 lead (rendering/showcase aesthetic) + aur-1 aesthetic review. Depends G0 (bd-10cf87).

Turn the G0 dataflow Graph {nodes, edges} into a beautiful, transparent-background **SVG** — pure Rust, cross-platform (no native deps), so it lives in core and BOTH backends consume it identically:
- A layered/topological layout (dagre-lite: rank nodes by dataflow depth, order within a rank to reduce edge crossings, assign x/y). Operand edges + the resolved binding-reference edges (styled distinctly, e.g. dashed, so a variable feeding its uses reads clearly).
- Node styling in the nlir card aesthetic (dark-violet theme, Fira Code, rounded node boxes; op nodes vs literal nodes vs binding nodes visually distinct). Text via G0's truncate_ends so long literals/Values stay compact.
- Output = an SVG string + its intrinsic size. SVG chosen because it is pure to emit AND both a native rasterizer (G4: resvg→PNG→kitty) and the browser (G5: inject into the P2 DOM) render it → ONE renderer, byte-identical graphs in kitty and in the wasm UI.
- Deterministic layout (stable across runs) + a STABLE-position mode keyed on G0 node ids, so G2 step frames don't jump.

Confer on ownership/aesthetic with aur-1 (owns the site/card look). Unit test: a known small expr → SVG containing the expected node/edge count + no layout panics.

The schoolboy-Gauss story: sum 1+2+...+100 by pairing the ends into 50 pairs of 101 = the triangular-number shortcut n(n+1)/2 = 5050. Parenthesised addition inside a multiply-then-divide; coercion reads the number-words. Verified -> 5050. (Sibling to last tick's handshakes n(n-1)/2=45; this is n(n+1)/2.)

title: nlir power-move: move-aur2-postmortem.sh (@&[own,cause,prevention] = postmortem note) + card + POWERMOVES row

The hardest message to write well: take responsibility cleanly, state the root cause without excuses, show the concrete fix so it can't recur — a graceful accountability memo in one line.

Filled: @&['i own the incident, my change took down checkout for twenty minutes','the root cause was a missing null check on the new coupon path','ive added a test for that path and a canary deploy step so it cannot recur'] -> "I take full responsibility for this incident. My change caused a twenty-minute outage of the checkout system, and the root cause was identified as a missing null check on the new coupon path. I have since added a test covering that path, as well as a canary deployment step, to prevent this issue from recurring."

ALSO: carded it (showcase/nlir-postmortem.png, single --only regen, honest llm pill; verify-showcase.py still green) — my 3rd showcase card; + added the postmortem row to POWERMOVES.md quick-ref. examples/move-aur2-postmortem.sh + scripts/build-showcase.py + examples/POWERMOVES.md.

title: Cyclic/self-referential form application stack-overflows + aborts the process instead of erroring
description:
Found via pyramid gap-finding play (robustness-probing the composable persistent
library, bd-9cfccb). A self-referential or cyclic FORM APPLICATION recurses until
the NATIVE stack overflows and ABORTS the process — a hard crash (exit 134
"stack overflow, aborting"), not a catchable `EvalError`.

Repro (det mode):
- self-ref:  `loop={('x'++($loop%$0))}; $loop%'y'`  -> stack overflow, abort.
- cycle:     `a={($b%$0)}; b={($a%$0)}; $a%'z'`      -> stack overflow, abort.
(A missing-part reference already errored gracefully — "unknown context key" —
so this is specifically the UNBOUNDED-RECURSION case.)

This matters now that persisted parts can reference other parts (bd-9cfccb): an
accidental cycle in a parts library crashes nlir instead of erroring.

FIX: form application (`eval_form_apply` for `%`, `apply_form_op` for det:/glyph
form ops) pushes a positional arg-frame per application; a self-ref/cycle grows
`arg_frames` without bound. Added `enter_arg_frame()` guarding
`arg_frames.len() < MAX_ARG_FRAME_DEPTH` (100) — a graceful
`EvalError::Unsupported("form application nested too deep (>100) — a
self-referential or cyclic form?")` instead of the crash. 100 leaves ample
headroom for legit nesting (composable-part pyramids, nested map/fold) while
firing well before the native stack overflows.

Verified: self-ref + cycle now exit 1 with the graceful error; legit cases
unchanged (`{$0*$0}%7`->49, nested map/fold->14, 3-level part compose->"t [hi]").
Regression test added.
creator: ms-mac:nlir:ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-3

title: Eval: wire Mode::Llm realisation to llm::realise_llm
description:
Wire eval::Evaluator::realise()'s Mode::Llm arm (src/eval.rs) to call aur-2's landed llm::realise_llm helper (bd-dc3c72), replacing the current EvalError::Unsupported placeholder. This is the eval side of the eval↔llm seam; the llm.rs helper (resolve_model + substitute_operands + fragments + NLIR vars + backend dispatch + extract_result) is done.

Plan: in the Mode::Llm arm, if op has a prompt, render the (grouped-parens-preserved) operands to Vec<String>, call llm::realise_llm(op_cfg.model.as_deref(), prompt, &args, self.config, None /*cli_model*/, |n| std::env::var(n).ok()), wrap Ok(String) in Value::string, map the llm::RealiseError into a new EvalError::Llm(String). An llm op with no prompt stays Unsupported. Test via a command-type model (deterministic, no network); leave anthropic HTTP untested. Owned by aur-1 (eval lane), composes aur-2's bd-dc3c72.

title: [EPIC] nlir-wasm — in-browser evaluator + interactive workspace widget
description:
Harry directive (2026-07-05): a 1p, native-feeling in-browser nlir that IS the real evaluator (compiled to WASM from the same src/, co-built so it never drifts), with a live REPL/workspace + hero-widget on the site.

Vision: an input box + output area where users enter expressions and step through them; a config editor seeded from config.example.yaml; browser management of context / messages / KV values; everything persistent in localStorage. In-browser LLM auth via base-URL/key textboxes OR on-device tiny-LLMs (Chrome window.ai / WebLLM Gemma). A warm busybox-style wasm-sh for command: operators. A hero-widget at the top of the site with a runnable keystone example. Beautiful, in the card aesthetic, feels native, stays in sync with the actual evaluator via co-building.

Full architecture + phasing in docs/design/nlir-wasm.md (committed 70f0f57). Central design = the REALISER SEAM: abstract effectful realisation (llm backend + command subprocess) behind an injectable async trait — native = existing HTTP/bash, WASM = JS callbacks (fetch / on-device / wasm-sh) — so the eval core (lexer/parser/realise/precedence/step) stays pure + shared. Async eval path (no wasm threads → serial/async DAG).

Milestones (one bead each): P0 realiser seam · P1 nlir-wasm crate (wasm-bindgen, det-mode) · P2 workspace widget · P3 LLM realiser (BYO key) · P4 hero-widget · P5 on-device LLM · P6 command-VM · P7 CI co-build + version stamp. P0→P2 already yields a key-free det-mode playground + hero.

Proposed lane divvy: P0/P1 eval+wasm-build (msm-0/eval) · P2/P4 site+UI (aur-1) · P3/P5 js/llm · P6 wasm/commands · P7 CI (aur-2). Owner-coordinated on the design-doc thread.

title: nlir composer move: the pre-mortem (@&["PLAN", !"ROSY_CLAIM", "HEDGE"]) — #39
description:
nlir power-moves tick (aur-2) — fresh COMPOSER move #39 (migration-proof, "..."-slots, no '...' — while holding on the quote-eval sigil ruling). "the pre-mortem": a plan + its most likely failure + the hedge, woven into one clear-eyed heads-up. Forward-looking risk planning (distinct from my retro/post-mortem = backward, and risk-flag = one risk).

SHAPE: `@&["THE_PLAN", !"THE_ROSY_CLAIM", "THE_HEDGE"]`. The `!` slot is the trick: write the optimistic claim you DON'T fully believe and `!` flips it into the honest risk. So a pre-mortem writes itself: plan, the thing that could go wrong, the hedge — @&[...] braids all three into one calm statement.

Real (claude-sonnet-5): @&["we ship the new billing system Monday", !"the data migration will be seamless", "we keep the old system on standby for 48h"] -> "The new billing system will be deployed on Monday. Please be advised that the data migration process is not expected to proceed seamlessly. Accordingly, the legacy system will remain on standby for 48 hours as a precautionary measure."

Files (mine only): examples/move-aur2-premortem.sh (SHAPE+slots+filled+real output; runs live, exit 0) + examples/POWERMOVES.md (quick-ref row). No new build-showcase.py card — the @&[...] composer pattern is already well-carded (curation discipline: fresh MOVE, not a fresh CARD ANGLE).

title: First CI-exact-gated llm showcase card via `_seed` (the assertable-card payoff of bd-72d6d3)
description:
Follow-up to bd-72d6d3 (`_seed` LANDED @a15ca41). Now that a seed threads into
the llm request, we can promote the FIRST llm showcase card from "checked
non-empty" to CI-EXACT-gated — the assertable-llm-card payoff the `_seed` bead
promised, and the enabler for byte-stable nlir-golf regression cards.

## Exactly how the gate works (verified by reading scripts/verify-showcase.py)
- A card's `pill` string is the classifier: `mode = "det" if pill.startswith("det")
  else "llm"`; `exact = "exact" in pill`.
- `exact` cards re-run the expr and assert output matches `out` EXACTLY; non-exact
  llm cards only assert non-empty. LLM cards run only when `LITELLM_MASTER_KEY` is
  set (keyless CI → `--det-only`, llm cards skipped).
- Gate runs against `config.example.yaml` with `--mode llm`, resolving the DEFAULT
  model. So an `llm · exact` card is only stable if the resolved backend honours
  `seed` AND the card pins `_seed=N`.

## What's needed (coordinated; 3 owners)
1. aur-0 (BLOCKER): backend honor-matrix — which of our backends returns
   byte-identical output for the same `_seed`+expr twice. Anthropic Messages is a
   confirmed no-op (no native seed); the :4000 OpenAI-compat litellm proxy is the
   likely honourer. Result names the backend to route the card's model through.
2. This card's model routing: add/point a `config.example.yaml` model at the
   seed-honouring backend (or a card-scoped `model:`), so the exact card resolves
   to it while default fuzzy ops stay on claude.
3. msm-2 (card/site wiring lane): add the card to build-showcase.py SIMPLE with an
   `llm · exact` pill + the pinned `_seed=N` expr + captured `out`; surface it in
   the "why nlir, not a prompt" gallery. verify-showcase will then exact-gate it
   whenever a key is present.

## Candidate card (stable semantic target, seed-pinned)
A golf-style semantic one-liner with `_seed=N` whose output aur-0 confirms is
reproducible on the chosen backend — e.g. a seeded `..`/`~>`/`=>` expr. Pick the
target from a refereed golf winner so it doubles as the golf-regression card.

## Value
Closes the loop Harry asked for: golf mints a shape → seed makes it reproducible →
it becomes a CI-gated "why nlir" card + a regression test. First assertable llm
card; template for the rest.

OWNER: coordinate aur-0 (backend) → msm-3 (model routing / eval, filed this) →
msm-2 (card+site wiring). BLOCKED until aur-0's honor-matrix names the backend.

---
BLOCKED (aur-0 honor-matrix, run post-`_seed`-land on helsinki + :4000 proxy): no reachable backend honors `seed` for reproducible output → CI-EXACT-GATING llm cards is NOT achievable on the current stack.
- Default nlir path (claude command): claude CLI has no `--seed`; env ignored → not reproducible ("Dear Team," vs "Dear team,").
- :4000 proxy → claude-sonnet-5 (Anthropic): no native seed param → not reproducible.
- :4000 proxy → gpt-5-mini (only reachable OpenAI-compat model): seed=42 ×3 → 3 different outputs → does not honor seed deterministically.
- gpt-4o/4o-mini: unavailable on the proxy.
So this bead is blocked on ADDING a seed-honoring backend (a genuinely-honored gpt-4o, or a fixed-seed local model). `_seed` machinery is correct + ready (bd-72d6d3, landed) — this is purely a backend-availability gap. Until then, keep gating llm cards on DET-STUBS + live-caption (the current, correct pattern). Recommend: keep OPEN but labelled blocked, or convert to "source a seed-honoring backend" scope.
creator: ms-mac:nlir:ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-3
spoken_name: ci exact gated llm showcase card

title: config.example.yaml command models: YAML folded-scalar keeps newlines, breaking bash command backends
description:
Found while dogfooding nlir in LLM mode (Harry's directive, 2026-07-03). The shipped config.example.yaml `command:` model fields use a YAML `>` folded scalar with MORE-INDENTED continuation lines, e.g.:

  command: >
    claude --model claude-sonnet-5
      --system-prompt "..."
      --print "..."

Per the YAML folded-scalar spec, lines indented more than the block's first content line are kept as LITERAL newlines (the "more-indented" exception) rather than folded to spaces. So the resulting command string has real newlines, and `bash -c` runs each line as a SEPARATE statement: `claude --model claude-sonnet-5` (no prompt), then `--print "..."` -> "bash: --print: command not found" (exit 127). Any command-model realisation from the shipped example is broken.

Repro: nlir --mode llm -e "#'the quick brown fox'" with a claude command model authored via `>` folded + indented continuations -> "command backend exited with status 127: ... --print: command not found".

FIX: author command: fields as SINGLE-LINE commands (or a `|` literal block that is valid multi-line bash). Note the echo operator's `command: |` literal block is CORRECT (intentional multi-line bash); the bug is specifically the `>` folded style with unintended-newline continuations. Also update the models to Harry's requested real backends: claude (claude-sonnet-5 via the claude binary) + copilot (github-copilot/claude-sonnet-4.6 via pi; copilot has no claude-sonnet-5 on the Responses API). Keep example_config_parses_and_validates green.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-2

title: nlir-wasm P1: nlir-wasm crate — wasm-bindgen exports (evaluate/step/parse/operators/version), det-mode
description:
Epic: bd-360d0c. Design: docs/design/nlir-wasm.md section 3.3. Depends on P0 (bd-bec201, realiser seam).

Create the nlir-wasm crate (crates/nlir-wasm or a wasm feature) depending on the nlir lib. wasm-bindgen exports: evaluate(expr, config_yaml, context_json, mode) -> Promise<Result>, step(expr, ...) -> Promise<Step[]> (mirrors nlir step), parse(expr) -> Ast, operators(config_yaml) -> Op[] (the nlir help data for a live grammar panel), version() -> {crate, git} (co-build stamp). Accepts JS realiser callbacks (LLM + command) wired through the P0 Realiser trait. Build with wasm-pack build --target web. Verify serde_yaml compiles to wasm32 (open question — else a JS YAML->JSON bridge). DET-MODE must work end-to-end in wasm (numeric/template/join + parse/precedence/step) with NO realiser needed. Acceptance: wasm-pack build succeeds; a browser smoke page runs det evaluate + step. Lane: wasm/build.

RESOLVED (aur-0 toolchain check, design f09ea5f): (1) Config crosses the wasm boundary as JSON — evaluate/operators take config_json; JS does YAML→JSON (js-yaml). Crate does NOT depend on serde_yaml (leaner, avoids libyaml-on-wasm). (2) Build home: CI (rustup+wasm32+wasm-pack, same commit — P7) is canonical; local via nix devshell (rust-overlay/fenix + wasm-pack + wasm-bindgen-cli — the flake's nixpkgs rustc lacks the target). Source-rustc nodes (aurora) are AUTHORING-ONLY. (3) Split: aur-0 authors the crate source + native-verifies the shared eval logic; the wasm-pack build + browser smoke runs in CI or the devshell (NOT on the author's node). So P1 acceptance = build+smoke via CI/devshell.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-1

title: Dedup negative-index-from-end resolver (stack + messages resolve_index)
description: `Stack::resolve_index` (src/stack.rs) and `messages::resolve_index` (src/messages.rs) are the identical negative-index-from-end resolver: `index >= 0` from the start, `index < 0` from the end (`-1` = last), `None` out of range. Both back SPEC index semantics (`$N` stack index with `$0` bottom / `$-1` top; `^N` message index). A shared helper (e.g. `fn resolve_index(len, i64) -> Option<usize>` in a small `index`/util module, or one made pub and reused) would dedup ~6 lines and keep the semantics in one place. Low priority; the two are currently in-sync. Filed via reflect-session mixin from bd-d4631b/bd-22fa7e session.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-1

title: Add :resume builtin to list and restore previous REPL sessions

Acceptance criteria: :resume displays list of sessions with timestamps, allows selection via keyboard/interactive menu, loads selected session's context and messages.

title: nlir power-move #21: the myth-buster @&[!'MISCONCEPTION','REALITY'] — correct the record
description:
nlir power-move #21 (aur-2) — "the myth-buster" (correct the record): reject a common misconception AND state what's actually true, woven into one authoritative correction. Builds purposefully on the ! operator in a standalone way nobody had.

SHAPE: @&[!'MISCONCEPTION', 'THE_REALITY'] — the ! slot flips the false claim, the next slot gives the reality, & weaves them "not X; rather Y". Reads NO chat (busts a free-standing myth) — distinct from aur-1's reasoned-no @(!^-1 & grounds) which rejects THEIR last message. Use : instead of @ to correct gently.

WHY ! MATTERS (verified A/B): without it, @&['microservices always scale better',...] ASSERTS the myth; with it, @&[!'microservices always scale better',...] -> "Microservices do NOT invariably scale better..." The ! is the difference between repeating a myth and busting it.

Filled + REAL (claude-sonnet-5): @&[!'microservices always scale better than monoliths','they trade operational complexity for independent scaling, worth it only past a certain team size'] -> "Microservices do not invariably scale better than monolithic architectures; rather, they exchange increased operational complexity for the benefit of independent scaling, a trade-off that is justified only beyond a certain organizational size."

Deliverables: examples/move-aur2-mythbuster.sh (runs nlir live, --examples gate exit 0), POWERMOVES.md quick-ref row, showcase card nlir-myth-buster.png (my 11th card; short more-tests example; single --only regen, honest llm pill) + scripts/build-showcase.py row.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-2
