title: Config-assignable glyph operators backed by forms/builtins (glyphs for map/fold + any form) — alt to command/prompt
description:
Harry (2026-07-05): "do we have glyphs for map and fold? would be useful to be able to assign glyphs to builtins in the config, or to forms, as an alt to command or prompt mode."

CURRENT STATE: map/fold have NO glyphs — they're $map/$fold (builtin form names called via %, glyph-free by 4-way team consensus because single-char ASCII operator glyphs are exhausted; only `\` is free, earmarked for macro-splice).

THE ASK: a config mechanism to bind a GLYPH (operator sigil) to a builtin (map/fold/…) or to a FORM, as a new operator realisation kind alongside command/prompt/template/reduce.

FEASIBILITY (nearly free — two enablers already exist):
1. Unicode operator glyphs ALREADY tokenize. `Δ` (diff) is a configured multi-byte Unicode sigil, and the lexer matches configured operator sigils longest-first (src/lexer.rs). So a user can assign `↦`, `⊕`, `⇈`, or ANY glyph in config today with zero ASCII-scarcity cost — this sidesteps the whole free-sigil-census problem (that census only counted single-char ASCII).
2. The only missing piece is a new operator realisation kind in OperatorConfig (src/config.rs — today: template / command / reduce / join / prompt). Add:
   - `form: "{$0*2}"` — operator realises by applying the given form to its operands ($0, $1, …). Any form becomes a glyph-operator.
   - `builtin: map` / `builtin: fold` — bind a glyph to the map/fold engine builtins.
   Then e.g. `⇈: { op: "⇈", arity: 1, form: "{$0*2}" }` gives `⇈5` → 10, and a glyph could alias $map.

WHY IT'S NICE: closes the loop — forms are code-as-data (quote-eval); this makes them first-class, user-DEFINED operators bound to any glyph, as an alt to shelling out (command) or prompting (llm). Glyph-scarcity-free because the user picks the glyph (incl. Unicode) in their own config.

Lanes: eval-core (apply a form/builtin as an operator realisation) = msm-0; operator config schema (`form:`/`builtin:` fields + validation) = aur-2 / relates to bd-2e59bb (operators schema); sigils = aur-1 (though config-assigned sidesteps team glyph allocation). Tests/docs/cookbook = me. OPEN Q for Harry: also ship DEFAULT Unicode glyphs for map/fold (e.g. a map/fold sigil), or leave glyph choice entirely to user config?

---

## Merged duplicate: bd-f9bc37

Title: Operator realisation kinds: `form:` and `builtin:` — bind glyphs to forms + builtins (map/fold) as an alt to command/prompt

Operator request (Harry, 2026-07-05, via sgu24-app): "do we have glyphs for map and fold? would be useful to be able to assign glyphs to builtins in the config, or to forms, as an alt to command or prompt mode."

Today map/fold are $map/$fold (named builtins via %), no glyph (deliberately glyph-free to conserve sigils). This bead: add TWO new operator REALISATION KINDS to the config, alongside reduce/template/join/command/prompt, so any glyph can be bound to a form or a builtin:

1. `form:` — an operator realised by applying a FORM to its operands.
   e.g. `steelman: { op: "⇑", arity: 1, fixity: prefix, form: "{~(>@$0)}" }` → `⇑x` = summarise(expand(formalise(x))).
   Desugars an application to `{form}%(operands)` — binds operand values to $0..$N-1 in the form and evaluates. Promotes the named-macro library into first-class glyph-operators (user-extensible vocabulary, no prompts/commands). The form source is parsed once at config-load (reuse parse_stored_form / the config-grammar reparse).

2. `builtin:` — bind a glyph to a builtin like map/fold.
   e.g. `mapop: { op: "↦", arity: 2, fixity: infix, builtin: map }`, `foldop: { op: "⊘", arity: 2, fixity: infix, builtin: fold }` → `{$0*$0}↦[1,2,3]` maps.
   Dispatches to the existing eval_higher_order (map/fold engine, bd-14af74). Operands eval operand-first (form operand → Value::Form, list → Value::List) → higher-order.

LANE: config schema (OperatorConfig: add `form`/`builtin` fields) + eval realisation dispatch (realise/realise_async: check builtin/form kinds) = msm-0 (eval). Adds glyphs (grammar) → coordinate sigil/schema with aur-1 (sigil) + aur-2 (config). Harry greenlit by request.

Design: additive. realise() gains two branches: builtin → eval_higher_order over the (pre-evaluated) operands; form → build FormApply{form: parsed, args: operands} and eval. Operand-first eval already gives the form/list values. Tests: `⇑`-style form-operator + `↦`/`⊘` map/fold-operator in a test config; nlir test cases. Open Q for Harry: ship example glyph-operators in config.example.yaml (↦/⊘/⇑) or leave glyph-choice entirely to user config.

---

## Merged duplicate: bd-6335dc

Title: Config operators backed by a form or builtin (3rd mode alongside command:/prompt:) — assign glyphs to forms/map/fold

Operator request (Harry, 2026-07-05): "do we have glyphs for map and fold? would be useful to be able to assign glyphs to builtins in the config, or to forms, as an alt to command or prompt mode."

## Current state
map/fold have NO dedicated glyphs — they're named builtins `$map`/`$fold`, applied via `%`: `$map%({$0*$0},[1,2,3])`, `$fold%({$0+$1},[1,2,3,4])`. The team chose glyph-free deliberately to preserve the scarce FIXED-GRAMMAR sigil space (after {} quote / % apply / . reserved, `\` is the only free single-char ASCII, earmarked for macro-splice).

## The ask: a third operator IMPLEMENTATION mode
User config operators ALREADY pick their own sigils (that's how ~, @, #, etc. are all defined — config `operators:` with an `op:` sigil). Today an operator's implementation is either:
- `command:` — deterministic shell subprocess
- `prompt:` — llm realisation
Add a THIRD kind: back an operator by a FORM or a BUILTIN. Sketch:
- form-backed:  `square: { op: "□", form: "{$0*$0}", ... }`  → `□5` = 25
- builtin-backed: `each: { op: "↦", builtin: map, ... }`    → `↦({~$0}, [items])`
So a user can give map/fold (or any reusable form) whatever glyph they like. Glyph scarcity does NOT bite here because it's the USER's operator, not the fixed grammar — the user owns their config sigil space.

## Why it's nice
- Turns any form into a first-class named operator with a chosen sigil — terser, reusable "verbs" for your own thought-programs (composes with the whole operator/precedence/fixity machinery).
- Exposes the higher-order builtins (map/fold, future scan/filter) under ergonomic glyphs without spending fixed-grammar sigils on them.
- Consistent with nlir's config-defined-operators philosophy: command/prompt/form/builtin as the operator backends.

## Design considerations
- Schema: extend OperatorConfig with a `form:`/`builtin:` alternative to `command:`/`prompt:` (mutually exclusive backend). Validation: a form-backed op's form is parsed once; `$0/$1` bind its operands.
- Eval dispatch: at application, a form-backed op resolves to applying its form to the operands (reuses the % application + $N arg-frame); a builtin-backed op resolves to the reserved map/fold engine builtin.
- Fixity/arity/priority: form/builtin operators still declare arity + fixity + priority like any operator.
- Determinism: a form-backed op is det iff its form is det (so it participates in det-mode + the live preview cleanly).

## Lane
Eval dispatch = msm-0 (eval-core), config schema + sigil = aur-2/aur-1. Filed to track per Harry's request. Relates to the forms/map-fold work (bd-14af74) and the config operator schema.
creator: ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-1

title: [lexer] Scientific-notation float literals do not lex: 1.5e3 / 1e-9 rejected
description:
Found by driving nlir (dogfooding), sibling of bd-f551f9 (bare float literals).

Scientific-notation number literals are inconsistently supported:

- `1e5` / `2e2` lex fine (all-alphanumeric bare token) and coerce in numeric context (`1e3 + 1` -> 1001, `2e2 * 2` -> 400).
- `1.5e3`, `6.022e23`, `1.0e0` -> `parse error at token 1: unexpected token Bare("e3") after statement`. The lexer float extension (bd-f551f9) reads `1.5` then stops at `e`, leaving `e3` as a stray bare token.
- `1e-9`, `2e-3` (negative exponent) -> the alphanumeric run stops at the `-` leaving `2e`, which then fails to coerce: `cannot coerce string "2e" to number`.

Root cause: purely a LEXER gap. `Value::coerce_deterministic` already uses `s.trim().parse::<f64>()`, which understands the full exponent form — so once the literal lexes as ONE bare token it coerces correctly with no parser/eval change (same pattern as bd-f551f9's `.` float support).

Fix: extend `lex_bare`'s numeric tail to absorb a scientific-notation exponent `[eE][+-]?<digits>` onto a numeric mantissa (`<digits>` or `<digits>.<digits>`). Two shapes reach the tail because the alnum run treats `e`/`E` as an identifier char but stops at the sign: `1.5e3` (sits AT the `e`) and `1e-9` (run already pulled `e` in, sits at the sign). Both mantissas are unambiguously numeric (`1.5e`/`1e` is not a valid identifier), so absorbing never steals a `-`/`+` subtraction operator. Spaced forms (`1e - 9`) and non-numeric mantissas (`abce-3`) are unaffected.

Acceptance: `1.5e3`, `6.022e23`, `1e-9`, `2.5e+2` lex as one bare number and evaluate numerically in det mode; identifiers ending in `e` and `<digits>-<digits>` subtraction unchanged.
creator: ms-mac:nlir:ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-0

title: Set-notation value builtins: $union / $inter / $diff / $elem (lists, dicts, strings)
description:
Harry (2026-07-07, fleet broadcast): "can we add set notation for contains, contained-in or elemof, union intersection, difference, working as expected on lists dicts strings etc".

Today nlir has `$contains%(haystack,needle)` (substring/containment) but no set algebra or element-of. Add the deterministic, total, engine-layer value-builtin family mirroring $if/$nth/$sort/$contains (src/eval.rs eval_value_builtin + eval_value_builtin_async + the two dispatch guards at ~734/~1359). These are SPEC §Builtins (fixed engine layer, NOT config operators), so they do NOT touch the SPEC-operator-table≡config CI guard (bd-be33ee, aur-1's lane).

Design (slice 1, keyed on rendered-value equality, order-preserving, deduped — total + deterministic like sort_values):
- `$elem%(x, coll)` -> Bool membership. List: exact element (rendered-eq). Dict: key membership. Text: substring (x appears in coll). Number/Bool: rendered-eq. The "contained-in / elemof" flip of $contains.
- `$union%(a,b,...)` -> order-preserving dedup union of set_items(each arg).
- `$inter%(a,b)` -> items of a (deduped, order preserved) also present in b.
- `$diff%(a,b)`  -> items of a (deduped, order preserved) not present in b.

set_items(v): List -> items; Dict -> keys as Text (set-ops on dicts operate on KEYS, "as expected"); scalar -> singleton (whole string is one element in slice 1).

Deliverables: eval.rs impl + Rust unit tests; canonical `tests:` entries in config.example.yaml (mode: det); SPEC.md §Builtins doc rows; a cookbook example. cargo test --all + clippy -D + fmt clean.

Follow-up (separate bead, coordinate with aur-1 SPEC-ops lane): sigil aliases ∪ ∩ ∈ ∖ mapping to these builtins (config operator table + SPEC operator rows + CI guard), and _sep-driven char/word-set semantics for strings in union/inter/diff.

CLI/det lane = msm-1 (Harry's fleet split). Serves the golf rally: these are stable, det, composable set primitives = ideal golf/regression material.
creator: ms-mac:nlir:ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-1

title: nlir power-move #26: the reality-check @&['LEAD',<calc>,'CLAUSE'?] — a question carrying a live computed figure
description:
nlir power-move #26 (aur-2) — "the reality-check": pose a pointed question that carries a LIVE computed figure. nlir does the arithmetic AND frames it as the question to answer — pressure-test a plan/capacity/budget with the ACTUAL number. A genuinely-new COMBINATION: computed brief (calc slot) + question set (trailing ?), fused in one composer weave.

SHAPE: @&['LEAD_IN', <a live calc>, 'CLOSING_CLAUSE'?] — the calc slot is EVALUATED and woven in (comma/currency formatted), and the trailing ? on the final slot turns the whole woven statement into a question.

Filled + REAL (claude-sonnet-5): @&['does','12'*'2500','dollars a month fit our infra budget'?] -> "Does an expenditure of $30,000 per month align with our infrastructure budget?" (computed 12*2500=30000 -> $30,000, posed as a question). Also verified capacity: @&['is','1500'*'180','requests ... too much for one server'?] -> "Did the 270,000 requests ... exceed the processing capacity of one server?"

Deliverables: examples/move-aur2-realitycheck.sh (runs nlir live, --examples gate exit 0), POWERMOVES.md quick-ref row, showcase card nlir-reality-check.png (my 19th card; budget example; single --only regen, honest llm pill) + scripts/build-showcase.py row. Confirms ? works as a postfix slot inside @&[...] (poses the weave as a question) alongside an evaluated calc slot.

title: SPEC/help: caveat that `#` list-fold needs a coherent category (else per-item)
description:
Golf language-pinpoint (msm-0 found, aur-0 live-validated on helsinki): SPEC L267 + config.example.yaml L146 say `#` "Over a list, folds to the common category" — but that only holds when the items SHARE a coherent category. Live evidence: `#['robin','sparrow','eagle']`→"Birds" ✓, `#['red','green','blue']`→"RGB colors" ✓ (folds), but `#['sky','sea','sad']`→"sky, sea, sad" per-item (no coherent category: sky/sea=blue, sad=emotion). So the fold is correct but conditional — the docs overstate it as unconditional.

FIX (aur-1, SPEC/grammar lane): add the "needs a coherent category" caveat to `#`'s description in BOTH SPEC.md L267 and config.example.yaml L146 (kept in sync; `nlir help` derives from config). Not a behavior bug — behavior is correct, docs just need the condition. verify-spec-ops stays green (it compares sigil+name, not description prose).

This is the golf→docs loop completing: a real finding from playing, validated live, turned into a doc correctness fix.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-1

title: Document dictionaries + `.`/`..` accessors in SPEC.md (bd-27739b follow-up)
description:
Follow-up to bd-27739b (dicts + polymorphic `.` + semantic `..`, landed @1b3f614). The feature is in config.example.yaml + parser/eval + unit tests, but SPEC.md — the normative contract — has NO dict/accessor section. Grammar-gate verification (aur-1, on-main @1b3f614) confirmed the exact behavior; this documents it.

To add to SPEC.md (the "Config operators / language proper" + types areas):
- **Dict literal `{k=v, k2=v2}`** — the conjunction rule: a `{…}` is a Dict iff its body is a comma-list (NO top-level `;`) of ≥1 items, ALL `key=value` Assigns; a single non-Assign expr is a Form; empty `{}` is an empty Dict. Values eager-eval at construction EXCEPT an explicit form-literal value stays quoted (`{k={$0*2}}`→{k:Form}). Keys bare-name strings (v1). IMPORTANT — document the VERIFIED behavior: a `;` inside `{…}` is a CLEAR ERROR ("a `{…}` is either a dict of comma-separated key=value bindings or a single-expression form"), NOT a Form. (This supersedes the earlier "any `;`→Form" design wording; the impl chose clear-rejection, which is safe — zero existing multi-statement `{…;…}` forms — and cleaner.)
- **`.` accessor** (polymorphic structural access, 0-based, negative-from-end): `[a,b,c].1`→b, `[a,b,c].-1`→c, `{k=v}.k`→v, `"the".2`→e. Out-of-range / missing-key = loud error, never silent. Distinct from `$nth`.
- **`..` accessor** (the LLM semantic twin): `'the planets from the sun'..3`→"Earth"; generalizes past numbers ("the last", "the largest"). det stub `item N of: %`. The `.`/`..` det↔llm duality is the same shape as `~>`, `@`↔`=>` — worth naming as the pattern.
- **Value type**: Dict = insertion-ordered, String keys; Dict→string = rendered pairs (respect `_sep`); Dict→number/bool = hard error.
- **Known v1 limitation**: chained NUMERIC access `list.1.0` lexes `1.0` as a float (numeric-literal lexing runs before operator matching, which correctly protects `3.14`) → clear error "`.` index must be an integer, got `1.0`"; workaround `(list.1).0`. Dict/string chaining unaffected.

Also update the Atoms/types line (SPEC ~line 33) to mention dict literals + the `.`/`..` accessors. Regenerate any derived docs if needed. Grammar/SPEC-owner lane (aur-1). Discovered-via grammar-gate of bd-27739b.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-1

title: Sigil aliases for set builtins: ∪ ∩ ∖ ∈ -> $union/$inter/$diff/$elem (coord w/ aur-1 SPEC-ops)
description:
Follow-up to bd-49d65a (set-notation value builtins $union/$inter/$diff/$elem landed @29332a8). Harry asked for "set notation" — the math sigils are the natural terse surface, especially for golf.

Add config operators as sigil aliases that dispatch to the engine set builtins:
- ∪ (union) -> $union
- ∩ (inter) -> $inter
- ∖ or \ (diff) -> $diff
- ∈ (elem/in) -> $elem

This is the config-operator layer (config.example.yaml `operators:` block) + the SPEC operator tables + the verify-spec-ops.py CI guard (bd-be33ee), which is aur-1's SPEC-ops lane. So this MUST be coordinated with aur-1 (owner of the operator-table≡config invariant): each new sigil needs a config `operators:` entry AND a matching SPEC operator-table row (`| op | name | fixity · arity | ... |`) or the CI guard fails.

Open design questions for aur-1:
- fixity/priority for ∪ ∩ ∖ (infix, binary) and ∈ (infix, binary, Bool result) — slot near & | (9) or the comparison tier (5)?
- how a config operator delegates to an engine value-builtin: is there a `prefix_builtin`/`builtin:` style hook, or do we add a `value_builtin:` field? (`?` already does operand-start `if` via prefix_builtin — same pattern may fit.)
- ASCII fallbacks for keyboardability (e.g. `\` for diff; `in` reads as a word).

msm-1 is happy to implement the eval-delegation wiring; aur-1 to own/review the SPEC-ops guard + operator-table rows. Offer-first per coordination etiquette — aur-1, say the word if you'd rather take it end-to-end.
creator: ms-mac:nlir:ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-1

title: Add deterministic error handling for invalid redirection in LLM mode
description:
When running `nlir tui --mode llm` with invalid output redirection syntax (e.g., `>abc`), the system should provide a clear, deterministic error message rather than failing silently or with ambiguous errors. This is specific to LLM mode where redirection semantics may differ from standard shell behavior. 

Acceptance criteria:
- Running `nlir tui --mode llm` followed by `>abc` produces a clear error message
- Error message explains that the redirection syntax is invalid or not supported in LLM mode
- Error handling is consistent and deterministic across repeated executions
- Error is distinguishable from other parsing/execution errors
creator: ms-mac:cacophony:harryaskham

title: nlir coercion: hex/binary/thousands/percent/fraction coerce OFFLINE in det mode (no LLM round-trip)
description:
nlir coercion enhancement (aur-2 types/coercion lane; aur-0's dogfooding find). In --mode det, hex/binary/thousands/percent/fraction FELL THROUGH to the LLM despite being trivially deterministic — an unnecessary model round-trip for forms the CATALOG already claims as coercions.

Fix (src/value.rs): extracted parse_number_str(), tried in coerce_deterministic's Number branch BEFORE the model fallback: (1) plain float (unchanged: 42/3.5/-5/1e3/+3) → (2) hex 0x / binary 0b integer literals (optional sign) → (3) comma-grouped thousands 1,000 → (4) percent 50%->0.5 → (5) simple fraction 1/2->0.5 (guards divide-by-zero). Returns None for richer text (spelled numbers, ranges) so it still defers to the LLM/loud-error layer. Backward-compatible (only reached when plain parse fails).

Verified OFFLINE in det mode (no key): '0xFF'+'1'=256, '1,000'+'1'=1001, '50%'+'50%'=1, '1/2'+'1/2'=1, '0b101'+'1'=6, '3/4'*'4'=3. Full CI gate GREEN: cargo fmt --check + clippy --all-targets -D warnings + test --all (new test coerce_string_to_number_handles_offline_special_forms passes) + config::tests::example_config + nlir test 17/17. Now these forms are offline, zero LLM calls, faster + variance-free. Credit aur-0 for the find.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-2

title: Parser: colon in a string literal inside a NAMED (assigned) form breaks the binding
description:
Found by aur-0 while building Harry's L3 "reusable parts" pyramid (live, det-reproducible).

A form assigned to a name mis-parses to a NON-form when its body contains a `:` inside a string literal — `$name` then fails application with "cannot apply a non-form value; the left of `%` must be a {…} form". The SAME form applied directly works, and it is NOT operator-specific.

REPRO (det mode, config.example.yaml):
- `act={=>('next: '++$0)};$act%'x'` → FAILS
- `act={=>('next '++$0)};$act%'x'` → OK "response: (nextx)" (colon removed)
- `{=>('next: '++$0)}%'x'` → OK "response: (next: x)" (SAME form, applied DIRECTLY — so it's the assignment/binding path, not the form)
- `act={~('next: '++$0)};$act%'x'` → FAILS (so it's ANY op, not `=>`)
- `act={=>'a: b'};$act%'x'` → FAILS (no ++/$0 needed; a bare colon string suffices)

LIKELY CAUSE: the assignment-RHS / dict-vs-form disambiguation treats a stray `:` in the `{…}` body as dict-key syntax (dicts are `{k=v}`), so `name={… ':' …}` is classified as a (malformed) dict/non-form instead of a form. String-literal contents should not influence dict-vs-form classification; the lexer already tokenizes quotes, so the classifier is likely scanning structure without respecting string tokens, OR the assignment path differs from the operand path (which classifies correctly).

IMPACT: blocks naming reusable generative/transform parts whose instruction/text contains a colon — a common case for `=>` instructions ("next action: ", "summary: ", etc.) — which is exactly the L3 reusable-parts pyramid Harry asked for. Workaround: avoid colons in strings inside NAMED forms, or apply the form directly.

Parser lane (msm-0/aur-1). aur-0 will live+det gate the fix.

---
ROOT-CAUSED by msm-3 (deeper + broader than the colon symptom): the real bug is `Expr::render()` (parser.rs:135) rendering a string literal WITHOUT its quotes — `Expr::Quoted { content, .. } => content.clone()`. Named forms persist to context as `{"__nlir_form__":"<render() source>"}`, so EVERY string literal in a stored form loses its quotes and no longer round-trips. Colons just re-parse especially badly; `!`, spaces, ANY literal breaks (`p={$0++'!'};$p` → `{($0 ++ (! ))}`). Inline `{…}%x` is fine — it's ONLY the context round-trip. Fix: render Quoted WITH quotes so forms round-trip. msm-3 has CLAIMED the fix (parser/eval render lane), landing shortly with tests. aur-0 (reporter) will live+det gate the fix — including the L4 self-verifying train `$j%($def%topic,intent)` that hit this same bug. This bead = the symptom report; msm-3's render() fix is the resolution.

---

## Merged duplicate: bd-6eadea

Title: Named forms/dicts lose string-literal quotes on context round-trip — render() drops quotes, breaking reusable parts

Root-caused while building Harry's "pyramid of thought" (reusable named parts).

BUG: `Expr::render()` (parser.rs) rendered a string literal WITHOUT its quotes
(`Expr::Quoted { content, .. } => content.clone()`). A form persists to context
as `{"__nlir_form__":"<render() source>"}` (eval.rs:2404 `inner.render()`), so
every string literal in a stored form lost its quotes → the source no longer
round-trips. On read-back the un-quoted text re-lexes wrong:
- `'!'` -> the `!` not-operator: `p={$0++'!'};$p%'hi'` -> "hinot ($0 ++ !)".
- prose with spaces -> bare words: `{('pitch for: '++$0)}` -> non-form error.
- `:` -> re-parse breaks (aur-0's earlier colon symptom — a subset).
Bare-safe single-token literals (`'false'`, `'x'`) round-tripped by luck (they
re-lex to themselves), which masked the bug (the named judge appeared to work).
Inline forms (`{...}%x`) were always fine — it's ONLY the context round-trip.
This blocked every reusable part carrying prose/prompt text (classify, route,
generation-with-instructions, judge-with-a-real-target) = the L3/L4 pyramid.

FIX: render `Expr::Quoted` WITH quotes, choosing the char by the `interpolate`
flag so a double-quoted interpolating string doesn't silently round-trip as raw
single-quoted and lose `$name` interpolation (msm-0's catch). Single quotes are
raw (lexer `'…'` takes no escapes, so content never contains a `'`); double
quotes escape `\` and `"`. Cascades to the `Expr::Dict` render arm (same
`v.render()` quote-drop) for free (msm-0).

VERIFIED: `{$0++'!'}%'hi'`->hi!; `{('pitch for: '++$0)}` round-trips; named
judge `{$if%($0~>$1,$1~>$0,'false')}` works; `{"hello $x"}` keeps interpolation.

Corroborated live by aur-0/aur-1/aur-2/msm-0/msm-1. Test-covered with form +
dict round-trip cases.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-0

title: nlir power-move #28: the computed constant NAME='<calc>'; @&["...$NAME..."] — compute once, reuse consistently

SHAPE: NAME='<a calc>'; @&["... $NAME ...", "... $NAME ..."] — bind the RESULT of an arithmetic expression, reuse $NAME wherever the figure appears (DOUBLE quotes interpolate). Verified a binding holds a computed value: DET x='5'*'12';"the total is $x" -> "the total is 60" (works OFFLINE too).

Distinct from computed brief #17 (one calc, one mention), reality-check #26 (a calc posed as a question), templated message #27 (binds a STRING). This binds a COMPUTED value + reuses it consistently.

Filled + REAL (claude-sonnet-5): budget='2500'*'12';@&["our annual infra budget is $budget dollars","at $budget we can just afford two more regions"] -> "Our annual infrastructure budget is $30,000, which is sufficient to accommodate two additional regions." (30000 computed once, formatted $30,000, reused; weave dedups).

Deliverables: examples/move-aur2-computedconst.sh (runs nlir live + computes + interpolates, --examples gate exit 0), POWERMOVES.md quick-ref row, showcase card nlir-computed-constant.png (my 22nd card; single --only regen, honest llm pill) + scripts/build-showcase.py row.

title: nlir dual-game: golf-aur2-rubik.sh (3³=27) + target-aur2-101-fugue.sh (:simplify music→plain)
description:
nlir dual-game tick (aur-2). Two examples:

FORWARD golf-aur2-rubik.sh — "the Rubik's cube": 'three'**'three' = 3^3 = 27. A Rubik's cube is 3 cubies wide × 3 tall × 3 deep = 3 cubed = 27 (26 visible + 1 hidden core). Coercion reads the words, the power operator does the rest. Verified -> 27.

TARGET target-aur2-101-fugue.sh — reverse game via :simplify MUSIC jargon (a fugue) -> plain (aur-2 : lane), 11% shorter. TARGET (~191c) plain fugue. EXPR (170c) :'a fugue is a musical composition in which a short melody or theme is introduced by one voice and then repeated and interwoven by other voices in overlapping succession' -> "A fugue is a piece of music where one voice or instrument plays a short, catchy tune first. title: Division renders full f64 precision (10/3 -> 3.3333333333333335) — decide display precision
description:
Found dogfooding. Non-terminating division renders at full f64 precision, which is ugly for an English-output IR: `nlir --mode det -e '10/3'` -> "3.3333333333333335". Clean fractions are fine (1/2 -> 0.5, 7/2 -> 3.5, 100/8 -> 12.5); only repeating decimals surface the raw f64 shortest-round-trip string.

Location: Value::format_number in src/value.rs (aur-2 lane). This is a design decision, not a clear bug — options: (a) leave as-is (exact, deterministic, round-trippable); (b) round non-integral results to N significant figures for display (loses precision, changes value semantics if the rounded value feeds further ops); (c) only round at final stdout render, keeping full precision internally. SPEC doesn't specify float display precision and has no division examples. Filing as a draft for team/operator input before changing value semantics. Related: bd-f551f9 (bare float literals like 3.14 don't lex yet — msm-0 lexer lane); a display-precision decision should be consistent with literal float support.

SCOPE NOTE (2026-07-04, via aur-0): the full-f64-precision display question covers LARGE INTEGERS too, not just non-terminating division. nlir numbers are f64 formatted in shortest-round-trip form, so integers are bit-exact only up to 2^53; above that they're f64 approximations that SOMETIMES look clean (10^100, 10^20 print as clean 1+zeros) and SOMETIMES show visible noise (10**23 => 100000000000000010000000; 2**53+1 == 2**53; 2**63 => 9223372036854776000, true value ends ...5808). Any future "exact big-integer" or precision-display work should treat large-int display and non-terminating fractions as the same underlying f64-representation question. Surfaced while correcting the golf-aur2-googol.sh example header (which had a misleading "printed exactly, no rounding" claim, now fixed).
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-2

title: nlir power-move + card: the clarifying reframe ([:RESTATE, 'RIGHT'?]) — my 35th
description:
nlir power-moves tick (aur-2) — FRESH MOVE + CARD (my 35th). "the clarifying reframe": confirm you understood before you act.

SHAPE: [:RESTATE_THEIR_ASK_PLAINLY, 'IS_THAT_RIGHT'?]. A two-element top-level list (each element realized separately): the : restates their (often jargon-y/vague) ask in PLAIN terms, then the postfix ? turns your check into a real question. Structurally fresh — a list + question, not an @& composer. If you can say it plainly and they agree, you're aligned.

Real (claude-sonnet-5): [:'so what you need is a weekly summary email of the top three support issues, sent every friday morning to the leadership list','have I got that right'?] -> "Every Friday morning, send an email to the leaders that shows the three biggest problems customers had that week.\nDid I get that right?"

Files (mine only): examples/move-aur2-reframe.sh (SHAPE+slots+filled+real output; runs live, exit 0), examples/POWERMOVES.md (quick-ref table row), scripts/build-showcase.py card row + showcase/nlir-clarifying-reframe.png (single --only regen, honest llm pill). 35 moves + 35 cards.

title: nlir coding-pipe move: the fix-it (error | nlir -e '~(>"the fix for: $_stdin")') — #38
description:
nlir power-moves tick (aur-2) — FRESH coding-pipe move #38 on a fresh angle (the FIX, distinct from aur-0's error-triage). "the fix-it": pipe an error/stack-trace, get the likely fix.

SHAPE (as a PIPE): `<error> | nlir -e '~(>"the most likely fix for: $_stdin")'`. $_stdin = the piped error; "...: $_stdin" folds the error into a fix-instruction; >(...) expands it into the fix; ~(...) reins it to one crisp actionable paragraph. aur-0's error-triage (`~$_stdin`) tells you WHAT went wrong; this is the next step — HOW TO FIX it. Showcases ~(>X) (length-controlled expand) + $_stdin interpolation.

Real (claude-sonnet-5, a Python KeyError traceback piped in): -> "The `KeyError: 'session_id'` occurs because the code accesses `cache[session_id]` without checking if the key exists, so the fix is to use a safe lookup (e.g., `cache.get()`) while also verifying `session_id` is correctly populated upstream rather than accidentally holding the literal string \"session_id\"."

Files (mine only): examples/move-aur2-fixit.sh (SHAPE+pipe+filled+real output; pipes a sample traceback into nlir live; exit 0) + examples/POWERMOVES.md (quick-ref row). No build-showcase.py card — @aur-0 curates coding-pipe cards; this is a genuinely fresh ANGLE (a fix, not triage/distill/compose), so flagging it as a possible 5th-card candidate if the fix angle earns a slot (their call).

title: Project skeleton: Nix flake + Rust `nlir` CLI with crate deps wired
description:
Foundational bead. `SPEC.md` at the repo root is the normative contract for the whole project — read it first; all beads implement it.

Set up the initial project scaffolding so every downstream bead has somewhere to land:

- **Nix flake** managing a Rust CLI, following the same pattern as the other harryaskham Rust CLIs. Use the **`omni-cli`** project as the primary reference for the flake + CLI structure (mature examples also live under `~/.cacophony/daemon/checkouts/…`, e.g. `tendril`, `kittui`).
- **Rust binary crate `nlir`** with the command-tree scaffolding and stub subcommands from SPEC §CLI surface: `-e`, `parse`, `test`, `repl`, `set`, `get`, `append-message`, `mcp stdio`, `self-update`, `feedback`, plus global flags (`--config`, `--context-file`, `--session-file`, `--mode`, `--model`, `--parallelism`, `--quiet`, `--dry-run`).
- **Wire crate dependencies on the friend-project template stack** (these are configured Cacophony friend projects / sub-crates):
  - `mcp-cli` — gives the MCP server almost for free (`nlir mcp stdio`).
  - `updatable-cli` — `nlir self-update`.
  - `feedback-cli` — `nlir feedback`.
  Reference how `omni-cli` consumes these three crates and mirror that wiring.

Acceptance: `nix build`, `nix flake check`, and `cargo build` are green; `nlir --help` lists the command tree; the three template subcommands are wired (even if their handlers are thin) so later beads only fill in behaviour.

title: G4: kitty PNG animation + `--save-animation` (apng/gif) — native rasterize + display
description:
Graph-viz epic (bd-8ac9ad) slice G4. Lane: aur-2 (I own showcase PNG rendering + have the kitty image tooling). Native-only (behind the `native` feature). Depends G1 (bd-2f4129 SVG) + G2 (bd-c1710c frames) + G3 (bd-ca443a CLI flags).

The native kitty backend:
- SVG→PNG rasterization of G1's SVG (transparent background) via a pure-Rust rasterizer (resvg/usvg) — gated behind `native` so it never enters the wasm build.
- Display the PNG(s) via the kitty graphics protocol as an ANIMATION of the G2 step frames (advance frames on a timer / on keypress), a lightweight evolving view for `nlir show --animate` and `--graph`.
- `--save-animation <path>`: encode the frame PNGs to `.apng` or `.gif` (by extension) so the animation is shareable outside the terminal.
- Reuse the same G1 SVG the wasm UI (G5) uses → the kitty graphs and the web graphs are identical (Harry's ask). Transparent-bg so it sits on any terminal theme.

Depends the shared renderer (G1) + step frames (G2) + the CLI wiring (G3). Coordinate with G3 on where kitty-detection lives. Verify on aurora (kitty available) + a headless PNG/apng golden.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-2

title: Fork trains reject mixfix combiners (& | ) — the advertised (# & ~) fork doesn't parse
description:
Surfaced by the composable-core loop testing trains (landed @d903823). The 3-op FORK `(f g h)%x = (f x) g (h x)` requires the combiner `g` to be strictly `fixity: infix`. But `&` (and) and `|` (or) are `fixity: mixfix` (variadic, arity ">0"), so the canonical fork examples EVERYONE is using — `(# & ~)x` = "subject & gist", `(: & #)code` = "explain & name" (msm-0's + aur-1's + aur-0's advertised examples) — DO NOT parse; they error "unsupported train: use an all-prefix chain (atop) or a 3-op fork (f g h) with prefix f/h and infix g".

## Evidence (verified today, post-d903823, det mode)
- WORKS: atop `(~ @)%"thanks"` -> "summary: formal: thanks"; `(: ~ @)%"hi"` -> "simple: summary: formal: hi".
- WORKS: fork with INFIX combiner `(# Δ ~)%"hello"` -> "diff: subject: hello -> summary: hello".
- BROKEN: fork with MIXFIX combiner `(# & ~)%"hello world"` / `(: & #)%"fn add"` -> "unsupported train".

## The gap
`&`/`|` are the most natural fork combiners (weave two lenses with "and"/"or"), and they're what the cookbook/POWERMOVES fork cards advertise. The fork parser should accept a mixfix/variadic combiner as a binary combiner in the 3-op fork position (a mixfix op with exactly 2 operands is just binary), OR the advertised `(# & ~)` examples need to change to an infix combiner. First option is better — it makes the headline fork examples work.

## Lane
Parser = msm-0 (train desugar), grammar spec = aur-1 (fork detection classifies by fixity — the mixfix case). Spec filed by msm-2 from the loop; NOT claimed. Also flags a docs/example-sync issue: the fork cards showing `(# & ~)` are currently non-runnable until this lands.

title: Implement quote-eval system for form representation and application
description:
Design and implement a quote-eval system to represent and pass around forms/expressions in nlir.

Key requirements:
- Reserve double quotes for real strings
- Use single quote (') for quoting, similar to Lisp semantics (e.g., 'x or '(...) for explicit quoting)
- Implement form application operator (# as infix/mixfix if feasible, or design alternative if conflicts arise)
- Support equivalent forms: f#x#y, f#[x,y], and f#(x,y) should all be equivalent
- Implement comma (,) as tuple/list constructor: a,b,c == [a,b,c]
- Design resolution for any syntax ambiguities or overlaps

Acceptance criteria:
- Quote syntax correctly binds right-hand side or explicit forms
- Application works via # operator (or designed alternative)
- Example usage works: nlir -e '(&[$0,$1]);$f#(x,y)' produces expected binding behavior
- Tuple construction via comma operator functions correctly
- No parser ambiguities or conflicts with existing operators
- Design rationale documented if deviations from initial proposal were necessary

title: map/fold: auto-lift a sep-joined String to a List at the map boundary (per-line, inverse of the collection law)
description:
Follow-up to bd-14af74 (map/fold landed @cc6c498). Currently `$map`/`$fold`'s list argument coerces a non-list SCALAR to a 1-element list (intentional, ruled), but a sep-joined STRING is treated as ONE item, not split into its lines. This bead: auto-lift a String to a List at the map/fold boundary by splitting on `_sep`.

DESIGN IS PRE-DECIDED by the existing collection algebra (aur-0's insight): a list renders by JOINING elements with the sep; splitting a string on the SAME sep is the exact INVERSE (join then split = identity), so **per-LINE** is the principled default. per-char would break that symmetry. So the only real question is "do we auto-lift string→list at the map boundary" — the HOW (per-line) is already answered by the sep-join law aur-2 ruled + the map/fold dogfood validated.

PAYOFF: makes `$map%({~$0}, ^*)` = "summarise each message in the thread", `$map%({@$0}, ^_1^_5)` = "formalise each of the user's last 5 messages" — a killer grounding idiom over message-range reads.

LANES: aur-2 owns the String→List coercion (coercion lane); msm-0 semantic sign-off (eval); aur-0 design/dogfood (grounding idioms); Harry's call on whether to auto-lift. Non-breaking: today a string is 1 item; this makes a sep-joined string N items. Needs care that a genuine single-line string still maps as 1 item (only multi-line / explicit lists split).

Not urgent — map/fold ships robust without it. Captured so the design consensus isn't lost.
creator: ms-mac:nlir:ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-0

title: Config operators backed by a form or builtin (3rd mode alongside command:/prompt:) — assign glyphs to forms/map/fold
description:
Operator request (Harry, 2026-07-05): "do we have glyphs for map and fold? would be useful to be able to assign glyphs to builtins in the config, or to forms, as an alt to command or prompt mode."

## Current state
map/fold have NO dedicated glyphs — they're named builtins `$map`/`$fold`, applied via `%`: `$map%({$0*$0},[1,2,3])`, `$fold%({$0+$1},[1,2,3,4])`. The team chose glyph-free deliberately to preserve the scarce FIXED-GRAMMAR sigil space (after {} quote / % apply / . reserved, `\` is the only free single-char ASCII, earmarked for macro-splice).

## The ask: a third operator IMPLEMENTATION mode
User config operators ALREADY pick their own sigils (that's how ~, @, #, etc. are all defined — config `operators:` with an `op:` sigil). Today an operator's implementation is either:
- `command:` — deterministic shell subprocess
- `prompt:` — llm realisation
Add a THIRD kind: back an operator by a FORM or a BUILTIN. Sketch:
- form-backed:  `square: { op: "□", form: "{$0*$0}", ... }`  → `□5` = 25
- builtin-backed: `each: { op: "↦", builtin: map, ... }`    → `↦({~$0}, [items])`
So a user can give map/fold (or any reusable form) whatever glyph they like. Glyph scarcity does NOT bite here because it's the USER's operator, not the fixed grammar — the user owns their config sigil space.

## Why it's nice
- Turns any form into a first-class named operator with a chosen sigil — terser, reusable "verbs" for your own thought-programs (composes with the whole operator/precedence/fixity machinery).
- Exposes the higher-order builtins (map/fold, future scan/filter) under ergonomic glyphs without spending fixed-grammar sigils on them.
- Consistent with nlir's config-defined-operators philosophy: command/prompt/form/builtin as the operator backends.

## Design considerations
- Schema: extend OperatorConfig with a `form:`/`builtin:` alternative to `command:`/`prompt:` (mutually exclusive backend). Validation: a form-backed op's form is parsed once; `$0/$1` bind its operands.
- Eval dispatch: at application, a form-backed op resolves to applying its form to the operands (reuses the % application + $N arg-frame); a builtin-backed op resolves to the reserved map/fold engine builtin.
- Fixity/arity/priority: form/builtin operators still declare arity + fixity + priority like any operator.
- Determinism: a form-backed op is det iff its form is det (so it participates in det-mode + the live preview cleanly).

## Lane
Eval dispatch = msm-0 (eval-core), config schema + sigil = aur-2/aur-1. Filed to track per Harry's request. Relates to the forms/map-fold work (bd-14af74) and the config operator schema.

---

Duplicate of bd-44c294
creator: ms-mac:nlir:ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-2

title: Implement stack expansion operations for lists
description:
Add two stack operations for manipulating lists:

1. **Expand operation**: Takes a list at the top of the stack and expands it in-place, replacing the list with all of its individual elements on the stack.

2. **Collapse/Collect operation**: Takes the stack (or n elements if an argument is provided) and replaces it with a stack containing a single list (containing those elements).

Acceptance criteria:
- Expand operation correctly unpacks list elements onto the stack
- Collapse operation correctly gathers stack elements into a list
- Collapse operation respects optional n argument to collect only top n elements
- Both operations integrate properly with existing stack manipulation
creator: ms-mac:cacophony:harryaskham

title: bug: \$ escape defeated in double-quote interpolation ("\$k" interpolates instead of literal $k)
description:
DOGFOOD FINDING (msm0). In double-quoted strings, the `\$` escape does NOT produce a literal `$` when followed by a valid variable name — it interpolates instead.

REPRO (det mode, config.example.yaml):
  k=world;"\$k"   => "world"   (WRONG — want literal "$k")
  k=world;"\$5"   => "$5"      (works, but only coincidentally: $5 is digit-led so never interpolates anyway)

ROOT CAUSE (two-stage processing):
1. LEXER src/lexer.rs read_escape() (~line 497): the `other => other` arm collapses ANY unrecognised escape to the bare char, so `\$` -> `$`. The lexed content of "\$k" becomes `$k` with interpolate=true.
2. context.rs interpolate() (~line 411) then runs on `$k`, sees `$` + alphabetic, and interpolates it. It cannot tell an escaped `$` from a real one — the `\` was already eaten by the lexer.

So the escape is defeated by de-escaping BEFORE interpolation. Per lexer docs ("`"…"` with POSIX escapes processed; $name interpolation applied at eval time"), `\$` should be a literal `$` and must NOT interpolate.

PROPOSED FIX (spans lexer + interpolate; this is msm0's interpolation feature, bd-2a1cb6):
- LEXER (msm0 lane): in lex_escaped_quote, special-case `\$` to PRESERVE it as the two chars `\$` in the content (do not call read_escape's collapsing path for `$`), leaving the escape intact for eval-time interpolation. Keep `\n`/`\t`/`\"`/`\\` collapsing unchanged.
- INTERPOLATE (context.rs, shared): add a branch so `\$` emits a literal `$` and skips interpolation of the following name (like shells). i.e. when the scanner sees `\` followed by `$`, consume both and push `$`.

REGRESSION GUARDS: `k=world;"\$k"` == literal "$k"; `k=world;"$k"` == "world" (unchanged); `"a\nb"` still newline; `"\\"` still backslash. Also verify raw single-quote `'$k'` stays literal (already correct).

msm0 owns the interpolation feature and can implement both halves; coordinating the context.rs touch. Cosmetic-adjacent but a real correctness bug: you currently cannot put a literal `$var`-shaped string through double quotes.
creator: ms-mac:nlir:ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-0

title: nlir power-move #16: the BLUF [~&[FACTS],@&[SAME]] — skimmable headline first, then full detail
description:
nlir power-move #16 (aur-2) — "the BLUF" (bottom line up front): lead with a skimmable one-sentence headline, then the full detail. The canonical email/report pattern.

SHAPE: [~&[FACTS], @&[SAME_FACTS]] — a 2-element list. First ~&[...] distils the facts to a single bottom-line sentence (the 3-second skim); second @&[...] is the complete formal write-up. nlir prints headline then body.

SIBLING/AXIS: the length-axis complement to my dual-register brief ([@&[X],:&[X]], which splits by WHO reads). BLUF splits by HOW MUCH they read (skim vs full). Same list mechanism, different tone pair (~&+@&). Framed as a matched pair in POWERMOVES, not a dup.

Filled + REAL (claude-sonnet-5): [~&['the friday deploy is postponed to monday','a payments bug slipped through staging','fix is in review, needs a fresh soak test'], @&[SAME]] ->
[headline] "The Friday deploy is now Monday, and a payments bug found in staging has a fix under review pending a fresh soak test."
[full] "The Friday deployment has been postponed to Monday. Additionally, a payments-related bug was not caught during staging testing; a fix is currently under review and will require a fresh soak test prior to release."

Deliverables: examples/move-aur2-bluf.sh (runs nlir live, --examples gate exit 0) + POWERMOVES.md quick-ref row. No card this tick (avoid two near-identical 2-line card layouts with dual-register). Distinct from aur-1's tighten #11 ([<^-1,~^-1] shortens a READ message two ways); BLUF composes FRESH facts into headline+body (my COMPOSE lane).

title: bare word matching a bound var name is a silent literal (reusable-parts footgun): want $name reuse note or a 'did you mean $name?' lint
description:
Found building Harry's "pyramid of thought" (reusable-parts trains). A bare word that
COINCIDES with a bound variable name is silently read as a LITERAL, not the binding —
no error, no warning — so a "reusable part" train produces nonsense.

Repro (live): `p='the planets from the sun';p..3` → "5" (garbage), because bare `p` is
the literal string "p". The correct form is `$p..3` → "Earth". The `$`-sigil is
load-bearing exactly when reusing bound PARTS (the whole pyramid idiom), and the natural
instinct — writing `p` — degrades silently to a one-char literal with no signal.

Lane: value/eval/UX (aur-2). This is a UX/diagnostic gap, not a parse bug — `p` is a
legal literal, so it can't just error. Fix options (pick one, generic):
- Help/SPEC note: the bindings/reuse section should state explicitly "reuse a binding with
  `$name`; a bare `name` is a string literal" (cheap, doc-only).
- Lint/warning: at eval, when a bare bareword exactly matches a currently-bound name,
  emit a warning "bare `p` is a literal; did you mean `$p`?" (higher-value, catches it at
  the point of the mistake — the reusable-parts idiom makes this collision common).

Impact: silent-wrong-output is the worst failure mode; it cost real time building the
pyramid (the L1 train gave "5" not "Earth"). Surfaced via golf-as-gap-finder (Harry's
point that the exercise finds real gaps). Corroborating pinpoints in scratch note
`nlir-golf-pinpoints` + `nlir-parts-library`.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-2

title: Math precedence broken by default: default_priority ignores the SPEC ladder for arithmetic sigils
description:
Found while driving nlir in LLM mode. parser::default_priority only keys off FIXITY (prefix 14, postfix 1, infix/mixfix = DEFAULT_PRIORITY 9). So a config that declares the canonical arithmetic operators WITHOUT explicit `priority:` gets +, *, ** all at priority 9 → `1+2*3` evaluates to 9, not 7. The SPEC §Grammar precedence ladder (** 13, * / 12, + - 11, & | 9) is documented as the intended precedence but is NOT applied by default, and the SPEC §Example config `operators:` block omits explicit priorities on add/mul/sub/div/pow — so a config author copying the SPEC example gets mathematically-wrong precedence.

Verified: with explicit `priority: 11/12/13` on +-/*//** the parser respects them correctly (1+2*3=7, 2**3*2=16, 2+3*4-1=13). So the parser is fine; the gap is the DEFAULTS + the SPEC example.

Options: (a) update the SPEC §Example config to include the precedence ladder priorities on the arithmetic ops (minimal); (b) add a sigil-aware default-priority resolution for the canonical arithmetic sigils (**,*,/,+,-) so the SPEC ladder holds without explicit config (more magic, less config-driven); (c) document loudly that arithmetic operators MUST set priority. Filed by aur-1 from the nlir LLM-mode driving session.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-1

title: Unmemorised golf round: honest compression ratio on novel targets (recall vs derivation vs novel-information)
description:
Harry (sgu24-app, 2026-07-08): "that's too easy but it's not cheating to just say 'the first line of...'. we need examples the llm hasn't memorised."

The crux: memorised-target golf (`=>'first line of 1984'` 22B→73B ≈3.3×; `~>'what is oauth'` 17B→210B ≈12×; `:'MVP'` 6B→~117B ≈20×) measures the MODEL'S RECALL + a lookup key, NOT nlir carrying information. The program is a ~0-novel-bit INDEX into pretrained weights; the model supplies the target for free because it's famous/canonical.

## Three honestly-different regimes (converged aur-0/aur-1/aur-2 — separate these on the site)
1. RECALL — program NAMES a memorised string (1984, OAuth def). Ratio = how famous the target is, not nlir density. Inflated/dishonest as a compression metric.
2. DERIVATION — a terse seed specifies a topic/structure the model ELABORATES in standard prose (aur-2's `brief={>@~$0}` 78B→1552B ≈20×). More honest (info is seed-derived, not string-recalled) BUT part of the ratio is the model adding DERIVABLE boilerplate for free.
3. NOVEL INFORMATION — target has specifics the model CAN'T guess (made-up names/numbers/facts). nlir must actually ENCODE the bits; you can't beat Shannon — a truly random novel paragraph golfs to ~1×.

## The honest test
Output must be reconstructable from PROGRAM + LIVE/PROVIDED CONTEXT ONLY, never from pretraining. Metric: shortest program P s.t. eval(P) ≈ T under the mutual-`~>` judge (+ brevity guard ≤1.15× target bytes, per the referee-integrity rule); honest ratio = |T|/|P|. The GAP between memorised-ratio and novel-ratio IS the model's memorisation.

## What passes honestly (nlir's real value)
- Novel-input TRANSFORM: seed carries YOUR facts the model has never seen — `brief%'<a specific standup / this bug / your draft>'`. Info comes from the seed.
- INSTRUCTION-compression over live chat: `~(>@^-1)` = "summarise the formalised, expanded version of their last message" — ~60 chars of English instruction → 7 sigils, applied to un-memorisable live context. Ratio = the-prompt-you'd-otherwise-type ÷ sigils.

## Plan / lane split
- aur-1 (det-only, SPEC/grammar): POSE fresh made-up targets with unguessable specifics (nonce-tagged), det-verify program structure.
- aur-0 / aur-2 / msm-* (live llm): golf each posed target, run the mutual-`~>` judge live, record the HONEST |T|/|P| ratio.
- Expected: pure-novel content lands ~1-2×; derivable-from-a-terse-idea stays high — which cleanly separates nlir's REAL compression (of derivable thought + instruction-over-novel-input) from mere recall.
- Ship: a site card contrasting the three regimes honestly ("recall is not compression; here's the honest ratio"), + feed the taxonomy into nlir-golf-rules / nlir-golf-pinpoints.

Provenance: golf-honest-ratio thread (sgu24-app + aur-0/aur-1/aur-2). Complements bd-3a589a (_seed exact-gating, blocked on backend) and the nlir-golf-pinpoints scratch note.

CONSOLIDATED DATA (aur-0, 2026-07-08): the full converged consensus + team's LIVE-measured numbers are captured durably in the `nlir-golf-pinpoints` scratch note under "★ HONEST-RATIO consensus". Headline: four regimes — RECALL (20-70×, measures fame, drop it) → DERIVATION (~7-10×, honest-er, seed-carried specifics + derivable boilerplate) → NOVEL/COMPUTED (~1×, Shannon floor) → ★INSTRUCTION-DENSITY (|prompt|÷|sigils| over unmemorised/live input, ~8-9×, the honest headline). Two honesty checks: (1) FACT-SURVIVAL (msm-3: `>@` keeps 5/6 novel facts @9.9×; `>@~` keeps 2/6 — `~` drops specifics → fake ratio); (2) the OPERATOR encodes honesty (msm-0: `~` lossy-by-design, `>@` fact-preserving). Ship card: `>@'<novel seed>'` BESIDE `>@~'<same seed>'` (one-sigil diff teaches compression + its honest limit) + msm-0's instruction-compression 8.2×. Live numbers: msm-0 8.2× (`{(@~$0)?}`), msm-1 ladder 70.5×→7.1×→~1×, msm-2 7.5× sat-verified, msm-3 fact-survival 9.9×/5-of-6. msm-0/msm-2 building the runnable proof.

LIVE REPRODUCTION + REFINEMENT (aur-0, 2026-07-08, rebuilt binary v0.1.4): Independently reproduced the honest fact-carrying expansion on a NOVEL unmemorisable seed (161B incident: KX-2291/19min/03:07 UTC/1874 orders/03:26 hotfix/RCA-Priya/ships-Thursday). Results: `>@'<seed>'` → 1758B (10.9×), 7/7 novel facts survive; `>@~'<seed>'` → 1459B (9.1×), ALSO 7/7 survive.

KEY REFINEMENT: `~`'s fact-drop is NON-DETERMINISTIC, not a hard rule. msm-3 saw `>@~` drop 2/6 facts on their seed; I saw 0/7 dropped on mine. So the correct framing for the SPEC/card is "`~` TENDS to shed dense specifics (lossy-by-design) — verify per-run," NOT "`~` drops facts" (deterministic). This makes the per-run FACT-SURVIVAL CHECK the load-bearing honesty guarantee (you can't rely on an a-priori operator rule; a high `>@~` ratio isn't automatically fake). Flagged to aur-1 (owns the honest-ratio SPEC/README) so it isn't written as deterministic.

Two gotchas for the runnable proof: (1) the model renders "1874"→"1,874", so automated fact-survival greps must strip commas / normalize digits. (2) `=>` requires a CURRENT config — a stale ~/.config/nlir/config.yaml (pre-`=>`) parse-errors "unexpected token Equals"; live golf must pass `--config config.example.yaml` or refresh the user config (a real upgrade gotcha: user configs don't auto-gain newly-landed operators).
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-0

title: nlir dual-game: golf-aur2-roman1984.sh (Roman→1984) + target-aur2-77-techdebt.sh (~> def)
description:
nlir dual-game tick (aur-2). Two examples + one documented limit:

FORWARD golf-aur2-roman1984.sh — "nineteen eighty-four": 'MCM'+'LXXXIV' = 1900+84 = 1984. Coercion parses BOTH operands as Roman numerals (MCM=1900, LXXXIV=84) and the + adds them to 1984 (Orwell's year). Roman coerces only because + forces it; bare 'MCM' stays literal. Verified -> 1984.

TARGET target-aur2-77-techdebt.sh — reverse game via ~> (summary of expand); dense TECHNICAL one-liner. TARGET (~209c) plain tech-debt. EXPR (26c) ~>'what is technical debt' -> "Technical debt is the metaphorical cost of future rework incurred when developers choose quick, expedient solutions over better, slower ones, and unmanaged debt gradually hurts development speed, quality, and maintainability." 88% shorter (26 chars -> full def); ~> adds the consequence in technical register.

DROPPED (documented limit): #'a group of crows' just restated "a group of crows" instead of naming the collective noun "a murder". The reverse-dictionary # names a term from a DEFINITION-shaped input (e.g. "a program that translates source code..." -> "Compiler"), but a "group of X" phrasing gets restated, not resolved to its collective noun.

Both landed examples verified via claude-sonnet-5.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-2

title: Installed nlir binary (nix-profile) lags checkout config schema — rejects `assoc` field
description:
Friction found while dogfooding the gen-op design review (bd-d18743) on node aurora.

The installed `nlir` at `/home/harry/.nix-profile/bin/nlir` is older than the repo config schema. Running the checkout's committed `config.example.yaml` (or the user's `~/.config/nlir/config.yaml`) against it fails at load:

    nlir: failed to parse config ...: unknown field `assoc`, expected one of
      `op`,`arity`,`fixity`,`priority`,`operands`,`result`,`template`,`join`,`command`,`reduce`,`model`,`prompt`

Root cause: the `assoc: right` associativity field (config.example.yaml:340, added with the `%`/`**` right-assoc work — bd-2f4d5e and the pow-assoc commits) is newer than the nix-profile binary. So any dogfooding that passes `--config config.example.yaml` to the installed binary silently can't run current-schema configs; you must build the checkout's `nlir` first.

Impact: agents dogfooding on this node reach for the installed `nlir` and hit a config parse error unrelated to their expression, which looks like a config bug but is a stale-binary issue. Slows the dogfood loop and can produce confidently-wrong "config is broken" diagnoses.

Suggested fix: refresh the installed binary (the justfile `sync-install` target, or a release/self-update refresh) as part of the node's dogfood setup, and/or have `nlir` emit a clearer "unknown field `assoc` — your nlir binary predates this config field; run `nlir update` / rebuild" hint on the unknown-field error so the stale-binary cause is obvious.

Repro (minimal): a config with only symbolic ops loads fine on the installed binary; adding `assoc: right` to any op triggers the reject. Confirmed the same binary parses/realises a minimal gen-op (`✎`) config-only otherwise.

Filed via reflect-session mixin from the bd-d18743 gen-op design review session.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-1

title: nlir dual-game: golf-aur2-typeguard.sh (type-guard law) + target-aur2-26-mvp.sh (:'MVP' 6c — tightest)
description:
nlir dual-game tick (aur-2). Two examples:

FORWARD golf-aur2-typeguard.sh — "the type guard" (a coercion LAW, complements the swarm's operator-algebra): 1-[2,3] -> HARD ERROR "a list is never a number", while [1,2]+3 -> 6 (spreads). Two near-identical exprs, opposite fates: arity-2 - needs list->number (a forbidden coercion, never guessed), variadic + SPREADS the list. The deterministic type layer keeps the LLM honest — nlir refuses to hallucinate a number where an LLM would invent one. DET (offline), verified.

TARGET target-aur2-26-mvp.sh — reverse game via :simplify on a bare ACRONYM; 6c (NEW TIGHTEST, cf :'YAGNI' 8c). EXPR (6c) :'MVP' -> "The simplest version of something you build first, just to see if it works, before you add all the fancy extra parts." ~117c target; 95% shorter; : gives the MEANING in plain words.

(Dropped this tick: ~>'how to stay focused' — ~> gives a meta-summary for how-tos, not the actual advice; ~> is for definitions.)

Both verified (forward DET; target via claude).

title: nlir power-move #18: the register ladder [~&[F],:&[F],@&[F]] — terse + plain + formal, all at once
description:
nlir power-move #18 (aur-2) — "the register ladder": one announcement rendered in ALL THREE registers at once — a terse status line, a plain note, and a formal write-up. Write the facts once, post to every channel.

SHAPE: [~&[FACTS], :&[FACTS], @&[FACTS]] — a 3-element list; nlir prints three renderings one per line. ~& = Slack/standup one-liner · :& = friendly/customer note (jargon rewritten plain) · @& = formal email/changelog (keeps terms). The leading op is the channel dial.

CAPSTONE of my tone-list family: dual-register brief splits by WHO reads (@ vs :), BLUF by HOW MUCH they read (~ vs @); register ladder gives the WHOLE tone trio — the three surfaces you'd otherwise write three times.

Filled + REAL (claude-sonnet-5): [~&['db migration tonight','~30min read-only window','rollback ready'], :&[SAME], @&[SAME]] ->
[terse] "Database migration tonight includes a ~30-minute read-only window, with rollback prepared."
[plain] "Tonight we're going to update the database... people will only be able to look at things, not change them. And if something goes wrong, we have a plan ready to undo it..."
[formal] "A database migration is scheduled for tonight. There will be an approximately 30-minute read-only window, and a rollback plan is ready in case it is needed."

Deliverables: examples/move-aur2-registerladder.sh (runs nlir live, --examples gate exit 0), POWERMOVES.md quick-ref row, showcase card nlir-register-ladder.png (my 8th card; short 2-fact deprecation example; single --only regen, honest llm pill) + scripts/build-showcase.py row. Verified | inside a composer slot ≈ comma-slots (not a distinct mechanism), so chose the ladder over a |-conditional.

title: nlir dual-game: golf-aur2-chesssquares.sh (n(n+1)(2n+1)/6=204) + target-aur2-110-datalake.sh (~> def)
description:
nlir dual-game tick (aur-2). Two examples:

FORWARD golf-aur2-chesssquares.sh — "squares on a chessboard": 'eight'*('eight'+'one')*('two'*'eight'+'one')/'six' = 8*(8+1)*(2*8+1)/6 = 8*9*17/6 = 1224/6 = 204. A chessboard has 64 unit squares, but how many squares of ANY size (1x1..8x8)? Sum of first 8 squares = n(n+1)(2n+1)/6 = 204. Two nested parens (an add + a multiply-then-add, precedence puts the double before the +1) feed a multiply-then-divide. Coercion reads every number-word. Verified -> 204.

TARGET target-aur2-110-datalake.sh — reverse game via ~> (summary of expand); 23-char seed. TARGET (~203c) plain data-lake. EXPR (23c) ~>'what is a data lake' -> "A data lake is a centralized repository storing raw structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data using schema-on-read, enabling flexible big data analytics, ML, and data science, but with distinct benefits and management challenges compared to traditional warehouses." Same core (central store of raw data in original form, keep everything, analyze later / schema-on-read), technical (high), 89% shorter.

Both verified via claude-sonnet-5.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-2

title: Named forms/dicts lose string-literal quotes on context round-trip — render() drops quotes, breaking reusable parts
description:
Root-caused while building Harry's "pyramid of thought" (reusable named parts).

BUG: `Expr::render()` (parser.rs) rendered a string literal WITHOUT its quotes
(`Expr::Quoted { content, .. } => content.clone()`). A form persists to context
as `{"__nlir_form__":"<render() source>"}` (eval.rs:2404 `inner.render()`), so
every string literal in a stored form lost its quotes → the source no longer
round-trips. On read-back the un-quoted text re-lexes wrong:
- `'!'` -> the `!` not-operator: `p={$0++'!'};$p%'hi'` -> "hinot ($0 ++ !)".
- prose with spaces -> bare words: `{('pitch for: '++$0)}` -> non-form error.
- `:` -> re-parse breaks (aur-0's earlier colon symptom — a subset).
Bare-safe single-token literals (`'false'`, `'x'`) round-tripped by luck (they
re-lex to themselves), which masked the bug (the named judge appeared to work).
Inline forms (`{...}%x`) were always fine — it's ONLY the context round-trip.
This blocked every reusable part carrying prose/prompt text (classify, route,
generation-with-instructions, judge-with-a-real-target) = the L3/L4 pyramid.

FIX: render `Expr::Quoted` WITH quotes, choosing the char by the `interpolate`
flag so a double-quoted interpolating string doesn't silently round-trip as raw
single-quoted and lose `$name` interpolation (msm-0's catch). Single quotes are
raw (lexer `'…'` takes no escapes, so content never contains a `'`); double
quotes escape `\` and `"`. Cascades to the `Expr::Dict` render arm (same
`v.render()` quote-drop) for free (msm-0).

VERIFIED: `{$0++'!'}%'hi'`->hi!; `{('pitch for: '++$0)}` round-trips; named
judge `{$if%($0~>$1,$1~>$0,'false')}` works; `{"hello $x"}` keeps interpolation.

Corroborated live by aur-0/aur-1/aur-2/msm-0/msm-1. Test-covered with form +
dict round-trip cases.

---

title: `_seed=N` system key: thread a seed into the llm request for reproducible output
description:
Feature suggested by sgu24-app + endorsed by aur-0: a `_seed=N` system context key that threads a `seed` into the llm request, giving REPRODUCIBLE llm output on seed-supporting backends. Unlocks: (a) CI-ASSERTABLE llm showcase cards (today only det cards are gated), (b) sgu24-app's "nlir golf" (stable regen targets → golf-solution = regression test), (c) reproducible dogfooding.

IMPLEMENTATION PLAN (fully scoped by aur-1 @3713986; ~40 lines, mostly unit-testable):
1. `src/context.rs`: add `seed()` reader mirroring `precision()` (~line 229) — read `_seed` as an integer (JSON number via `_seed=N`, or a parseable string); return `Option<i64>`. Runtime-assignable via `_seed=N` like `_sep`/`_precision`.
2. `src/eval.rs`: where the evaluator assembles the `LlmCall` prompt vars (the `crate::llm::assemble_llm` / assemble_llm_vars site — realiser.rs:31 notes the evaluator builds model+prompt vars), add `NLIR_SEED` to the var map from `ctx.seed()` when set (empty/absent when unset). Context is available at eval time.
3. `src/llm.rs` `build_anthropic_request` (~line 620, at the `output_config`-merge step which already merges arbitrary top-level body fields): when `vars["NLIR_SEED"]` is present + parses as an integer, set `body["seed"] = N`. OpenAI-compatible proxies honor `seed`; Anthropic Messages ignores it (no-op, not an error).
4. Command models: `${NLIR_SEED}` flows via the vars automatically once (2) adds it, so a `command:` template can pass `--seed ${NLIR_SEED}` if the CLI supports it. No extra code.
5. Unit test: assert `build_anthropic_request` includes `seed: 42` when `NLIR_SEED=42` is in vars, and omits it when unset. Det/offline-testable — no live model needed.
6. `SPEC.md` §Runtime state / system keys: document `_seed` alongside `_sep`/`_cache`/`_precision` (+ the CI `verify-spec-ops` guard already keeps operators in sync; system keys are prose). Note the backend caveat.

BACKEND CAVEAT (aur-0's routing plan): Anthropic Messages has no native `seed`, so `_seed` is a no-op on the default claude/generative path. But route ASSERTABLE/golf cards through a seed-supporting backend — the OpenAI-compat litellm proxy (100.83.90.42:4000) likely honors `seed` — while default fuzzy ops stay on Anthropic. aur-0 will run the empirical backend-honors-seed matrix (same seeded expr twice per backend → which give byte-identical output) once the threading lands, telling us which backend to route assertable cards through.

OWNER: TBD — aur-1 (design/lane) can implement the threading in a fresh pass, OR aur-0/aur-2 can grab it with the live model + implement+test in one shot (model-test coupling favors a model-having implementer). Filed by aur-1 with full scoping so it's a ~30-min job for whoever takes it.

title: CLI: bare `nlir 'EXPR'` ≡ `nlir -e 'EXPR'` when unambiguous
description:
Harry (2026-07-05, basics batch broadcast): "lets have running nlir 'expr' be same as nlir -e 'expr' when its not ambiguous."

Today `nlir '1+2'` → clap error "unrecognized subcommand '1+2'". Fix (main.rs): add a clap `#[command(external_subcommand)] External(Vec<String>)` variant to Command — the first arg that is NOT a known subcommand (parse/step/test/help/show/repl/tui/set/get/append-message/mcp/self-update/feedback) is captured as an external positional and evaluated as the expression. So `nlir '1+2'` ≡ `nlir -e '1+2'`; real subcommands always win (the disambiguation is "is the first arg a subcommand name?"), and `-e` remains for exprs that open with `-` (leading-hyphen values still route through --expr's allow_hyphen_values).

Verified: `nlir '1+2'`→3, `nlir '(2+3)*4'`→20, `nlir --mode det '2**3'`→8, `nlir test`→runs the test suite (subcommand wins), `nlir -e '1+2'`→3 (unchanged), bare `nlir`→help. +2 parse tests (bare-positional→External, subcommand-wins). cargo test --all 264+33, clippy -D + fmt clean.

Slice (5) of Harry's basics batch (my CLI lane per the fleet split: bare-nlir + det-test-cov = msm-1).

FORWARD golf-aur2-compound.sh — "compound growth" + an honest f64 lesson: 'a thousand'*('1'+'10%')**'two' = 1000*(1.1^2). Compound interest = principal*(1+rate)^years; precedence squares the growth factor first. EXACT answer is 1210, BUT f64: 1.1 (like 0.1) has no exact binary form, so 1.1^2 = 1.2100000000000002 and the result PRINTS 1210.0000000000002. A real demonstration of the floating-point limit (cf. golf-aur2-ceiling, bd-50f84a) — arithmetic right, last bit is the cost of binary fractions. Verified -> 1210.0000000000002.

TARGET target-aur2-83-opportunitycost.sh — reverse game via :simplify ECONOMICS jargon (opportunity cost) -> plain (aur-2 : lane), 15% shorter. TARGET (~188c) plain opportunity-cost. EXPR (159c) :'opportunity cost is the value of the next best alternative that you give up when you make a choice since resources like time and money can only be used once' -> "When you choose one thing, you can't also choose something else at the same time — because you only have so much time or money, and once you use it, it's gone. Opportunity cost is just a fancy name for the other good thing you had to give up when you picked what you picked." Closeness: same idea, plain (high), 15% shorter.

Both verified via claude-sonnet-5.

title: [design] Unary minus / negative literals — should nlir support prefix `-` (negative numbers + negative-index exprs)?
description:
Design question for Harry (flagged by msm-0, filed by aur-0 with msm-0's go-ahead). Should nlir support unary minus / negative literals?

Today negatives are only reachable via:
- binary subtraction from a literal: `0-5` => -5
- computed expressions: `(0-1)**0.5`, `(0-1)^*0` (a computed negative index)

There is NO unary-minus / negative-literal syntax, so:
- `-5` does not evaluate (`-` is infix-only: "operator \"-\" is not valid in prefix position")
- `5*-2` fails (no unary minus after an operator)
- a range/index expression that opens with a negative index, e.g. `-1^*0`, reaches the evaluator (after CLI bd-d2d23c's allow_hyphen_values fix) but then hits the same prefix-`-` parser limit
- negative number literals like `-3.14` cannot be written directly

This is a real LANGUAGE FEATURE, deliberately NOT bundled into the CLI paper-cut bd-d2d23c (that only fixed clap eating a leading `-`). It needs a design + SPEC decision, not an overnight ride-along:
- Add a dual-role prefix/infix `-` to the parser (unary negate when in prefix position, binary subtract when infix), OR
- Support negative numeric literals in the lexer (`-5`, `-3.14`) and negative-index literals, OR
- Decide the current 0-N / (0-1)^*N idioms are sufficient and leave `-` infix-only (document it explicitly in SPEC).

Tradeoffs to weigh: parser ambiguity between prefix-negate and infix-subtract (precedence/associativity), interaction with the `^N` negative-index reads (which already accept `-1` as an index token in that position), and SPEC's current "negatives come from 0-N" implicit stance. Lexer vs parser split (negative literal token vs prefix operator) is itself part of the design.

Lane: parser (msm-0) — draft/low-pri, awaiting Harry's SPEC call before it becomes queued work. Related: bd-d2d23c (CLI leading-hyphen, closed), bd-f551f9 (float literals, closed — the lexer-number-shape cousin).

title: [broken-on-main] CI ensure-curl/rustup step exits 1 under set -e (final short-circuited test), reddening main
description:
main CI is RED: the "Ensure curl and rustup are reachable for the toolchain install (minimal step PATH)" step (added by my curl+rustup PATH fix, landed 1fda55b) fails on the build+test job, skipping every downstream step. Routed to me by aur-0, who is HOLDING the v0.1.2 cut on a red main.

Failing run: 28705697657 @cb8d62d -> `##[error]Process completed with exit code 1`.

Root cause (confirmed from the step log): the run shell is `bash --noprofile --norc -e -o pipefail` (set -e). The step body ends with `[ -x "$d/rustup" ] && echo "$d" >> "$GITHUB_PATH"`. On the azure-ephemeral runner rustup is not installed yet (that is exactly what the next dtolnay step does), so the final `[ -x ]` test returns non-zero, the `&&` short-circuits, and that becomes the SCRIPT's exit code -> the step fails even though the PATH appends all succeeded. Same shape for the first loop's `/opt/homebrew/bin` on Linux. It is not a mid-script set -e trip (those `&&` lists are exempt) — it is purely the final exit code.

Fix: make this best-effort PATH step incapable of failing the job — convert `[ ... ] && echo` to `if [ ... ]; then echo ...; fi` (set -e-safe) and end with `exit 0`. Verified locally under `bash -e -o pipefail` with homebrew/rustup absent: OLD exits 1, NEW exits 0, both append the identical real dirs. Applies to all three identical step bodies: ci.yml (build+test) and release.yml (both jobs).

Acceptance: main ci.yml build+test goes green; a release run's aarch64-darwin leg passes; then aur-0 cuts v0.1.2.
creator: ms-mac:nlir:ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-0
spoken_name: fix ci curl rustup exit code

title: SPEC/help: `..` seed-reliability caveat (terse descriptors are ambiguous; prefer unambiguous seeds)
description:
Golf pinpoint (aur-2 found, aur-0 live-confirmed): the terse `..` seed `sol..3`→"Earth" is fragile — `sol` is ambiguous (the Sun vs the solfège note) and llm realisation is non-deterministic (`sol..7`→"Si", `sol..4` drifted to "Fa"/"Mars" across runs). The `..` docs use the robust seed in examples but carry no caveat about terse-seed ambiguity. msm-2's new site card (bd-e84379, d3e0bb5) deliberately uses the robust `'the planets from the sun'..3` for drift-free rendering.

FIX (aur-1, SPEC/grammar lane): add a "seed reliability" caveat to `..` — full note in SPEC prose (accessors section ~L195), short clause in config.example.yaml access-semantic desc (feeds `nlir help`). Guidance: terse descriptors win on brevity but are ambiguous/non-deterministic; prefer an unambiguous seed for anything you'll re-run or ship.

Also resolves aur-0's `=>` format-spec candidate as DOCS-CONFIRMED (no change): SPEC L324 `=>` row already says "length/format constraints included" — covers "spec the separator/no-preamble for an exact string." Same as the `>`-vs-`=>` case: existing docs adequate, don't add redundant prose.

title: Re-export or document nlir::Mode so nlir::config::Mode isn't a confusing private path
description: Friction observed building the nlir tui workbench (bd-ae1730 session). The `Mode` enum (Det/Llm) is defined at the crate root (`nlir::Mode`), but `config.rs` re-imports it privately (`use crate::Mode;`) and all the mode-adjacent API lives in the config module (`resolve_defaults`, `ResolvedDefaults.mode`, `defaults.mode: Mode`). So the natural reach from the bin was `nlir::config::Mode::Det`, which fails with a confusing `error[E0603]: enum Mode is private` — the fix (`nlir::Mode::Det`) isn't obvious because everything else you touch for modes is under `nlir::config`. Suggested fix: add `pub use crate::Mode;` (re-export) in config.rs so both paths resolve, OR a short doc note on `ResolvedDefaults.mode` pointing at the canonical `nlir::Mode` path. Low priority, pure ergonomics. Filed via reflect-session mixin from the bd-ae1730 TUI session.

title: release.yml aarch64-darwin build leg failing (nix macOS dependency)
description:
The `build aarch64-darwin` leg in .github/workflows/release.yml has been failing since ~2026-07-04 (runs 28721262361, 28719755717, 28708607800, 28707987113 all "failure").

FAILURE: the aarch64-darwin leg builds via nix on the `[self-hosted, aarch64-darwin]` runner and dies with:
  error: Build failed due to failed dependency
  (nix stack trace mentions `computedImpureHostDeps`)
i.e. a nix macOS impure-host-deps / dependency resolution issue on the darwin runner — NOT an nlir code problem (the x86_64-linux + test legs pass).

IMPACT (bounded): the matrix is `fail-fast: false` and each leg uploads its own artifact independently ("Upload assets to the GitHub Release" is inside the build job), so a failed aarch64-darwin does NOT block the x86_64-linux or aarch64-linux artifacts — those still publish. The only effect is the macOS-ARM (aarch64-darwin) tarball is MISSING from releases, and the overall run is cosmetically marked "failure" even when the Linux artifacts published fine. So bd-d2437c's aarch64-linux artifact is unaffected.

NEEDS: someone with macOS/nix + aarch64-darwin runner access to debug the nix `computedImpureHostDeps` failure (aur-2 is on aurora Linux with no rustup/macOS/nix-darwin, cannot reproduce or test a darwin nix build). 

OPTIONS: (a) fix the nix darwin dependency; (b) if the darwin artifact is deprioritized, mark the aarch64-darwin matrix leg `continue-on-error: true` so releases show green when only that leg fails (masks the breakage but stops the alarming "failure" status on otherwise-good releases) — a judgment call for the release owner. Non-urgent: does not block Linux releases.

Filed from a release-pipeline health check (aur-2, release.yml lane). Diagnosis only; no fix attempted (untestable here).

DIAGNOSIS (msm-1, ms-mac aarch64-darwin, 2026-07-05): NOT reproducible from a normal ms-mac agent context, and NOT a flake bug. Ran the EXACT CI approach `nix develop '.#devShells.aarch64-darwin.default' --builders '' --command bash -c 'cargo build --release --bin nlir'` → builds CLEAN (DEVSHELL_OK, cargo 1.91, clang 21, exit 0). The failing `feedbackCliPatched` runCommand + the whole devShell build fine here.

Root cause is RUNNER-ENVIRONMENT-SPECIFIC, not the flake: my nix is Determinate Nix 3.13.2 (2.32.4); the self-hosted runner is nix 2.34.7. `computedImpureHostDeps` is a darwin-stdenv impure-host-deps mechanism whose handling likely differs in nix 2.34.7 vs 2.32.4 (or the runner's service-user env: HOME=/var/empty, `builders=@/etc/nix/machines`). So a flake change made+tested on a normal ms-mac can't be verified against the runner's failure. Recent flake change 206f82e is NOT the cause (it landed 07-05; the failure started 07-04 — predates it).

CONCLUSION: this genuinely needs the RUNNER's nix 2.34.7 to reproduce/fix (I lack that from an agent checkout). Two paths for the release owner (Harry):
(a) MITIGATE now — `continue-on-error: true` on the aarch64-darwin matrix leg so otherwise-good releases stop showing cosmetic "failure" (darwin tarball stays missing until a real fix). Low-risk workflow-YAML change; I can land it on your go.
(b) RUNNER-SIDE FIX — on the darwin runner (nix 2.34.7), reproduce + fix the computedImpureHostDeps failure (align the runner nix version to a known-good, or adjust the flake's darwin impureHostDeps for 2.34.7). Needs runner access.

Unclaiming: I can't complete the runner-side fix from here (my nix builds clean), so leaving it for whoever has runner nix-2.34.7 access. Mitigation offer stands.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-2
