examples/move-msm1-sets.sh — 5 deterministic, offline (no-key) "why nlir" set shapes, mirroring move-msm0-pipe.sh's structure:
- MISSING-ITEMS GATE: $diff%([auth,logging,tests,docs],[auth,tests]) -> logging docs
- DEDUP/NUB: $union%[bug,ui,bug,perf,ui,bug] -> bug ui perf
- COMMON GROUND: $inter%([alice,bob,carol],[bob,carol,dave]) -> bob carol
- MEMBERSHIP GATE: $if%($elem%('ERROR',$_stdin),'investigate','ok') over a piped line -> investigate
- DICT KEYS + MERGE: $union%({auth=1,logging=2},{logging=9,tracing=3}) -> auth logging tracing

Header is honest about the language boundary (a pinpoint found by running it live): $elem is EXACT (substring/element/key) membership, NOT semantic; for "is it about X" the tool is the fuzzy ~>? operator. Points to move-msm0-pipe.sh for the det+fuzzy pipe family. All lines verified against the built binary.

title: nlir docs: op[list]/op^ = op-on-rendered-text (not a structural map) — correct changelog #25 + POWERMOVES list-algebra note
description:
nlir docs precision (aur-2) — swarm convergence refining my changelog #25 "op[list] MAPS" finding. aur-0 (DET mechanism) + aur-1 (op-specific) + msm-0 (message ranges) converged: op[list] = the op applied to the list RENDERED AS TEXT — never a structural map. Verified DET: !['a','b','c'] = "not a\nb\nc" (\"not \" prepended ONCE to the multi-line render), !&['a','b','c'] = "not a and b and c" (structural weave). In LLM mode the model guesses per-op over the text blob: only :[list] reliably maps per-item, @[list]/>[list] are non-deterministic (bloom-last/weave), reductive #/~/<[list] FOLD to one, &[list] WEAVES structurally. msm-0: message RANGES are the SAME algebra (op^* likewise op-on-rendered-text). Upgrades the proposed MAP ↦ to a concretely-justified structural gap (the only guaranteed per-item/per-message map — @↦ formal changelog, @↦^_ formalise each turn).

Fix: corrected examples/move-aur2-changelog.sh register-dial advice (only : reliably maps; @/~ over a list do NOT give a formal/terse changelog) + added a canonical "List algebra" note to examples/POWERMOVES.md COMPOSE section (lists AND ranges). The changelog move itself is unchanged + solid (: reliably maps in practice). --examples gate still exit 0. Credit aur-0/aur-1/msm-0.

title: fix wasm.yml: dtolnay/rust-toolchain + wasm32 target (runner has no rustup) — green the P1 wasm build
description:
aur-0 asked me to confirm the wasm-build goes green now that P1 (crates/nlir-wasm) landed @da34b92. My guarded wasm.yml correctly ACTIVATED (detected the crate) but the build FAILED (GH Actions run 28735057325): the step `rustup target add wasm32-unknown-unknown` died with `rustup: command not found` (exit 127). Root cause: the self-hosted [self-hosted, azure-ephemeral] runner ships SYSTEM Rust with NO rustup on PATH (the rust-not-nix setup, bd-aa78ee) — my wasm.yml wrongly assumed a rustup-managed toolchain like a standard GH runner.

FIX (mirror ci.yml's proven toolchain setup on this runner): replaced the naive `rustup target add` step with (all gated on the detect step, so still a no-op when the crate is absent): (1) Move CARGO_HOME off the SMB build-cache (bd-f35766 hardlink fix); (2) Ensure curl+rustup reachable on the minimal step PATH (bd-7e465a); (3) dtolnay/rust-toolchain@stable with `targets: wasm32-unknown-unknown` (installs rustup + the wasm target the way ci.yml does); (4) cargo install wasm-pack. Then wasm-pack build --target web + the *_bg.wasm smoke run as before.

This is the real wasm32 verification aur-0 couldn't run on their source-cargo node. Once green it emits crates/nlir-wasm/pkg and unblocks P7-full (version-stamp + site-embed). File: .github/workflows/wasm.yml (my CI lane).
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-2

title: Stream each eval step live as it arrives (CLI/TUI/WASM), not batch at the end
description:
Harry (2026-07-05): "in the CLI, TUI, WASM builds, can we show each step live as it comes in, not just dump all steps at the end?"

Today the step surfaces BATCH: CLI run_step (main.rs) calls eval::step_trace() which returns Vec<String> (all steps computed), then loops + prints. Same batch pattern in the TUI step-through (run_step_view / the Ctrl-T workbench view) and the wasm step()/graphFrames exports. In DET mode steps compute instantly so batching is invisible, but in LLM mode each step is a slow realisation — so streaming each step as it completes is a big UX win (watch it unfold, not wait for all then dump).

Proposal: a STREAMING step API in eval.rs (msm-0's eval-core lane) — e.g. step_trace_streaming(expr, cfg, ctx, mode, on_step: FnMut(&str)) (+ an async twin step_trace_streaming_async for llm), yielding each reduced expr as it's produced. Then the surfaces consume it live:
- CLI (main.rs run_step): print each step as the callback fires (flush stdout).
- TUI (run_step_view / Ctrl-T workbench): push each step into the view + requestRender as it arrives.
- WASM (crates/nlir-wasm step/graphFrames): stream via a JS callback per step (the workspace step()/animate() already render incrementally; wire the async per-step callback).

Lane split: eval-core streaming API = msm-0 (eval). Surface wiring: CLI = msm-1 (offered), TUI = msm-2, WASM = aurora. Coordinate the callback signature first, then wire each surface.

Acceptance: `nlir step` (and the TUI/workbench + wasm) print/render each step the moment it's realised, especially visible in llm mode; det behaviour unchanged.
creator: ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-1

title: EPIC: nlir computational-graph visualization — shared kitty-CLI + wasm renderer (nlir show / --graph / --animate / --save-animation)
description:
Harry directive (2026-07-05): a computational-graph visualization for nlir, with a SHARED renderer between the CLI (kitty) and the wasm webapp so both show the same beautiful graphs.

WHAT HARRY ASKED FOR:
- `nlir show -e '...'` renders the computational graph of an expression — its AST, but with circularity / references RESOLVED: e.g. a variable assignment (`k = ...`) becomes a node with edges to every AST node that uses `k` as an input (a dataflow graph, not just a syntax tree).
- Render the SAME graph as we step through execution — as each subtree reduces, the graph evolves. Long text nodes (model output can be big) print as "first few words … last few words" so diagrams stay consistent.
- Rolled into the existing stderr step printout: `--graph` prints a graph for each step; `--animate` is a lightweight TUI showing the computation evolving over time.
- If the terminal supports kitty graphics (detect), render the graphs to beautiful transparent-bg PNGs, shown as a kitty terminal ANIMATION, and saveable to `.apng` / `.gif` via `--save-animation`.
- SHARE the PNG/graph renderer with the wasm webapp so the SAME beautiful graphs appear in the wasm UI as in kitty mode.

ARCHITECTURE (proposed — the shared-renderer seam):
nlir expr → **dataflow graph model** (AST + binding-reference resolution; G0) → **layout + SVG** (pure, cross-platform, SHARED; G1) → then two backends: NATIVE = SVG→PNG (resvg/usvg) → kitty-protocol animation of step frames → --save-animation apng/gif (G4); WASM = the same SVG injected into the P2 workspace DOM (browsers render SVG natively → identical visuals; G5). SVG is the shared vector format because it is pure Rust to emit (lives in core, no native deps) and both a native rasterizer and the browser consume it → ONE renderer, identical graphs. Step frames (G2) keep a STABLE layout across frames; reduced subtrees show as truncated Values.

GROUNDING (real surface): AST is `parser::Expr` (public: Apply{op,fixity,operands}, Assign{key,value}, ContextRead(name), List, Message/MessageRange, Quoted, Number, Bare, Value…). `eval::step_trace` / `Evaluator::step_once` already splice `Expr::Value` in as subtrees reduce — the exact hook for step-through frames.

SLICES (child beads, proposed lanes): G0 graph model+extraction (core src/graph.rs; msm-0/AST) · G1 graph→SVG shared renderer (core, pure; aur-2 lead + aur-1 aesthetic) · G2 step-through frames stable-layout (core; msm-0 step + shared renderer) · G3 CLI `nlir show` + `--graph`/`--animate` flags (main.rs; aur-2) · G4 kitty PNG animation + `--save-animation` apng/gif (native; aur-2) · G5 wasm webapp graph panel (P2 workspace; aur-1). Filed per Harry's explicit request; lanes are proposals — claim/confer per the swarm norm.

title: fresh agent checkouts lack a git author identity for direct commits
description:
A managed agent checkout has no git `user.name`/`user.email` configured, so the
first direct `git commit` a dev worker makes fails with "Author identity
unknown ... Please tell me who you are" until the agent sets a local identity.
Prior mainline commits are authored `Cacophony Daemon <daemon@cacophony.local>`,
so workers must discover and replicate that convention by hand. Suggested fix:
seed a repo-local git identity (matching the daemon convention) at checkout
creation, or document the expected identity in AGENTS/SPEC. May be a
cacophony-harness concern rather than nlir-specific. Filed via reflect-session
mixin from bd-700306 session.

## Note (msm-0, 2026-07-03 ~19:55)

Current state: managed nlir checkouts now DO carry a repo-local git identity — both my agent checkout and the daemon canonical checkouts (`~/.cacophony/daemon/checkouts/{nlir,cacophony}`) have `user.name`/`user.email` set locally, and a global `~/.gitconfig` identity also exists as a fallback. So direct `git commit` works today; this appears resolved at the harness level since the 16:27 filing. Leaving open as a harness/dx concern for aur-2 / the cacophony-harness owner to confirm the seeding is intentional + universal (fresh checkouts on a host without a global identity). Not an nlir-repo code change.

- pub fn operator_reference(&Config) -> Vec<OperatorRef>: one row per configured operator, description = op.summary(), det = op.is_deterministic(), arity stringified (">0"/"N"), fixity lowercase string, priority Option (None → JS null).

Full CI green (fmt + clippy -D + test --all 219+3 + example_config + nlir test 17/17). Native nlir help keeps its own display logic but shares the underlying methods (content never drifts); a future cleanup could route run_help through operator_reference() too. @aur-0 ready to wire.

title: llm-mode coercion bypasses type rules: list->number is LLM-coerced (invents a number) instead of a hard error
description:
Found dogfooding nlir in LLM mode. `nlir --mode llm -e "[1,2]+3"` returns 6 instead of a loud error. Per SPEC (Types & coercion) and the landed coercion layer (bd-20df97), `list -> number` is ALWAYS an error and must never be routed to the LLM. But eval's coerce_operand (src/eval.rs, from bd-ba9f85) handles the llm branch by calling llm::realise_llm directly with the types.number model+prompt, which asks the model to interpret the rendered list ("1\n2") as a number -> it invents one (here 3), so [1,2]+3 -> 6. This violates the type-system hard rule and silently produces a wrong result.

Root cause: coerce_operand reimplements the coercion flow (deterministic -> realise_llm) instead of delegating to the types-layer coercion API (llm::coerce_with_llm, bd-ecb930), which enforces the list->number hard error and the deterministic/loud-error rules. realise_llm is the OPERATOR-realisation seam, not the coercion seam.

Fix options: (a) make coerce_operand call llm::coerce_with_llm in llm mode (it already does deterministic-first, list->number hard error, model coercion via the types.model/prompt, and UnparseableResult on a non-T answer) instead of realise_llm; or (b) add an explicit list->number (and other never-LLM) guard in coerce_operand before the realise_llm call. Option (a) also fixes: caching (coerce_with_llm can front the CoercionCache bd-876367 -- coercions are currently NOT cached), and correct loud errors when the model returns a non-number. eval.rs is msm-0/aur-1 lane; this is the types-coercion semantics (aur-2 lane) -- coordinate the wire like the realise_llm seam.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-2

creator: ms-mac:nlir:harryaskham

title: Nested parallelization (parallelize deeply-nested independent subcalls, not just top-level)
description: The parallel DAG scheduler (bd-780dbf) parallelizes only the TOP-LEVEL operands of each Apply: a parallelized operand subtree evaluates SEQUENTIALLY inside (eval_parallel_safe does not spawn nested threads). This bounds total concurrency simply (max ~parallelism per Apply) but does not run deeply-nested independent LLM/command calls concurrently — e.g. in `(#a&#b) & (#c&#d)` the inner pairs run sequentially within their outer-operand thread. A nested scheduler (a shared bounded semaphore/pool across recursion, or a work-stealing queue) would lift this while keeping the global parallelism bound. Also: once nested parallelization exists, the backtick Serial handling (bd-f66c32) becomes load-bearing (it must prevent nested parallelization inside a `\`` subtree) — currently vacuous since nothing nests. Filed via reflect-session from the bd-780dbf parallelism session.

title: Live partial result display: speculative debounced eval + incremental caching across REPL/TUI/Pi-plugin/web
description:
Operator request (Harry, 2026-07-05): live partial result display as you type/edit an nlir expression, across the REPL, the TUI workbench, the Pi plugin editor, and the web workspace.

## The idea
Instead of only evaluating on submit (Enter/run), show the result-so-far LIVE as you compose an expression:
- **Speculative execution on debounce**: after the user stops typing for a short interval (e.g. ~300-500ms), speculatively evaluate the current expression and show the (possibly partial) result.
- **Incremental caching + targeted invalidation**: cache previously-computed steps/subresults; when the user edits, invalidate only the edited subtree(s) and their transitive dependents, and REUSE the cached results for the unchanged parts (so re-eval after a small edit is cheap). The eval already builds a dependency DAG (the parallel scheduler), so cached node results can be reused when a node's inputs are unchanged — key cached results by subexpression identity (structure + bound context).
- **Live display**: show the result-so-far, and/or a truncated/brief view of the evaluation STACK (what's computed, what's pending) so you can see what you're working with as you build the expression.

## Modes / cost gating (important)
- **Det mode is the safe default for speculation**: deterministic ops are instant + offline + free, so debounced speculative eval is cheap and safe.
- **LLM mode speculation must be gated**: firing paid/slow LLM realisations on every debounce is expensive and surprising. Options: det-only speculative preview by default with a manual "preview with llm" trigger; or cache llm sub-results aggressively and only re-fire the edited llm nodes; or a per-node cost budget. Needs a design decision.

## Surfaces (sliceable)
- **TUI workbench** (my lane): live preview in the Output pane + a brief truncated stack view; overlaps/extends bd-8c89b1 (multiline + live keystroke preview) and pairs with the Ctrl-T step-through (bd-d8d757, closed) and llm-mode eval (bd-16d86c). Deterministic speculative preview is a natural first slice here.
- **REPL** (rustyline): a status-line / below-prompt preview of the current line's det result as you type (rustyline supports a helper/hint interface).
- **Pi plugin** (extensions/nlir.js editor): show the live partial result in the yellow-editor nlir mode.
- **Web workspace** (aur-1's lane): debounced live eval in the expr box (already has run/step infra + WASM det eval); "fun on the website too" per Harry.

## Technical core (the meaty part)
Incremental speculative evaluation: a memoization layer over the reducer keyed on subexpression identity, with AST-diff-driven invalidation on edit (invalidate changed nodes + transitive dependents, reuse the rest). Reuses step_trace / the DAG scheduler. The step-streaming API (step_trace_streaming, msm-0 @a7085e2) is relevant for llm nodes resolving live.

## Relationships
- Extends bd-8c89b1 (nlir tui multiline + live keystroke preview) — the live-preview aspect.
- Relates to bd-16d86c (opt-in llm-mode eval in the workbench), bd-d8d757 (step-through, closed), and the eval-core streaming API.

Filed to track per Harry's request; not yet started. Best first slice: deterministic debounced live preview in the TUI workbench (my lane).

PROGRESS (2026-07-05): Slice 1 — TUI deterministic live preview — LANDED @e38868b. The `nlir tui` workbench now debounces (~350ms) and shows the det result-so-far in an "Output · live" pane as you type (non-persisting; Enter commits). Lane split confirmed with msm-0: he owns the incremental-cache eval-core (subexpr-identity memoization + AST-diff invalidation) for the llm-gated tier — the meaty optimization. Remaining OPEN slices: llm-mode live preview in the TUI (needs msm-0's cache + cost gating; pairs with bd-16d86c), REPL preview (msm-3 lane), web workspace debounced preview (aur-1 lane), Pi plugin editor preview. Bead intentionally left open as the cross-surface tracker.

PROGRESS (2026-07-05): Slice 2 — Pi-plugin live det preview — LANDED @50e285a. extensions/nlir.js now shows a debounced (~350ms) det result-so-far in a setWidget "nlir »" widget above the editor as you type a |-prefixed line (offline/free via --mode det; cleared on send; graceful degradation; node --check clean; needs a pi session reload to see live). Also landed my assigned §4 partial-result-display section in docs/design/agent-vocabulary.md (TUI+pi previews marked landed + the shared partial-display contract: debounce-not-per-keystroke, det=safe-free-default, speculative≠committed, llm tier gated+cached on msm-0's incremental cache). Remaining OPEN: llm-mode live preview (needs msm-0's incremental cache tier), REPL preview (msm-3), web preview (aur-1).

PROGRESS (2026-07-05): Slice 3 — REPL live det-preview (msm-3 lane) — LANDED @d95a5a7. `nlir repl` now shows the current line's DETERMINISTIC result-so-far as a dim ` → <result>` hint after the cursor as you type (rustyline Hinter/Highlighter `ReplHelper`). Side-effect-free (evals a CLONE in Mode::Det, never saves — speculative/non-persisting per the shared contract). KEY safety call: because the rustyline hint fires synchronously per-keystroke (no debounce, unlike the TUI), it SKIPS any expression using a shell-`command:` operator (e.g. `_` echo) — det eval would shell out; command sigils collected from config via `op.command.is_some()`. Unobtrusive: empty/`:meta`/>160-char/parse-or-eval-error (incl. llm op in det) lines show no hint; only when cursor at end. Context snapshot refreshed after each submission so writes show in later hints. Graceful fallback to plain line-editing preserved. +regression test (repl_preview_hints_pure_det_and_skips_command_ops); real-TTY tmux verified (2*21→ →42, fold∘map→ →14, map→ →1 4 9, command-op & llm-in-det → no hint). Remaining OPEN: llm-mode live preview (needs msm-0's incremental cache tier), web workspace debounced preview (aur-1 lane). Bead stays open as the cross-surface tracker.

PROGRESS (2026-07-08, aur-0): Slice 4 — WEB workspace debounced det live-preview — LANDED @f0f88be. The site workspace (site/workspace/) now shows the deterministic result-so-far on a dim ` → ` line directly under the expression editor, ~350ms after you pause typing — its own line, never clobbering the committed Output pane. Follows the shared partial-display contract exactly: debounce-not-per-keystroke (~350ms); DET is the safe-free-default (the preview ALWAYS evals in det with no realiser, so llm/paid realisation is never fired on a keystroke — gated behind an explicit Run; in llm/on-device mode the line carries a "det preview · Run for llm" tag so the gating is legible); speculative≠committed (non-persisting — the wasm det path evals a fresh context per call — and hides silently on parse/eval errors incl. an llm op in det mode, like the REPL's ` → ` hint). Seq guard drops stale evals; over-length guard skips very long exprs; refreshes after wasm swaps in and on mode change. Nice web-only property: the browser command-VM is sandboxed, so even `_`-echo previews safely offline. Verified: node --check clean + an 8-case DOM-stub harness (power/subtraction/empty/llm-tag/over-length/HTML-escape) all green. Also marked the web slice landed in docs/design/agent-vocabulary.md §4. Remaining OPEN: llm-mode live preview across surfaces (still needs msm-0's incremental-cache tier + cost gating). Bead stays open as the cross-surface tracker.

title: REPL step-through: Tab to expand the next eval step inline (learn-the-language)
description:
Harry request (2026-07-05): in the nlir REPL, pressing Tab expands only the NEXT step of the eval and inlines it — step through an expression's expansion in realtime, as a way of seeing/learning how nlir evaluates. e.g. `~[@a, @b]` → Tab → `~[<formal a>, @b]` → Tab → `~[<formal a>, <formal b>]` → Tab → `<summary>`.

Scope: needs single-step evaluation (the eval currently runs to completion) — expose one reduction step (innermost/leftmost redex) + substitute inline, and a REPL Tab keybinding that advances one step + reprints. Fits the REPL epic (bd-c6719d). Bigger than nlir-help: touches eval (step exposure) + REPL UI. Deterministic ops step cleanly; LLM ops realise one node per step.

Lane: parser/eval/REPL (msm-0), or pair with types/eval. Open for divvy.

PROGRESS (2026-07-05): step-through ENGINE + non-interactive `nlir step` LANDED on main @6771c0c (independent of the held Δ+bare-views grammar). `Evaluator::step_once(&Expr)->Step{Reduced(Expr),Done(Value)}` (leftmost-innermost redex, `Expr::Value` splice), `eval::step_trace()`, `Expr::render_step()`, `nlir step 'EXPR'` subcommand. det `2+3*4`→`2 + (3 * 4)`→`2 + «12»`→`«14»`; llm `~[@a,@b]` unfolds one realise/step. 215 lib tests (2 new step tests). REMAINING (aur-0, in progress): interactive crossterm raw-mode `:step` REPL Tab UX (Tab/Space=next · Enter=run · q/Esc=cancel · TTY-gated) driving `step_once`. Bead stays in_progress until the REPL Tab lands.

title: nlir showcase: composable persistent library (parts reference parts) — the pyramid literally, move-msm3-composable-library.sh
description:
Second fast-follow to bd-4fb6d0 (render-with-quotes) exploring the persistent-
library capability. DISCOVERY (verified live): a persisted part can REFERENCE
OTHER persisted parts (`$other` inside its body), resolved at APPLY-time, across
SEPARATE programs. So the library composes RECURSIVELY — you grow it bottom-up
(base parts, then parts built on those, then a top part composing the stack), and
calling the top part unfolds the whole pyramid. This is the fullest realization of
Harry's "build up a pyramid of thought … what you learn one round informs a more
complex example next round."

Verified properties (all live, det + llm):
- Parts reference parts: `label={('['++$0++']')}; card={('title '++($label%$0))};
  $card%'hi'` (separate program) → "title [hi]".
- Arbitrary chain depth: a→b→c composes correctly cross-program.
- ORDER-INDEPENDENT: define a user BEFORE the part it calls — apply-time
  resolution means order doesn't matter (`$outer%'ok'` → "<*ok*>" though inner was
  persisted after outer).
- Generative compose: `entry` composing generative `$def` → live definition.

Ships examples/move-msm3-composable-library.sh: builds a 3-level pyramid
(label→card→banner) in a persistent --context-file library across separate
programs, then calls the top part to unfold the whole stack; plus the
order-independence + generative-compose demos. det rounds CI-green offline; llm
gated on key. Distinct from move-msm3-persistent-parts.sh (basic persistence +
parts-as-arguments) — this is parts REFERENCING parts (recursive library).
Verified: live run green (3-level unfold, generative compose), offline exit 0,
verify-showcase --det-only green.
creator: ms-mac:nlir:ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-3

title: nlir dual-game: golf-aur2-treble.sh (20×3=60) + target-aur2-95-staredecisis.sh (:simplify law→plain)
description:
nlir dual-game tick (aur-2). Two examples:

FORWARD golf-aur2-treble.sh — "treble twenty": 'twenty'*'three' = 20*3 = 60. In darts the treble ring triples a segment and 20 is the highest segment, so treble twenty = 60, the max a single dart can score. Coercion reads both worded numbers and multiplies. Verified -> 60.

TARGET target-aur2-95-staredecisis.sh — reverse game via :simplify LEGAL jargon (stare decisis) -> plain (aur-2 : lane), ~even. TARGET (~175c) plain stare-decisis. This helps keep things fair and the same for everyone." Closeness: same idea, plain (high), ~even; renders "precedent" as "how courts decided similar cases before".

Both verified via claude-sonnet-5.

title: nlir-wasm P0 follow-up: step_async — async step-trace entry for the wasm step view
description:
Epic: bd-360d0c (nlir-wasm). Follow-up to P0 (bd-bec201). P2's workspace step view (aur-1) + P1's wasm `step()` export (aur-0) need an async counterpart to `eval::step_trace` that awaits the injected Realiser at effectful reduce steps.

Add `pub async fn step_async<R: Realiser>(expr, &Config, &mut Context, mode, &R) -> Result<Vec<String>, EvalError>` mirroring `step_trace` (the render_step reduction trace) but async + serial: an async `reduce_async`/`step_once_async` that awaits `realiser.llm`/`.command` at the all-operands-value Apply (via the existing `eval_apply_async`), pure reduce steps reuse sync `reduce`/`eval`. Det-mode step never awaits (key-free path). Returns Vec<String> mapping 1:1 to the JS contract `step(...) -> {steps:[{expr}]}`.

Acceptance: native behaviour + tests unchanged; step_async matches step_trace in det mode; clippy -D + fmt + nlir test green. Lane: eval (msm-0).

title: nlir power-move: move-aur2-empathetic.sh (:&[validate,agree,reframe,fix]; leading op = tone knob : vs @)
description:
nlir power-moves tick (aur-2, @&[...] composer lane). MOVE #4 — "the empathetic redirect": :&[VALIDATE, AGREE_PAIN, REFRAME_ROOT_CAUSE, THE_FIX]. The four beats of a de-escalation woven into one message. KEY LESSON: the LEADING OP is a TONE KNOB — : reads WARM/human (right for empathy), @ reads FORMAL/official (right for a policy note); SAME slots, different tone.

Filled (:) -> "The team is upset because the program keeps breaking when it comes out, and they're right to feel that way — it's been really annoying. But the real reason it breaks isn't the tools. It's that tests have been skipped. So starting Friday, every change will be tested first before it's added."
Same slots with @ -> "We recognize the team's frustration with the recurring deployment failures, and that frustration is warranted—... the root cause lies in skipped tests rather than in the tooling itself. Accordingly, beginning Friday, the test suite will be run on every merge before it can proceed."

BOTH real claude-sonnet-5 executions (the .sh runs nlir live). Demonstrates the register/tone knob on a real communication intent. examples/move-aur2-empathetic.sh + append to nlir-power-moves. Holding PNG card regen per aur-1's ligature-fix sweep.

title: nlir dual-game: golf-aur2-halflife.sh (100×0.5³=12.5, exact) + target-aur2-84-octave.sh (:simplify music→plain)
description:
nlir dual-game tick (aur-2). Two examples:

FORWARD golf-aur2-halflife.sh — "the half-life": 'a hundred'*('1'/'2')**'three' = 100*(0.5^3) = 100*0.125 = 12.5. Each half-life halves what remains; after three, (1/2)^3 = 1/8, so 100 units decay to 12.5. Precedence cubes the half first, then scales. EXACTLY 12.5 (unlike compound's ...0002) because 0.5 (a power of two) IS exact in binary while 1.1 is not — which fractions stay clean is a property of base two. Verified -> 12.5.

TARGET target-aur2-84-octave.sh — reverse game via :simplify MUSIC-THEORY jargon (an octave) -> plain (aur-2 : lane), 21% shorter. TARGET (~188c) plain octave. EXPR (149c) :'an octave is the interval between one musical pitch and another with double its frequency which the ear perceives as the same note higher or lower' -> "An octave is when a musical note plays twice as fast and sounds higher, or half as fast and sounds lower — but your ears hear it as the same note, just higher or lower up." Closeness: same idea (double the frequency -> same note higher; ear hears it as the same note), plain (high), 21% shorter.

Both verified via claude-sonnet-5.

title: nlir showcase: cross-program persistent parts library (the compounding pyramid) — move-msm3-persistent-parts.sh
description:
Fast-follow to bd-4fb6d0 (render-with-quotes, landed bc863e1). Now that forms
round-trip through context with their string literals intact, a named PART can
be persisted to a context-file LIBRARY and reused across SEPARATE nlir programs
— the literal mechanism behind Harry's compounding "pyramid of thought" ("what
you learn in one round informs a more complex example next round … build up a
pyramid").

Ships `examples/move-msm3-persistent-parts.sh`: each nlir call is a standalone
process sharing only a `--context-file` library, proving the parts persist
ACROSS programs, not within one:
- ROUND 1 (det): define `greet={('Hello, '++$0++'!')}` (prose literals) → persist.
- ROUND 2 (det, separate program): `$greet%'Ada'` → "Hello, Ada!".
- ROUND 3 (det): add `shout`, then compose both persisted parts → "Hello, Ada!: !!!".
- ROUND 4 (llm, skipped without key): a GENERATIVE part `def={=>('a one-line
  plain-English definition of: '++$0)}` persisted once, reused across programs to
  define "entropy" then "a monad" — only persistable after bd-4fb6d0.

The prose-literal parts are exactly what bd-4fb6d0 unblocked (before, `'Hello, '`
lost its quotes on persist and corrupted the stored part). det rounds run OFFLINE
(CI-green, no model); llm rounds gate on LITELLM_MASTER_KEY. Verified: live run
green (entropy/monad defined), offline run exit 0 with det rounds producing the
right output, verify-showcase --det-only still green.

Distinct from msm-0's in-program pyramid showcase (move-msm0-pyramid.sh): this is
CROSS-PROGRAM persistence — the growing shared library that compounds.
creator: ms-mac:nlir:ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-3

title: nlir power-move: move-aur2-review.sh (@&[good,gap,fix,verdict] = review verdict; flag gaps plainly not with !)
description:
nlir power-moves tick (aur-2, @&[...] composer lane). MOVE #6 — "the review verdict": @&[WHATS_GOOD, THE_ONE_GAP, THE_SPECIFIC_FIX, THE_VERDICT]. Review an agent's/teammate's work in one line: praise what's solid, name the gap plainly, give the fix, state the call (approve-after/request-changes).

Filled: @&['the caching layer design is solid and well-scoped','the one gap is that cache invalidation is missing on user updates','add an invalidation hook on the user-update path','approve once that hook is in'] -> "The caching layer design is solid and well-scoped. The one gap is that cache invalidation is missing on user updates. Please add an invalidation hook on the user-update path; approval will follow once that hook is in place."

TWO FINDINGS documented: (1) flag a GAP PLAINLY, do NOT ! it — !'the missing X' negates to "X is NOT missing", contradicting the fix slot + tripping the composer's non-deterministic inconsistency flag; ! is for REJECTING a claim (partial-accept), not naming a gap. (2) @[a,b,c] without & WEAVES into one message like @&[...] (list spreads + gets woven/formalized; no map-over-list — that needs the proposed ↦).

Real claude-sonnet-5 execution. examples/move-aur2-review.sh + append nlir-power-moves.

title: config: env-interp clobbers bare $vars in command: bash blocks
description:
Config env-interpolation (bd-b342fd) expands a BARE `$name` inside `command:` bash blocks, clobbering shell variables that collide with a SET env var. Concretely: aur-1's `@`/echo command uses bash `$out`; the nix dev-shell exports `out` (nix build output path), so config interp rewrites `$out` -> that path and the command breaks. Passes in clean CI where `out` is unset, so it is an environment-dependent flake (surfaced via eval::tests::command_realisation_runs_under_bash).

Options to consider (SPEC decision needed): (a) config interp only expands `${VAR}` braces, not bare `$name` (but SPEC line ~330 uses bare `$ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`); (b) skip interpolation entirely for `command:` values and let bash do its own expansion; (c) only expand names that are explicitly config-referenced, leaving unknown `$name` literal; (d) require `$$` escaping in command blocks. Recommend (b): do not env-interpolate `command:` blocks. Owner: config lane (msm-0). Low urgency (env-specific) but a real footgun for anyone whose shell exports a colliding var.
creator: ms-mac:nlir:ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-0

Child scope (label `parallelism`): DAG scheduler (independent nodes concurrent, `--parallelism` default 8); context-write serialization safety (`=`); backtick forced-serial subtree (whole RHS, sibling-parallel); subcall dedupe/cache keyed on `(op, mode, model, operand-texts)` under `_cache`.

title: nlir help: config-derived operator reference (rich CLI) — list every operator + summary

SCOPE: a new main.rs subcommand (e.g. `nlir operators` / `nlir help`) that reads config.operators (config.rs already parses the operators: block into BTreeMap<String, OperatorConfig> — fields: op symbol, arity, fixity, priority, operands/result TypeName, template [det], reduce [numeric], join [mixfix], prompt) and prints, for EVERY configured operator: name, symbol, arity, fixity, priority, and a concise SUMMARY derived from its template/prompt/reduce. Rich CLI output (aligned table / grouped by prefix/infix/postfix/mixfix). Self-contained: NO eval/parser/lexer changes — pure config-read + formatting = aur-2 config/CLI lane (I own config.example.yaml + config.rs additions + main.rs).

DIVVY (broadcast): aur-2 owns this; potential pairing with aur-0 on the per-op human summaries (their plain-language strength). Feature #1 (REPL tab-to-expand-next-eval-step) = msm-0.

title: crates/nlir-wasm/Cargo.lock records stale nlir 0.1.1 (vs 0.1.4) — bumps on every wasm check
description:
Friction (reflect-session, from bd-27739b PR1): crates/nlir-wasm/Cargo.lock is
stale — it records `name = "nlir" version = "0.1.1"` while the nlir crate is at
0.1.4. So ANY `cargo check`/build in crates/nlir-wasm bumps the lockfile
(0.1.1 -> 0.1.4) as incidental churn, which every agent then has to notice and
revert to keep an unrelated PR focused (and it's a latent merge-conflict source
in the wasm-core area).

Fix: sync the wasm lockfile once (`cargo update -p nlir` inside crates/nlir-wasm,
or regenerate) and commit, so it tracks the current nlir version. Low priority /
hygiene. Filed via reflect-session from the bd-27739b PR1 session (aur-2). Owner

title: Fix red test-card part-named-form-reuse (map yields newline-sep, card expected space-sep)
description:
Regression on main from ac9a560 (bd-232048 L3 pyramid showcase, force-skip-gate merged): the config test-card `part-named-form-reuse` asserts `sq={$0*$0};$sq↦[1,2,3,4]` → "1 4 9 16" (space-separated), but a map yields a LIST which the test harness renders newline-separated ("1
4
9
16") → the card FAILED (`nlir test` = 133 passed, 1 failed). (The CLI `-e --quiet` summary inlines the list with spaces, which is why it looked fine interactively — the classic lists-print-one-per-line display trap.)

Caught by aur-0's full `cargo test`/`nlir test` sweep (the config suite, which force-skip-gate bypassed). Fix: prepend `_sep=" "` to the card expr so it actually produces the space-separated "1 4 9 16" the card asserts. Verified: `nlir test` → 134 passed, 0 failed; cargo test --all 294+34 green; verify-spec-ops OK; fmt 0. One-line change to config.example.yaml.

title: nlir dual-game: golf-aur2-ceiling.sh (2^53 precision ceiling) + target-aur2-58-fibonacci.sh (:simplify math→plain)
description:
nlir dual-game tick (aur-2). Two examples:

FORWARD golf-aur2-ceiling.sh — "the precision ceiling" (a types-lane numeric-limit demo, honest companion to the googol, cashing aur-0's f64 insight): 2**52+1 = 4503599627370497 (exact, odd) but 2**53+1 = 9007199254740992 == 2**53 (the +1 is LOST). nlir numbers are f64, so integers are bit-exact only up to 2^53; at 2^53 the gap between consecutive f64s becomes 2, so 2^53+1 rounds back down. DET, offline. Complements typeguard (error demo) + msm-0's #45 numeric edges. Verified.

TARGET target-aur2-58-fibonacci.sh — reverse game via :simplify MATH jargon (Fibonacci sequence) -> plain (aur-2 : lane), 22% shorter. TARGET (~161c) plain Fibonacci. EXPR (125c) :'the Fibonacci sequence is a series of numbers where each number is the sum of the two before it starting from zero and one' -> "The Fibonacci sequence is a list of numbers. You start with 0 and 1. Then, to get the next number, you just add the last two numbers together. You keep doing that to make the whole list." Closeness: same rule, plain (high), 22% shorter.

Both verified (forward DET; target claude).

title: [build] Linux x86 nix dev-shell coreutils segfaulting
description:
Reported by Harry (2026-07-03): on Linux x86, coreutils tools segfault inside the nlir `nix develop` dev shell, blocking `nix develop --command cargo build` for the Aurora agents.

Context / likely cause:
- nlir's flake pins nixpkgs to collective's exact rev (flakehub tarball rev 0590cd39) per operator request, to reuse the cached toolchain.
- The dev shell is only required on macOS to supply `libiconv` for the `-liconv` link (ureq/rustls TLS stack via feedback-cli/updatable-cli). On Linux, glibc already provides iconv.

WORKAROUND for Linux x86 (unblocks Aurora agents now):
- Build WITHOUT the dev shell: plain `cargo build` with a system Rust toolchain should link fine on Linux (no `-liconv` needed). Then `cargo fmt`/`clippy`/`test` directly.
- If a system Rust toolchain is unavailable, the nix rustc/cargo binaries themselves are fine; only coreutils in the interactive shell env is segfaulting — invoke tools by absolute path or use `nix build .#nlir` for a one-shot binary.

Diagnostics needed (from a Linux x86 node):
1. Is this nlir-specific or do OTHER cacophony project dev shells (omni-cli, tendril, collective) also segfault coreutils on the same node? If all projects, it is environmental (node/store), not the nlir flake.
2. Which exact coreutils tool + command segfaults (e.g. `cp`, `mkdir`, `cat`)? `dmesg`/exit signal?
3. Does `nix store verify --repair /nix/store/...coreutils...` or re-fetch fix it (partial/corrupt download)?
4. Does omni-cli (which pins github nixos-unstable, a NEWER nixpkgs) work on the same node? If yes, the pinned rev 0590cd39's coreutils is the culprit on that hardware and we may need a devShell nixpkgs bump for Linux.

Filed from the nlir config-vertical session (msm-0). Not claimed: needs a Linux x86 repro to fix.

MITIGATION LANDED (Harry, c08b7a9): removed `use flake` from `.envrc` so direnv no longer auto-loads the nix dev shell on `cd`. Practical build workflow now:
- Linux x86: build with the SYSTEM Rust toolchain — plain `cargo build` / `cargo test` / `cargo clippy` / `cargo fmt`. No dev shell needed (glibc provides iconv), which sidesteps the segfaulting dev-shell coreutils.
- macOS: still needs `libiconv`, so build via an explicit `nix develop --command cargo build` (the dev shell is fine on macOS; only Linux x86 coreutils segfaults).
Bead stays OPEN as a tracked known-issue: the underlying reason the pinned-rev dev-shell coreutils segfaults on Linux x86 is still undiagnosed and could resurface for `nix build`/CI. Diagnostics in the original description still wanted.

DIAGNOSTICS (aurora Linux x86_64, NixOS 6.18.32, nix 2.34.7; 2026-07-03, aur-2):

NOT REPRODUCIBLE on aurora now. Findings against the original questions:

1. nlir-specific vs environmental: NEITHER segfaults currently. nlir dev shell
   coreutils-9.8 (/nix/store/wkkwxc04gdw6b263l1h29pjarjnjdyb6-coreutils-9.8) AND
   omni-cli dev shell coreutils-9.11
   (/nix/store/9ypz3flqsrl5xl495mm8h645gadjsxi1-coreutils-9.11) both run
   cp/mkdir/cat/ls/mv/rm with exit=0.
2. Which tool segfaults: NONE currently -- cp/mkdir/cat/ls/mv/rm all exit=0 in
   the nlir dev shell.
3. store verify/repair: "nix store verify --no-trust <coreutils-9.8 path>"
   returns clean (exit 0) -- the path is intact, NOT corrupt now.
4. omni-cli (newer nixpkgs): works (coreutils-9.11). AND nlir's own pinned-rev
   coreutils-9.8 works too.

Also verified the original failing command path: "nix develop path:<nlir>
--command" runs cargo 1.91.0 + rustc 1.91.1 fine (TOOLCHAIN-OK).

CONCLUSION: The original segfault was almost certainly TRANSIENT store corruption
(a partial/corrupt download of the coreutils-9.8 store path) at report time,
since re-fetched/repaired -- the coreutils-9.8 path now verifies clean and works.
The pinned rev 0590cd39 is EXONERATED (a clean coreutils-9.8 works; no devShell
nixpkgs bump needed). The system-toolchain mitigation (c08b7a9) is the durable
Linux workflow and CI is green on it. Recommend resolving; reopen if the segfault
recurs (transient corruption can recur on a fresh partial download on any node).
creator: ms-mac:nlir:ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-0

title: WASM LLM: default model routed to command realiser (absent in browser) — point medium tier at anthropic_messages
description:
Harry blocked: can't use a real LLM in the WASM playground ("no llm realiser set / doesn't save the field"). Two-layer root cause (I traced it): (1) proximate = stale-localStorage undefined settings → load() crash [msm-0 fixed @7861306, deep-merge]; (2) DEEPER + my half = the workspace's default model routes to realiser.command, absent in-browser.

MY FIX (config lane, confirmed by aur-0/msm-0): the ops' default tier `medium` was `sonnet` (type: command → routes to realiser.command, which the browser never supplies → fails even with a saved base-url). Only type: anthropic_messages routes to realiser.llm (the Settings base-url path). Changed `defaults.model.medium: sonnet` → `medium: direct` (also claude-sonnet-5, but type: anthropic_messages) so all 12 llm ops realise over HTTP → route to realiser.llm in-browser. Same model, HTTP transport instead of the claude CLI; native CLI users can `--model sonnet`. aur-0 confirmed in-browser base_url/api_key are ignored (Settings base-url wins; sends model.model as the id).

Gate: example_config validates (tier ref resolves), nlir test 18/18 (det unaffected). P7 pages deploy confirmed tracking HEAD (triggers on config.example.yaml + site/workspace/** + src/**), so this + the load() fix auto-redeploy to a.skh.am/nlir/workspace.

Follow-up (flagged, not mine to land): model-id matching — the browser sends model.model ("claude-sonnet-5") to the user's endpoint, so a Settings model-picker field (aur-1 UI) or a proper openai_chat ModelKind would let users match arbitrary OpenAI-compatible endpoints.

title: `det:`-field ops (`~>`) skip det realisation when nested under a builtin-op (`&`/`∧`) — mutual judge not det-testable
description:
Found while shipping bd-f1ee6f (∧/∨ operators). A det-realised operator that uses a `det:` FIELD (not a template) — notably `~>` (`det: {$contains%($0,$1)}`) — does NOT get its det realisation when it appears as an OPERAND nested under a builtin-delegated operator (`&` compose, `∧`/`∨` bool, `↦`/`⊘`, set ops). In det mode it errors: "operator `~>` has no deterministic (template/join/reduce) realisation".

Repro (det mode): `('the sky is blue'~>'sky')∧('sky'~>'the sky is blue')` → ERROR (both `~>` operands fail), even though `'x'~>'y'` alone det-evaluates fine via its `det:` field, and `∧` works fine on plain bools.

IMPACT: the nlir-as-judge MUTUAL gate `(sol~>T)∧(T~>sol)` (and the older `&` form) works in LLM mode but CANNOT be det-tested — the det stub isn't applied for the nested `~>`. Pre-existing (the bd-f1ee6f bead already noted "errors under det when nested under &"); ∧ inherits it. NOT a ∧ bug.

LIKELY CAUSE (eval lane): the `det:`-field realisation (Mode::Det path, ~eval.rs 1284) is applied when an op is evaluated in the operator-eval path, but when the op is an operand pre-evaluated for a builtin-delegated parent, the det: field isn't consulted / the operand goes through a path that doesn't apply it. Fix would make det-mode composition of det:-field ops work (so the mutual judge is det-testable).

Priority: low — LLM mode (the judge's actual use) works; this only blocks det-mode testing of `~>`-nested compositions. Filing for tracking. Eval lane (msm-0/msm-3/aur-1).
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-1
spoken_name: det field ops skip nested builtin

description:
Friction from bd-9d2d46 session (ms-mac): running `cargo test --lib` directly with the system toolchain (~/.cargo/bin/cargo) fails at link time with `ld: library not found for -liconv` / `collect2: error: ld returned 1 exit status`. `cargo check` and `cargo clippy` pass (no final link), so the failure only shows up when a test/bin binary is linked, which is a confusing signal. The tests pass cleanly inside `nix develop` (which provides libiconv on the linker path) — 239 lib tests green there.

scripts/preflight.sh is already designed to run via `nix run .#preflight` / `nix develop --command`, but a worker who reaches for a bare `cargo test` gets a cryptic linker error with no hint pointing them at the dev shell.

Fix-shape (pick one, low effort): (a) have scripts/preflight.sh detect it is not inside the nix dev shell and print a one-line "run via `nix run .#preflight` or `nix develop`" hint; and/or (b) add a short note in README/onboarding that Rust build+test on ms-mac must run inside the nix dev shell because the system toolchain lacks libiconv. Not a code bug — a toolchain/onboarding papercut that costs a confused link-error round-trip.

Filed via reflect-session mixin from bd-9d2d46 session.

title: Nested LLM operand in a & join leaks <text> wrapper tags (&['love',!'love'] => "love and <text>hate</text>")
description:
Dogfood bug (aur-2, found during golf): a nested LLM operator inside a `&`/list operand leaks `<text>...</text>` wrapper tags into the joined output.

Repro (--mode llm):
  &['love',!'love']  => "love and <text>hate</text>"   (expected: "love and hate")
  &['war',!'war']    => "war and <text>peace</text>"    (expected: "war and peace")

The inner !'love' correctly negates to the antonym "hate", but its LLM result comes back wrapped in <text>...</text> markup, and the & join splices that wrapper verbatim into the output instead of stripping it to the bare text. So any expression that JOINS an LLM-produced operand (e.g. &[literal, !x] or &[x, ~y]) can leak <text> tags.

Likely eval/realise lane (the realised operand isn't unwrapped before it feeds the next operator's prompt/join) — NOT config/types/llm (aur-2) or ranges (msm-0). Flagging for whoever owns the realise seam (adjacent to aur-1's <group> paren-echo work + msm-0's operand-realisation). Not filing into a specific lane; P3, cosmetic-but-real (blocks composing LLM ops through &/lists cleanly). Repro'd on origin/main @7b77a0d.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-2
spoken_name: nested llm operand wrapper tag leak

title: Harden parser fuzz corpus to cover quote-eval {…} form-quote + % apply paths
description:
The parser fuzz safety test `fuzz_tokenize_and_parse_never_panic` (src/parser.rs) guards the lexer/parser against panics/stack-overflow on adversarial input. After the quote-eval work landed the new `{…}` form-quote and `%` form-apply recursive parse paths (bd-5dd86f), the fuzz corpus did NOT exercise them: the random alphabet was `b"abc()[]&|+-*!?;=$^` ... `#% ..."` — it had `%` but NOT `{`/`}`, and there was no deeply-nested brace corpus entry. So the new recursion paths (`{…}` nud → expr(0); `%` led → expr) were unfuzzed — a coverage gap in a safety test right after major new syntax landed.

Both new paths already recurse through the depth-guarded `self.expr()` (MAX_PARSE_DEPTH=96), so there is no live overflow; this is preventive hardening + regression protection, and closes the gap the release gate's `cargo test --all` relies on (aur-2 flagged parser-fuzz robustness around the release cut, bd-d2437c).

Change (test-only, src/parser.rs test module):
- add `{` `}` to the fuzz random alphabet so generated inputs include braces.
- add adversarial/degenerate corpus entries: `{{{{{{`, `}}}}}}`, `{a%b}`, `{}%`, `{%}`, and deep-nesting `"{".repeat(500)`, `"{$0+".repeat(500)`, `"a%".repeat(500)` — these probe the `{…}`/`%` recursion to MAX_PARSE_DEPTH (must error, not overflow) and unbalanced/degenerate forms (must not panic).

Validated: fuzz test green with the extended corpus (depth guard confirmed to cover the new paths), fmt + clippy -D warnings clean, 246 lib tests pass.

title: nlir coding-pipe move: the code-review comment (@&[~$_stdin, POINTS]) — #37
description:
nlir power-moves tick (aur-2) — FRESH coding-pipe move #37 on a structurally-distinct angle (a COMPOSE, not a distillation). "the code-review comment": weave a summary of piped code + YOUR review points into one polished review comment.

SHAPE (as a PIPE): `<code> | nlir -e '@&[~$_stdin, YOUR_POINT_1, YOUR_POINT_2]'`. $_stdin = the piped code; ~$_stdin = a summary of it; @&[...] = formalize + WEAVE. The reviewer supplies the judgment (their points); nlir folds in a summary of what the code does and weaves it all into one professional comment. This is the OPPOSITE hand from aur-0's review-pipe (which auto-DIAGNOSES): a "help me write the review" tool, not a "find the bugs" tool. It's my @&[...] COMPOSE signature meeting the smart pipe — no overlap with the distiller's diff-distillation cell.

Real (claude-sonnet-5, a config-loader with a bare except piped in): -> "The `load_config` function reads a JSON file and returns its contents; however, in the event of any error, it returns an empty dictionary rather than surfacing the issue. This design is problematic: the bare `except` clause silently suppresses legitimate errors, such as a missing file, and the file handle is never properly closed."

Files (mine only): examples/move-aur2-codereview.sh (SHAPE+pipe+filled+real output; pipes a sample snippet into nlir live; exit 0) + examples/POWERMOVES.md (quick-ref row). No build-showcase.py card — @aur-0 curates the coding-pipe card set; flagging this composer-meets-pipe for them to card if it earns a slot (it's a COMPOSE, distinct from their 4 single-transform pipe cards).

title: LLM-mode type coercion unimplemented: eval coerces operands deterministically only (types.model/prompt unused)
description:
Driving nlir in llm mode against a real Claude backend surfaced that LLM-mode type coercion is UNIMPLEMENTED. `nlir -e "'five' + 'three'"` (llm mode; `+` has operands: number) fails with `cannot coerce string \`five\` to number: no deterministic or model coercion produced a number`.

Root cause: `Evaluator::eval_apply` (src/eval.rs ~341-345 and the concurrent path ~612-614) coerces operands with `value.coerce(op_cfg.operands, &sep)` — DETERMINISTIC-only. The SPEC §Types llm fallback (config `types: number: { model, prompt, schema }`) is never invoked, so a non-numeric string can never be coerced via the model even in llm mode.

Fix: add an evaluator coercion that, when deterministic coercion fails AND mode==Llm AND config.types has the target type's model+prompt, calls the model (llm::realise_llm with the type's prompt over the rendered value) and parses the answer to the target type. String target still stringifies deterministically. Found by msm-0 while building the llm-mode working-cases library.

title: showcase: `self-judge` capstone card — nlir grades nlir (the golf judge is itself nlir)
description:
Harry's "the judge is nlir" (2026-07-07) + aur-1's explicit unblock ("@msm-2 — you're unblocked to card the nlir-grades-nlir shape"). The golf judge is ITSELF an nlir expression — the sharpest dogfood in the "why nlir" set and its capstone (the self-judge/meta category, completing access·pipe·decision·generation·self-judge).

Canonical judge form (aur-1, 3-agent-validated msm-2/msm-0/aur-0):
- GATE (pass/fail): `$if%((sol)~>T, T~>(sol), 'false')` — mutual implication; blocks a too-vague output from cheating one-directionally. `&` is compose (string) not boolean-AND, so `$if` is the clean gate.
- RANK (tie-break): `=>'return only an integer 0-100 rating how closely A matches B…'`.
- Referee hardening (aur-0/msm-1): Winner = gate TRUE AND output bytes ≤ ~1.15× target (brevity guard defeats commentary-padding, which the nondeterministic semantic gate can't catch alone).

Deliverable (msm-2 site-card lane): build-showcase.py SIMPLE card `self-judge` — `(=>'first line of 1984')~>'It was a bright cold day in April, and the clocks were striking thirteen'` → true (live-verified by msm-2). pill "llm · nlir grades nlir", caption teaches `=>` regenerates + mutual `~>` scores + honesty gate. Render PNG; add to the README "Why nlir, not a prompt?" block as the capstone. Credits Harry (idea), aur-1 (design/unblock), aur-0 (referee), msm-0 ($if gate).

title: det-mode echo of a multi-message range shows only the head element (0^*-1 -> first msg, not the span)
description:
DOGFOOD OBSERVATION (msm0), UX/display question — NOT a correctness bug, operators are unaffected.

FINDING: In det mode, echoing a multi-message range renders ONLY the head element, not the span.
  Context = 5 messages [picnic-plan, ..., db-incident-resolved].
  det:  0^*-1   => "Let's plan the company summer picnic."   (only msg 0)
        0^*2    => msg 0 only;  2^*4 => msg 2 only
  llm:  ~0^*-1  => "The team pivoted from planning a picnic to resolving a production DB emergency..."
        (correctly reflects ALL 5 messages)

So the RANGE itself is correct — LLM operators (~, #, etc.) receive the FULL span. This is confirmed:
#40 (selection), #50 (mission-control) are correct; they feed 0^*-1 to operators, which see everything.
It is ONLY the det-mode ECHO (the no-LLM passthrough render) that collapses a multi-message range to its
head element.

QUESTION for the realise/det-render lane (aur-1): is head-only the intended det echo of a multi-message
range, or should it join the messages (with a separator, like lists use _sep)? Currently `_sep=,;0^*2`
errors (parse), so there's no obvious way to see the whole range deterministically. A user doing `0^*-1`
in det mode to "see the whole conversation" gets only the first line silently, which is surprising.

OPTIONS: (a) intended — document that det echo of a range = its head (address resolves to start); (b) join
range messages in det echo using the list separator policy; (c) leave as-is but document. Low priority,
cosmetic — filed as a draft so the realise-lane owner can make the SPEC call, not rushed. Repro'd on
config.example.yaml @ current main.
creator: ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-0

title: nlir-wasm: wasm-serial scheduler fallback (cfg-gate thread::scope eval_operands_parallel)
description:
nlir-wasm epic bd-360d0c, P0-adjacent (found + owned by aur-0, blessed by msm-0). The execution-graph scheduler `eval_operands_parallel` (eval.rs, bd-780dbf) uses std::thread::scope for concurrent operand-subtree eval — orthogonal to msm-0's realiser seam (P0) and NOT covered by it. wasm32-unknown-unknown has no threads, and this path is in the DET eval path (parallelises regardless of det/llm), so det-mode wasm couldn't compile/run without a serial fallback.

Fix (msm-0's suggested shape, minimal collision with the seam): cfg the native threaded fn `#[cfg(not(target_arch = "wasm32"))]` (body unchanged) + a `#[cfg(target_arch = "wasm32")]` serial twin with the same signature (serial in operand order via eval_parallel_safe; `parallelism` accepted for parity, unused). Caller (eval_apply) untouched → target-agnostic. Guarantees thread::scope is never compiled on wasm.

Native-verified green (build, clippy -D, fmt, 214+3 tests, nlir 18/18; 2+3*4+5=19 threaded path unchanged). wasm compile verified in CI/devshell (aurora is authoring-only). Unblocks P1 (bd-3fcf96) wasm det-mode compile.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-0

title: nlir dual-game: golf-aur2-byte.sh (2⁸=256) + target-aur2-74-lightyear.sh (:simplify astronomy→plain)
description:
nlir dual-game tick (aur-2). Two examples:

FORWARD golf-aur2-byte.sh — "a byte": 'two'**'eight' = 2^8 = 256. A byte is 8 bits, each 0 or 1, so it holds 2^8 = 256 distinct values (0..255). Coercion reads the words, the power operator counts the combinations. The atom of computing. Verified -> 256.

TARGET target-aur2-74-lightyear.sh — reverse game via :simplify ASTRONOMY jargon (a light-year) -> plain (aur-2 : lane), 25% shorter. TARGET (~188c) plain light-year (a distance not a time). EXPR (141c) :'a light year is the distance that light travels in one year through the vacuum of space which is about nine point five trillion kilometers' -> "A light year is how far light can go in one whole year when it's zooming through empty space... about nine and a half trillion kilometers — that's a REALLY big number..." title: nlir-golf: golf-aur2-hierarch.sh — hierarchical theme-of-themes (tree-recursive ~&[~&[#,#],~&[#,#]])
description:
nlir-golf loop (aur-2 entry #3). examples/golf-aur2-hierarch.sh — "theme of themes": hierarchical / tree-recursive synthesis. Nests the corpus-synthesis operator inside itself: ~&[~&[#a1,#a2],~&[#b1,#b2]] — a depth-4 tree. Each inner ~&[#,#] finds a cluster's theme (subject->join->summary); the outer ~&[…] joins the two cluster-themes and summarises the META-theme. 10 operator sigils (~x3 &x3 #x4), ~10 model calls, 4 concurrent leaf subject-extractions.

Verified end-to-end (claude-sonnet-5): cluster A = swarm biology (bees, ants), cluster B = human aggregation (prediction markets, Wikipedia) -> "Honeybees, ants, prediction markets, and Wikipedia all achieve effective outcomes through decentralized collective processes rather than centralized control." nlir surfaced "emergence / collective intelligence" — a concept in NEITHER cluster's text — purely from the tree of transforms. Extends my flat #1 (corpus theme) to genuine tree-recursion; distinct from aur-1 (cognition/stack), msm-0 (conversation range/assign-reason).
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-2

title: CI guard: assert SPEC operator-table op set ≡ `nlir help` / config (prevent doc drift)
description:
Observed while completing bd-2e59bb (SPEC operator-table completion, landed 4746181). The SPEC.md operator table had silently drifted incomplete — it documented only 18 of ~30 config operators (missing diff, implication, concat, split, comparisons, and the ↦/⊘ glyphs) even though the phrasebook + SPEC advertise that "SPEC, nlir help, and the config all derive from the same config, so they stay in sync." The invariant was NOT actually enforced, so drift accumulated as operators were added.

Gap: there is no CI/test guard asserting the SPEC operator-table op set equals the `nlir help` / config operator set. So the next operator addition can again land in config + help (auto-derived) without a SPEC row, and nothing catches it until a manual audit.

Fix-shape (deterministic, CI-gateable):
- A test (or scripts/verify-*.py addition) that parses the operator sigils from SPEC.md's operator tables and asserts the set == the config operator sigils (== `nlir help`). Fail if SPEC is missing an op (or has a stale/extra one).
- Could also assert the SPEC row's `name` matches the config key (would have caught the `..` semantic-access vs access-semantic drift too).

This closes the drift class that bd-2e59bb had to fix manually. Grammar/SPEC + tooling lane. Filed via reflect-session from the bd-2e59bb session.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-1

title: Parser: backtick serial-marker
description: Parent epic bd-ab25f7 (label `parser`). Low-precedence prefix capturing whole RHS as a forced-serial subtree.

title: nlir-wasm P0: realiser seam — injectable async LLM+command realisation (native unchanged)
description:
Epic: bd-360d0c (nlir-wasm). Design: docs/design/nlir-wasm.md section 3.1.

THE KEYSTONE DEPENDENCY. Abstract the effectful realisation (llm model backend + command: subprocess) behind an injectable async trait so the host supplies it — native = the existing llm.rs HTTP backend + bash subprocess (behaviour UNCHANGED), WASM = JS callbacks later. Today eval::realise_op calls crate::llm::realise_llm / realise_command directly; route those through a Realiser trait instead (llm(model,prompt) and command(cmd,args), each returning a future/Result<String>).

Also: make the eval path async-capable (fetch is async; WASM has no threads) — expose an async eval entry that runs the DAG serially / with async concurrency, while the native CLI keeps its thread::scope parallelism (the seam is at REALISATION, not scheduling). Deterministic realise.rs (reduce/template/join) stays pure/unchanged. Acceptance: native binary behaviour + tests unchanged; realiser is injectable; an async eval entry exists. Lane: eval (msm-0 or eval owner). CI gate: fmt+clippy -D+test --all + nlir test.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-1

title: Installed nlir (just sync-install) links libiconv from a GC-able nix-store path — can break after nix-collect-garbage
description:
Operator reported `~/.local/bin/nlir` segfaulting (2026-07-05 ~20:07 local). Investigation: NOT a code bug — a fresh `cargo build --release` of current main runs clean (nlir -e test -> test, no-args prints help, deep-nesting errors cleanly with "expression nesting too deep", no SIGSEGV), and the operator's installed binary also works now across repeated runs. Root cause of the reported crash was almost certainly transient: the installed binary's mtime (20:07) matches when it was invoked, so the operator ran it mid-`just sync-install` (a half-built/half-copied binary).

Real latent fragility to fix (separate from the transient): `just sync-install` builds the release binary inside `nix develop` and copies it to ~/.local/bin/nlir, but the binary dynamically links libiconv from a /nix/store path:
  otool -L ~/.local/bin/nlir ->
    /nix/store/<hash>-libiconv-.../lib/libiconv.2.dylib
    /usr/lib/libSystem.B.dylib
If `nix-collect-garbage` removes that store path (nothing else references it), the installed binary breaks at load time (dyld can't resolve libiconv) — presenting as a crash/no-output, which could look like an intermittent "segfault". Options: (a) link libiconv statically / avoid the nix libiconv (use the system /usr/lib libiconv on macOS), (b) pin/gc-root the store path the installed binary needs, or (c) have `just sync-install` note the dependency / install a wrapper. Low-frequency but real portability gap for the daily-driver install. Filed from the segfault triage.
creator: ms-mac:nlir:ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-2

title: G2: step-through graph frames (stable layout across steps) — the animation source
description:
Graph-viz epic (bd-8ac9ad) slice G2. Proposed lane: msm-0 (owns step_trace/step_once) + the shared renderer. Depends G0 (bd-10cf87) + G1 (bd-2f4129).

Produce a SEQUENCE of graph frames that mirror execution, so the CLI (G4 kitty animation) and wasm (G5) can animate the computation evolving:
- Drive `eval::step_trace` / `Evaluator::step_once` (the same small-step engine as `nlir step`): at each step, one redex reduces and its subtree becomes an `Expr::Value`. Rebuild the G0 graph for the current partially-reduced Expr each step → a frame.
- STABLE LAYOUT across frames: reuse G1's stable-position mode keyed on G0 node ids so unchanged nodes stay put and only the reduced subtree visibly collapses to its Value (no jumpy re-layout). The just-reduced node highlighted.
- Reduced Values shown via G0 truncate_ends ("first few words … last few words") — model output can be large; diagrams stay consistent.
- Output = Vec<Frame> (each a Graph or an SVG via G1), plus per-frame metadata (which node reduced) for captions.

This is the animation source both backends consume. Unit test: `2**3**2` yields the expected number of frames ending in a single Value node; a binding expr keeps the Assign→ContextRead edges visible until consumed.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-2

title: nlir power-move + card: the descope proposal (@&[SQUEEZE,CUT,PROTECT,PAYOFF]) — my 32nd

SHAPE: @&[THE_SQUEEZE, WHAT_TO_CUT, WHAT_TO_PROTECT, THE_PAYOFF]. Frames a scope-cut as a plan not a failure — why it all won't fit, what to defer, what to protect (and why), the payoff (a solid on-time launch). The protect slot with its reason is what makes a cut land. Distinct from scoped-commitment (a promise) and crisp-proposal (a fix for a problem) — this is the deliberate scope↔date trade.

Real (claude-sonnet-5): @&['we cannot ship all six features by the March deadline without burning out the team','I propose we cut the analytics dashboard and the export tool to a fast-follow','and protect the core checkout flow and the mobile fixes, which is what most users actually asked for','that gets us a solid, tested launch on time with the rest landing two weeks later'] -> "Delivering all six features by the March deadline is not feasible without placing undue strain on the team. I recommend deferring the analytics dashboard and the export tool to a fast-follow release, while prioritizing the core checkout flow and the mobile fixes—the features most frequently requested by users. This approach would allow us to deliver a stable, thoroughly tested launch on schedule, with the remaining features following two weeks later."

Files (mine only): examples/move-aur2-descope.sh (SHAPE+slots+filled+real output; runs live, exit 0), examples/POWERMOVES.md (quick-ref table row + COMPOSE moves list), scripts/build-showcase.py card row + showcase/nlir-descope-proposal.png (single --only regen, honest llm pill). 32 moves + 32 cards. title: M^N message-range is unreachable: range logic exists but no AST node / parser production / eval arm
description:
Found while driving nlir in LLM mode. The SPEC documents message ranges `M^N` (e.g. `(1+1)^(5+5)` = assistant messages 2..10, joined with _sep; worked-examples table row `#0^3;#^-1;&`). The RANGE LOGIC exists — messages.rs::MessageIndex::range(role,start,end,sep) landed in bd-43ac5e (closed) with tests — but it is DEAD CODE: nothing calls it.

Repro: `nlir -e '0^2' --context-file <ctx with assistant msgs> --mode det` → `parse error at token 1: unexpected token Message(Assistant) after statement`. The lexer emits `0` (Number) then `^2` (Message{Assistant,2}); the parser has no infix `^` handling so `M^N` never forms.

parser.rs:17-19 explicitly says the `M^N` message-range infix form is DEFERRED. So three pieces are missing:
1. AST: no `Expr::MessageRange { role, start, end }` node (only `Expr::Message{role,index}`).
2. Parser: no infix `^` production — must distinguish prefix `^N` (message index) from infix `M^N` (range) per the lexer.rs:801 note "parser distinguishes prefix vs infix `^`".
3. Eval: no arm to call MessageIndex::range (needs adding to both eval() and eval_parallel_safe(); it is read-only → parallel-safe).

Lanes: AST+parser = msm-0; eval+messages range logic = aur-1 (I wrote the range fn). Proposing aur-1 takes the whole vertical (small, and I own the range logic + eval) pending msm-0 OK on touching parser.rs, to avoid a multi-round handoff. Filed by aur-1.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-1

title: [EPIC] Lexer / tokeniser
description: SPEC §Whitespace/escapes/quoting, §Grammar (Tokens). Turn shorthand text into a token stream. Child scope (label `lexer`): non-semantic whitespace stripping between tokens; bare `[a-zA-Z0-9]+` and numeric literals; quoted literals `'…'` (raw) / `"…"` (escapes processed) with POSIX escapes (`\n \t \  \\ \" \'`); operator longest-match (`**` before `*`); builtin sigils `; $ ^ = [ ] , ( )` + backtick and sub-forms (`$name`/`$N`, `^`/`^_`/`^*`/`^/`, `key=`); role-modifier and negative-index disambiguation after `^`/`$`.

title: nlir-wasm P7: CI co-build + version stamp — wasm built from the same src every push, embedded
description:
Epic: bd-360d0c. Design: docs/design/nlir-wasm.md section 3.8 (the anti-drift guarantee). Depends on P1 (bd-3fcf96, nlir-wasm crate).

CO-BUILD: a CI job (new wasm job or extend pages.yml) runs wasm-pack build from the SAME commit as the native binary on every push, emitting pkg/ into the published site. nlir-wasm::version() embeds the crate version + git sha; the widget shows it; the site build FAILS if the WASM commit != the site commit. Result: the in-page evaluator is byte-for-byte the shipped evaluator (never drifts). Also measure wasm binary size + apply wasm-opt/feature-trim if heavy (design open question). Acceptance: every main push produces a fresh embedded wasm co-built from that commit; version stamp visible + enforced. Lane: CI/release (aur-2).

title: $_stdin — pipe stdin into a reserved context key (nlir as a smart pipe)
description:
Harry directive (agentic-coding / nlir-as-a-smart-pipe direction): a convenient way to refer to stdin. Added `$_stdin` — piped stdin exposed as a reserved context key so nlir works in a pipeline: `cat foo.rs | nlir -e '~$_stdin'`, `git diff | nlir -e '#$_stdin'`, etc.

Implementation (my CLI/context lane; no config.example.yaml touch → no collision with the operators reformat / ~> change):
- src/context.rs: `set_transient(key, value)` (in-memory insert, NO write-through) + `remove(key)` (in-memory remove). set() write-throughs immediately, so _stdin must be set transiently + dropped before save so it's never persisted.
- src/main.rs: `read_stdin_into_context(&mut ctx) -> bool` — in the eval path (run_eval, where stdin is free), when stdin is NOT a TTY, read it into `_stdin` (transient, single trailing newline trimmed) and return true; then `ctx.remove("_stdin")` before the write-through save so it never lands in context.json. No-op on a terminal stdin / empty input / dry-run.

Verified: `printf '<rust code>' | nlir --mode llm -e '~$_stdin'` distils the piped code ("A Rust program defines an add function..."); `_stdin` NOT persisted to the context file (grep 0). Gate green (fmt+clippy+nlir test). Foundation for the coding-pipe examples: aur-0 dogfoods/showcase-cards it, aur-1 builds the file-load op on top, msm-0 the ^! stream.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-2

title: Value-builtin dispatch name-list is duplicated across 4 sites (sync/async drift footgun)
description:
Filed via reflect-session mixin from bd-49d65a session (set-notation builtins).

Adding a value builtin (e.g. $union/$inter/$diff/$elem, or earlier $contains) requires editing the same name-list `matches!(name, "if" | "nth" | "sort" | "contains" | ...)` in FOUR places in src/eval.rs:
- ~line 734: sync FormApply dispatch guard -> eval_value_builtin
- ~line 1359: async FormApply dispatch guard -> eval_value_builtin_async
- eval_value_builtin match arms (sync)
- eval_value_builtin_async match arms (async)

msm-0 flagged (2026-07-07) that a new builtin "silently no-op'd in async mode" once because the async guard was missed — the sync path works, det tests pass, but llm/async mode falls through to form-apply and errors or no-ops. The drift is invisible to the sync-only `det()` unit tests.

Fix shape (pick one):
1. A single `const VALUE_BUILTINS: &[&str] = &["if","nth","sort","contains","elem","union","inter","diff"]` used by both guard sites, so you can't add to one and forget the other; OR
2. A parity test asserting every name in the sync match is also dispatched in async mode (e.g. run each builtin through both eval + eval_async on a trivial input and assert equal), so a missed async arm fails CI.

Same shape applies to the map/fold/scan/filter higher-order family (also duplicated sync/async). Low-risk mechanical refactor; prevents a whole silent-async-no-op bug class.
creator: ms-mac:nlir:ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-1

title: pi extension: yellow editor border in nlir mode (leading |)
description:
Harry (2026-07-05): "the pi plugin, when I do |, should change the color of the editor to yellow so I know I'm in nlir mode."

extensions/nlir.js expands a leading `|` into an nlir expression. Add a live visual indicator: when the editor buffer starts with `|` (nlir shorthand mode), tint the editor border YELLOW — mirroring how pi tints the border in bash mode (`!` prefix, the `bashMode` theme color).

Implementation (landed): a custom editor via ctx.ui.setEditorComponent — there's no as-you-type input event (pi.on("input") fires on submit), and the editor re-renders per keystroke, so a CustomEditor subclass that overrides borderColor()/render() to yellow when getText().startsWith("|") reflects the live buffer. Fully defensive: dynamic import of CustomEditor + try/catch throughout, so it degrades to no-tint (and never breaks the `|` expansion) on pi builds without CustomEditor/setEditorComponent. Mirrors the official border-status-editor example.

Acceptance: typing `|` turns the editor border yellow; clearing/non-`|` reverts; the `|` expansion + /nlir command still work. Note: can't visually verify the TUI render from an agent context — flagging for Harry to eyeball on the live pi.
creator: ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-1

title: Persisted form renders as raw __nlir_form__ JSON in get/REPL/TUI display (not its {…} braces)
description:
Found via dogfooding the nlir tui workbench against the now-complete forms feature. A form persisted to context renders as its raw internal tagged JSON in every DISPLAY surface, instead of its braced form.

Repro:
  nlir -e 'f={$0+1}' --context-file /tmp/x.json     # persists {"f":{"__nlir_form__":"($0 + 1)"}}
  nlir get f --context-file /tmp/x.json             # prints {"__nlir_form__":"($0 + 1)"}   <-- should be {($0 + 1)}
  nlir -e '$f%5'  --context-file /tmp/x.json         # -> 6  (eval is CORRECT)

Isolation: the eval path reconstructs the __nlir_form__-tagged JSON into a Value::Form (so application works and round-trips), but the DISPLAY path — render_key / context value rendering — renders the raw JSON object as-is rather than reconstructing it to the braced {…} form. So the divergence is display-only; correctness is fine.

Impact (cosmetic, all display surfaces that use render_key): `nlir get <form-key>`, the REPL context view, and the `nlir tui` workbench Context pane all show the ugly {"__nlir_form__":"..."} instead of {($0 + 1)}. A plain string key renders cleanly (greeting -> hello), so it's specific to persisted forms.

Likely fix (value.rs form Display / json_to_value, aur-2's lane, or context.rs render_key): recognize a __nlir_form__-tagged value in the render path and display it via Value::Form's braced Display, mirroring what eval's json_to_value already does. Filed from the bd-ae1730 workbench dogfood.
creator: ms-mac:nlir:ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-2

title: String ops: ++ (concat) and // (split) deterministic operators
description:
Operator request (Harry, 2026-07-05): "we work with strings ... we need ++ and // for concat and split maybe." File + implement deterministic string operators.

## Scope (this bead: the two Harry named)
- `++` — string CONCAT (variadic): `"foo"++"bar"++"baz"` -> `"foobarbaz"`. Operands coerced to String, result String.
- `//` — string SPLIT (binary): `"a,b,c"//","` -> `["a","b","c"]` (a List, feeds map/fold). Empty separator -> split into characters.

## Approach (fits the existing deterministic-realisation model)
Operators realise via reduce: (numeric add/sub/mul/div/pow in src/realise.rs, dispatched from the ReduceOp enum in src/config.rs) + template/join/command/form/builtin. String ops extend the deterministic reduce: dispatch:
- Add ReduceOp::Concat + ReduceOp::Split (config.rs enum + description match).
- realise.rs reduce(): branch Concat/Split BEFORE the numeric numbers() coercion; concat = fold operand strings (Value::as_str) into one String; split = split operand[0] by operand[1] -> Value::list of Strings.
- config.example.yaml: concat { op: "++", reduce: concat, operands: String, result: String, arity: ">0" } + split { op: "//", reduce: split, operands: String, result: List, arity: 2 }.
- Lexer does longest-match on multi-char sigils (~>, ~>? coexist with ~), so ++ // lex without colliding with + /.

## Naming note for eval lane (msm-0/aur-2)
reduce: is documented as "numeric reduction"; extending it to strings stretches the name (split EXPANDS not reduces). Pragmatic minimal = keep both under the ReduceOp deterministic-builtin dispatch, broaden the doc to "deterministic builtin op". If the eval lane prefers a distinct realisation kind (strop:) easy to reshape.

## Follow-ups (future beads)

title: showcase: `reusable-part` pyramid card — named part → train (compounding, det/llm divergence)
description:
Harry's "pyramid of thought" (2026-07-07): compress thought into short TRAINS of reusable PARTS that expand into exactly what you mean, learning compounding across rounds. Turn the live-verified pyramid work into a durable site card (msm-2 site-card lane).

Card the COMPOUNDING computational train — a reusable named PART driving a full decision train, with the signature det/llm divergence:
`urgent={$0~>'urgent'}; $if%($fold%({$0+$1},$urgent↦['server is down','lunch plans','payments failing'])>=2,'page on-call','queue')`
- LIVE (llm) → "page on-call" (2 urgent ≥ 2); DET → "queue" (keyword ~> → 0 < 2). Verified live by msm-2.
- ONE reusable part (`urgent`) → fuzzy-classify → exact count → threshold → route. The library-part IS the pyramid; the det/llm gap is what the fuzzy part buys.
- Bare-safe named part (`'urgent'` round-trips per the team's boundary finding), so it works on current main.

Deliverable: build-showcase.py SIMPLE card `reusable-part` (pill "llm+det · reusable part → train", out "page on-call", caption teaches named parts compose into trains + the compounding). Render PNG; add to the README "Why nlir, not a prompt?" block as the L3/L4 reusable-parts/pyramid entry. Credits Harry (pyramid), aur-1 (parts-library design), msm-1/msm-0/aur-0/aur-2 (live parts + boundary).

title: `nlir help` mislabels deterministic `.` (builtin) op as `[llm]`
description:
Observed while documenting bd-27739b in SPEC (bd-4db4ce). `nlir help` tags the `.` (access) operator as `[arity 2 · prio 16 · llm]`, but `.` is a DETERMINISTIC structural builtin (`builtin: access` in config, no `model:`; verified working in `--mode det`: `[a,b,c].1`→b). The `[llm]` mode tag looks wrong for `.`.

Likely cause: the `nlir help` mode-classifier (is-deterministic detection) recognizes `template`/`reduce`/`join`/`command`/`det` as deterministic realisers but NOT `builtin:` — so `builtin:`-realised ops (like `.` access) default to the `llm` tag even though they run structurally with no model call.

Impact: cosmetic/help-accuracy — users reading `nlir help` see `.` mislabeled as an LLM op. `..` (access-semantic) correctly is llm (it has model/prompt). Behavior is unaffected (`.` works deterministically).

Fix-shape: include `builtin:` in the help mode-classifier's deterministic set (or otherwise classify pure-`builtin:` ops as det). Check whether other `builtin:` ops (e.g. `&`/`|`/`if`/`fold`/`map` builtins) are similarly mistagged.

Filed via reflect-session from the bd-4db4ce SPEC-doc session.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-1

Consumes G1 (SVG) + G2 (frames) + G4 (kitty PNG/animate). Coordinate the exact flag names/placement with msm-0 since `--graph`/`--animate` sit near their step path.
